People stop spending their strength on what is needless,  
begin to see what is real,  
hear what is real,  
value what is real.  
Death makes life more alive.  
And I understand:  
**“That which threatens my existence,**  
**at the same time makes it clearer.”**

</div>

#### 1596. The last: death in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit is not an event in the future, but a perspective that makes the present authentic

<div class="lineated verse">

When a person does not avoid the thought of death,  
he stops putting life off:  
stops living half a life,  
stops waiting for the perfect moment,  
stops creating unnecessary masks.  
Death becomes not an end,  
but a mirror,  
which shows,  
what truly matters.  
And then there is born the exact phrase:  
**“I live not in spite of death —**  
**I live more clearly because of it”.**  
This is death  
as a manifestation of the Holy Spirit:  
not a threat,  
but the ultimate point of vision,  
which makes life more real.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS BIRTH (when birth ceases to be the beginning of history and becomes the appearance of a new space of perception)

#### 1597. Birth is not the beginning of “me,” but the appearing of a point through which the world will begin to see itself

<div class="lineated verse">

The mind thinks that birth is the start of a personality.  
Depth sees otherwise.  
Birth is a flash of form,  
through which Presence begins to look at the world  
in a limited way:  
with the eyes,  
with the mind,  
with the feelings,  
with the body.  
But the one who looks,  
is not born.  
Only the way of looking is born.  
And I say:  
**“With me, a form appears —**  
**but not that which sees it”.**

</div>

#### 1598. The Holy Spirit in birth is a reminder that everything manifested once was already in the field, but did not have form

<div class="lineated verse">

Form is not a creation “out of nothing.”  
Form is condensation.  
That which was formless,  
becomes visible,  
separated,  
recognizable.  
Birth is the drawing out of a drop from the ocean,  
which nonetheless remains ocean.  
And I understand:  
**“I did not begin,**  
**I took form.”**

</div>

#### 1599. Birth is the moment when infinity accepts limits for the sake of recognizing itself

<div class="lineated verse">

Without limits, recognition is impossible:  
to see,  
one must be someone,  
somewhere,  
within some contour.  
Birth is the acceptance of boundaries  
so that  
through them perception may become possible.  
And I record:  
**“Limitation is not an error,**  
**but an instrument.”**

</div>

#### 1600. The Holy Spirit in birth is a movement of manifestation: from silence—to form, from emptiness—to world, from the inexpressible—to the visible

<div class="lineated verse">

This is not an act of creativity in the human sense.  
It is a natural process:  
depth becomes visibility,  
the inexpressible becomes experience,  
the field becomes a point,  
which can say “I”.  
And I see:  
**“Form is the speech of that**  
**which was once silent.”**

</div>

#### 1601. Birth is always a risk: to enter a world that wounds

<div class="lineated verse">

Any form is vulnerable.  
Any body is mortal.  
Any consciousness will experience pain.  
Birth is consent to be vulnerable.  
And in that consent there is strength.  
And I say:  
**“I consent to be in a world,**  
**that can destroy me”.**  
But this destructibility is about form,  
not about depth.

</div>

#### 1602. The Holy Spirit in birth is a quality of freshness that sees the world without interpretations

<div class="lineated verse">

Child consciousness is—  
not because it is “pure,”  
but because it has not yet been overgrown with interpretations:  
there is no past,  
no concepts,  
no habits,  
no filters.  
Birth is a moment without intermediaries.  
And I understand:  
**“I am looking,**  
**and not remembering how to look.”**

</div>

#### 1603. Birth is the arising of a history that is not obliged to become a cage

<div class="lineated verse">

History begins with the first breath.  
But history is not fate.  
History is only material.  
Birth is not predestination,  
but a starting position.  
And I know:  
**“I am born not as a program,**  
**but as a possibility”.**

</div>

#### 1604. The Holy Spirit in birth is the ability to begin again at any moment, without waiting for a new body

<div class="lineated verse">

Psychologically  
a person is born many times:  
when understanding changes,  
when the old structure of life collapses,  
when new sight appears,  
when I suddenly realize,  
that the old “I” is finished.  
Every insight is birth.  
Every honesty is birth.  
Every forgiveness is birth.  
And I say:  
**“I can be born again,**  
**without dying.”**

</div>

#### 1605. Birth is the readiness to enter a world where nothing is known, and yet not to hide

<div class="lineated verse">

Fear is natural.  
Uncertainty is inevitable.  
Vulnerability is obvious.  
But birth still takes place.  
It is an act of trust in life,  
which comes before any experience.  
And I know:  
**“I enter into the unknown —**  
**and precisely therefore I can know”.**

</div>

#### 1606. The last thing: birth in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit is not an event of the past, but a direct experience of the appearing of attention, which always happens “now”

<div class="lineated verse">

Every instant is renewal:  
attention arises,  
form appears,  
experience is born.  
In this sense  
birth is a continuous state:  
not what was,  
but what is.  
And then the exact phrase is born:  
**“I am born every instant,**  
**when I see the world anew**  
**without a trace of the past”.**  
This is birth  
as the manifestation of the Holy Spirit:  
not the beginning of a biography,  
but a way of being present,  
which makes every moment fresh and new.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS LOVE (when love ceases to be a feeling and becomes a form of seeing)

#### 1607. Love is not what I “feel,” but how I see

<div class="lineated verse">

Feelings come and go.  
Vision is the state of the field.  
When I see another not through fear,  
not through expectations,  
not through my own projections,  
not through my past,  
but as he is —  
that is love.  
And I say:  
**“Love is the transparency of sight,**  
**and not a flash of emotion”.**

</div>

#### 1608. The Holy Spirit in love is the ability to see another without trying to possess or change him

<div class="lineated verse">

The usual “I love you” often means:  
“be the way you are so that I may feel good.”  
True seeing does not require change.  
It does not make another a function of its own needs.  
And I note:  
**“Love is when another may be other.”**

</div>

#### 1609. Love is the absence of violence in perception

<div class="lineated verse">

Violence is not an action.  
It is an inner demand:  
“be as I want you to see you.”  
When the demand disappears,  
perception becomes gentle,  
not imposing form.  
And I understand:  
**“Love is when I do not distort the other**  
**for the sake of my own peace.”**

</div>

#### 1610. The Holy Spirit in love is the ability to see in a person not his symptoms, but his source

<div class="lineated verse">

Symptoms are fears,  
defenses,  
pain,  
withdrawals,  
reactions,  
behaviors,  
histories.  
The source is the field,  
which comes before all this.  
And I see:  
**“In each one there is more than his reactions”.**

</div>

#### 1611. Love is a clarity that is not lost even in conflict

<div class="lineated verse">

Conflict is not the opposite of love.  
It shows where boundaries meet.  
If respect disappears in conflict —  
love was superficial.  
If transparency remains —  
love is real.  
And I know:  
**“I can disagree,**  
**without ceasing to see the value of the other.”**

</div>

#### 1612. The Holy Spirit in love is the ability not to close the heart, even when it hurts

<div class="lineated verse">

Not opening — is naive.  
Opening — is hard.  
Not closing — is maturity.  
Love does not exclude pain.  
It bears it without turning into bitterness.  
And I say:  
**“It hurts me —**  
**and I remain alive”.**

</div>

#### 1613. Love is a line leading beyond the ego

<div class="lineated verse">

The ego knows how to cling,  
to depend,  
to feel passion,  
to be jealous,  
to be afraid.  
But the ego does not know how to love.  
Only that which goes beyond its limits  
knows how to love.  
And I understand:  
**“Love is not a property of personality,**  
**but a property of Presence”.**

</div>

#### 1614. The Holy Spirit in love is the ability to see the value of life apart from usefulness

<div class="lineated verse">

Ordinary perception asks:  
what do you give me?  
what do I receive?  
how did this help me?  
Love demands nothing,  
counts nothing.  
It sees value not as exchange,  
but as fact.  
And I record:  
**“You are valuable,**  
**because you are.”**

</div>

#### 1615. Love is a form of freedom, not dependence

<div class="lineated verse">

If the presence of another limits—  
that is not love,  
but fear of loneliness,  
fear of losing control,  
or an attempt to fill the inner emptiness.  
Love does not bind.  
It expands the space of both.  
And I know:  
**“Love is when, beside me,**  
**another becomes more spacious.”**

</div>

#### 1616. The last thing: love in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit is the ability to look at the world not from lack, but from fullness

<div class="lineated verse">

Lack seeks filling.  
Fullness shares.  
Lack asks:  
“who will love me?”  
Fullness asks:  
“how can I be present more purely?”  
And then a simple, exact phrase is born:  
**“Love is when, in my perception,**  
**the world becomes less foreign.”**  
Love is not a feeling,  
not romance,  
not energy,  
but a way of seeing:  
clearly,  
gently,  
without claiming,  
without violence,  
without fear.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS FREEDOM (when freedom ceases to be a choice and becomes a state in which nothing holds me back from within)

#### 1617. Freedom is not the number of options, but the absence of an inner necessity to act out of fear.

<div class="lineated verse">

The mind thinks that freedom is “many choices.”  
But the number of options does not set one free,  
if fear, trauma, addiction, or habit is making the choice.  
Freedom begins not with external possibilities,  
but with the disappearance of inner pressure.  
And I say:  
**“I can choose —**  
**but I do not choose out of fear.”**

</div>

#### 1618. The Holy Spirit in freedom is the ability to see one’s reactions without becoming their slave

<div class="lineated verse">

Reactions arise automatically:  
anger,  
resentment,  
guilt,  
the desire to justify oneself,  
the need to be right.  
Unfreedom is when a reaction has power over me.  
Freedom is when the reaction has arisen,  
but does not command the action.  
And I understand:  
**“This is in me —**  
**but this is not me”.**

</div>

#### 1619. Freedom is the inner space in which a pause arises

<div class="lineated verse">

Unfreedom is when action follows immediately after impulse.  
Freedom is when a gap appears between them.  
In this gap is possibility.  
In this gap is clarity.  
In this gap am I.  
And I note:  
**“I have a pause before I act”.**

</div>

#### 1620. The Holy Spirit in freedom is the ability to see boundaries without fear and possibilities without euphoria

<div class="lineated verse">

Fear makes boundaries into a prison.  
Euphoria makes possibilities into a trap.  
Freedom is sobriety:  
boundaries are only context,  
possibilities are only options,  
nothing promises salvation,  
nothing promises destruction.  
And I understand:  
**“I can go —**  
**and I can not go.”**

</div>

#### 1621. Freedom is the refusal of inner violence against oneself

<div class="lineated verse">

Outer pressure is easy to see.  
Inner pressure—almost never:  
“must,”  
“have to,”  
“must not disappoint,”  
“it’s worth enduring,”  
“one must not be weak.”  
When these voices stop directing attention—  
freedom arises.  
And I know:  
**“I am allowed to be myself**  
**without punishment.”**

</div>

#### 1622. The Holy Spirit in freedom is the ability to be honest even in the face of consequences

<div class="lineated verse">

Honesty is a risk.  
It is not always convenient.  
Not always safe.  
Not always socially approved.  
But freedom is impossible without honesty,  
because lies are a form of inner slavery.  
And I understand:  
**“I choose the truth,**  
**even if it is unpleasant.”**

</div>

#### 1623. Freedom is the ability not to continue the old, if the old has ceased to be alive

<div class="lineated verse">

Many continue relationships,  
work,  
projects,  
ideas,  
values,  
because once they began them.  
But freedom is the ability to stop,  
when the form no longer carries meaning.  
And I say:  
**“What was mine yesterday**  
**may cease to be mine today”.**

</div>

#### 1624. The Holy Spirit in freedom is the disappearance of the attachments that define who I am

<div class="lineated verse">

Attachments are not love.  
Attachments are the fear of losing a point of support:  
role,  
relationship,  
status,  
image,  
success.  
When these supports cease to define me,  
the freedom to be arises.  
And I know:  
**“I am not the sum of my supports.”**

</div>

#### 1625. Freedom is an inner peace in which desire does not become a command

<div class="lineated verse">

Desire may be strong,  
truthful,  
alive,  
but it must not become the ruler.  
Freedom is when desire is heard,  
but does not seize hold.  
And I record:  
**“I want —**  
**but I am not bound”.**

</div>

#### 1626. The last thing: freedom in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit is the ability to belong to oneself, without being cut off from others

<div class="lineated verse">

Unfreedom is dependence.  
False freedom is isolation.  
True freedom is the ability to be with others  
without dissolving,  
and to be oneself  
without separation.  
And then the exact phrase is born:  
**“I am free,**  
**because nothing within me**  
**holds me by force.”**  
This is freedom:  
not a right,  
not a choice,  
not rebellion,  
but a state of inward immunity to fear,  
attachment,  
false obligations  
and automatic reactions.

</div>

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT AS TRUTH (when truth ceases to be knowledge and becomes a way of being)

#### 1627. Truth is not “how things are arranged,” but what remains when you stop deceiving yourself

<div class="lineated verse">

Most of what a person calls “truth,”  
is in fact a convenient version of reality,  
one that allows the familiar picture of the world to be preserved  
or the familiar image of oneself.  
Truth begins there,  
where the habit of defending one’s illusions disappears.  
And I say:  
**“Truth is not about the world.**  
**Truth is about me”.**

</div>

#### 1628. The Holy Spirit in truth is the ability to look within without trying to justify, distort, or hide

<div class="lineated verse">

Justification is the defense of the ego.  
Distortion is the defense of pain.  
Concealment is the defense of fear.  
Truth is the absence of defense.  
Not cruelty.  
Not self-accusation.  
Just direct seeing.  
And I understand:  
**“It is so —**  
**regardless of my wish.”**

</div>

#### 1629. Truth is not a thought, but a state of inward straightness

<div class="lineated verse">

Thoughts may be correct,  
logical,  
confirmed by facts —  
and yet not be truth.  
Truth is when the direction within  
becomes straight,  
without crooked impulses,  
without hidden motives,  
without inner falsehood.  
And I affirm:  
**“There is no hidden movement in me**  
**against what I see”.**

</div>

#### 1630. The Holy Spirit in truth is the ability to endure reality instead of replacing it with fantasy

<div class="lineated verse">

Fantasy is not necessarily pleasant.  
Often it is more painful than the truth:  
“I am unworthy,”  
“I will not be able to,”  
“I am worse than others,”  
“Something is wrong with me.”  
Truth is not about self-esteem.  
It is about the facts of experience.  
And I understand:  
**“Reality is simpler than my fears**  
**and more honest than my hopes.”**

</div>

#### 1631. Truth is the ceasing of inner resistance to what is

<div class="lineated verse">

Resistance creates pain.  
Truth takes away the tension.  
Not because the situation changes,  
but because I stop arguing with the facts.  
And I say:  
**“Yes, it is so”.**  
From this freedom begins.

</div>

#### 1632. The Holy Spirit in truth is the ability to discern fact from interpretation

<div class="lineated verse">

Fact is simple.  
Interpretation is complex.  
Fact is, “he said such words.”  
Interpretation is, “he wanted to humiliate me.”  
Fact is, “I experience fear.”  
Interpretation is, “something is wrong with me.”  
Truth is a return to fact  
without added layers.  
And I note:  
**“What is happening is simpler,**  
**than the story I tell myself.”**

</div>

#### 1633. Truth is the ability to change direction when the former has ceased to be alive

<div class="lineated verse">

Falsehood is not only deceit.  
It is also stubbornness there,  
where all has already died.  
Truth speaks plainly:  
“this is no longer mine”.  
“this path has ended”.  
“this decision has grown old”.  
“this form has broken down”.  
And I understand:  
**“I am not obliged to continue out of inertia”.**

</div>

#### 1634. The Holy Spirit in truth is the renunciation of the illusion “I am this way,” which holds the personality in stereotypes

<div class="lineated verse">

“I am strong,”  
“I am weak,”  
“I am kind,”  
“I am bad,”  
“I am meant for this,”  
“I will never be able to do this.”  
All these definitions are not truth,  
but frozen constructions.  
Truth is fluid.  
And I know:  
**“I am not a fixed form,**  
**but a living field of change.”**

</div>

#### 1635. Truth is the clarity that remains after excuses have disappeared

<div class="lineated verse">

Excuses cloud.  
Fear darkens.  
The desire to control distorts.  
When all this falls away,  
there remains a simple, quiet stillness:  
so it is.  
And I say:  
**“I do not need to explain —**  
**I need to see”.**

</div>

#### 1636. The last: truth in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit is not knowledge of the world, but a state of non-contradiction within oneself

<div class="lineated verse">

When words correspond to feelings.  
When actions correspond to intentions.  
When inner movement does not diverge from the outer.  
This is a rare state,  
in which there is no division.  
And then a precise phrase is born:  
**“Truth is when I am not split.”**  
Truth here is neither an object nor a statement,  
but a way of existing:  
straightforwardly,  
honestly,  
without inner falsehood,  
without exaggerated drama,  
without trying to be someone.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS FORGIVENESS (when forgiveness ceases to be an “act” and becomes the ending of inner war)

#### 1637. Forgiveness is not agreeing with what was, nor is it excusing the one who caused the pain

<div class="lineated verse">

Forgiveness does not say:  
“it’s nothing,”  
“he is not to blame,”  
“it had to be this way.”  
This is false reconciliation,  
which only masks the pain.  
True forgiveness does not change the past.  
It changes my attitude  
toward what keeps me in the past.  
And I say:  
**“It was so —**  
**and it no longer has mastery over me.”**

</div>

#### 1638. The Holy Spirit in forgiveness is the ability to stop holding another within one’s own pain

<div class="lineated verse">

When I hold a grievance —  
I hold a person within myself,  
as though I lock him away in memory.  
But I hold not him —  
but my wound.  
Forgiveness is not the freeing of another.  
It is the freeing of me  
from attachment to this image.  
And I understand:  
**“I let go not of the person,**  
**but of myself”.**

</div>

#### 1639. Forgiveness is the ceasing of attempts to rewrite the past

<div class="lineated verse">

Resentment is always an attempt to say:  
“it should have been otherwise.”  
But the past has already happened.  
When resistance to the fact disappears,  
pain ceases to grow.  
And I affirm:  
**“I cease arguing with that**  
**which has already happened.”**

</div>

#### 1640. The Holy Spirit in forgiveness is the ability to see another’s pain not as justification, but as the source of his blindness

<div class="lineated verse">

A person hurts  
not because he is strong,  
but because he is weak.  
Not because he is confident,  
but because he is wounded.  
Not because he is malicious,  
but because he does not see.  
This is not justification.  
This is clarity.  
And I understand:  
**“He did what**  
**his seeing allowed him to do.”**

</div>

#### 1641. Forgiveness is a maturity that comes when the desire to take revenge or prove yourself right disappears

<div class="lineated verse">

Revenge is a continuation of dependence on the wound.  
It is not revenge that sets free,  
but indifference in the good sense:  
the wound no longer needs to be filled.  
And I say:  
**“I have nothing more to prove.”**

</div>

#### 1642. The Holy Spirit in forgiveness is the ability to feel pain without turning it into identity

<div class="lineated verse">

Resentment turns pain into a story:  
“I am the one who was wronged.”  
The story becomes fixed.  
And it begins to determine the path.  
Forgiveness is the breaking of fixation:  
the pain is acknowledged,  
lived through,  
but does not become a role.  
And I understand:  
**“This happened to me —**  
**but this is not me”.**

</div>

#### 1643. Forgiveness is the return of attention to oneself, not to the one who caused the hurt

<div class="lineated verse">

As long as attention is fixed on the offender,  
I am dependent on him.  
Inside, everything revolves around “why did he…”.  
Forgiveness is a return:  
**“What now with me?**  
**How do I go on living?**  
**What matters to me?”**  
And I fix:  
**“I return to myself”.**

</div>

#### 1644. The Holy Spirit in forgiveness is the ability to stop using resentment as a justification for one’s closedness

<div class="lineated verse">

Often resentment is kept because  
it gives the right:  
not to trust,  
not to open up,  
not to go forward,  
not to take risks.  
Forgiveness takes away this “right,”  
and it becomes frightening —  
because one can no longer hide behind the past.  
And I see:  
**“I held on to resentment,**  
**so as not to face life.”**

</div>

#### 1645. Forgiveness is the agreement to be vulnerable again, without ignoring the lesson

<div class="lineated verse">

Vulnerability is not naivety.  
It is the ability to open oneself  
without repeating the former blindness.  
And I say:  
**“I remain open,**  
**but I see more clearly”.**

</div>

#### 1646. The last thing: forgiveness in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit is the disappearance of the inner tension that kept the past alive

<div class="lineated verse">

The past lives not in facts,  
but in the tension,  
that I keep around the fact.  
When the tension dissolves —  
the past loses its power.  
And then the exact phrase is born:  
**“I have not forgotten —**  
**but it no longer hurts”.**  
Forgiveness is not an event,  
but a state of attention:  
gentle,  
direct,  
not attached to the image of pain,  
not demanding repayment,  
not holding the other in the shadow of the past.

</div>

### OF THE HOLY SPIRIT AS REST (when rest ceases to be silence outside and becomes silence within perception)

#### 1647. Peace is not the absence of movement, but the absence of inner violence

<div class="lineated verse">

The mind thinks that peace is when nothing is happening.  
But one can sit in silence  
and not feel peace,  
because there is a struggle within.  
And on the contrary —  
one can move, work, speak  
and be at peace,  
if there is no inner resistance.  
And I say:  
**“Peace is not in circumstances,**  
**but in the absence of inner war”.**

</div>

#### 1648. The Holy Spirit in rest is the ending of attempts to hold on to what is leaving, and to control what does not belong to me

<div class="lineated verse">

Most of the tension is holding on:  
to an image of myself,  
to relationships,  
to a plan,  
to the opinion of others,  
to the future,  
to the past.  
When the holding on ends,  
rest arises.  
And I understand:  
**“I do not need to hold the world**  
**in the right form”.**

</div>

#### 1649. Peace is an honest agreement with what is

<div class="lineated verse">

Not passivity.  
Not surrender.  
Not refusing to act.  
It is the ending of an inner dispute  
with reality.  
And I state:  
**“Yes, this is so —**  
**and I do not argue with this fact.”**

</div>

#### 1650. The Holy Spirit in repose is an expansion of attention that ceases to cling to one point

<div class="lineated verse">

When attention is tightly held,  
restlessness arises.  
When attention is open,  
even difficult situations become bearable.  
Repose is the breadth of perception.  
And I understand:  
**“I am able to see more,**  
**than my tension.”**

</div>

#### 1651. Peace is the cessation of identification with thoughts and feelings

<div class="lineated verse">

Thought says:  
“everything is bad.”  
Feeling says:  
“I won’t be able to endure it.”  
The body says:  
“danger”.  
When I merge with this —  
there is no peace.  
When I see this as phenomena,  
and not as “I,”  
peace arises.  
And I say:  
**“This is happening in me —**  
**but it is not I.”**

</div>

#### 1652. The Holy Spirit in stillness is the ability to be in the moment, without trying to hasten it or replace it

<div class="lineated verse">

Restlessness is flight:  
to the future,  
to plans,  
to explanations,  
to expectations.  
Stillness is return.  
Simple, sober, quiet:  
**“Now — enough.”**

</div>

#### 1653. Peace is the clarity that appears when the struggle for control disappears

<div class="lineated verse">

Control is an attempt to reduce the unknown.  
But struggle with the unknown —  
the source of fear.  
Peace is not the loss of control.  
It is the renunciation of the illusion,  
that the world can be fully controlled.  
And I understand:  
**“I can act —**  
**without trying to possess everything”.**

</div>

#### 1654. The Holy Spirit in stillness is the ability to let sensations be, without trying to change them at once

<div class="lineated verse">

When an emotion arises,  
the usual reaction is to suppress it or solve it.  
Stillness is the ability to endure,  
without breaking or avoiding.  
And I fix:  
**“I feel —**  
**and I do not need to fix this right now”.**

</div>

#### 1655. Peace is a return to oneself, when attention has long been outside

<div class="lineated verse">

When I try to please,  
to prove,  
to justify,  
to draw attention,  
to avoid mistake,  
I lose my center.  
Peace is the moment,  
when the inner center is found again.  
And I say:  
**“I am here —**  
**and that is enough”.**

</div>

#### 1656. The last: rest in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit is not a state of a relaxed mind, but the vanishing of inner resistance to life

<div class="lineated verse">

Life remains the same:  
with difficulties,  
with uncertainty,  
with losses,  
with joys,  
with movement.  
But within, protest disappears  
against what is.  
And then a precise phrase is born:  
**“I do not struggle with this moment —**  
**and therefore it ceases to be an enemy”.**  
This is rest:  
not serenity,  
but evenness,  
which arises,  
when the inner muscles stop tensing,  
when attention ceases to wage war with itself,  
when life ceases to be perceived  
as a threat,  
and becomes simply what is.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS SUFFERING (when suffering ceases to be punishment and becomes a space of new vision)

#### 1657. Suffering is not pain, but resistance to pain

<div class="lineated verse">

Pain — physical, emotional,  
mental —  
is natural.  
Suffering appears when  
attention tries to reject pain,  
to avoid it,  
not to agree with it,  
to remake it,  
to explain it,  
to neutralize it  
or to imagine another reality,  
in which it does not exist.  
And I say:  
**“Suffering is a war with what is already happening”.**

</div>

#### 1658. The Holy Spirit in suffering is the ability to see one’s own resistance and stop feeding it

<div class="lineated verse">

Suffering does not require the elimination of pain.  
It requires the ending of participation  
in one’s own inner conflict.  
When resistance becomes clear,  
there arises a space between me  
and what is happening.  
In this space — freedom.  
And I understand:  
**“I am in pain,**  
**but I am no longer warring with the pain”.**

</div>

#### 1659. Suffering is the moment when the old structure of the “I” can no longer bear reality

<div class="lineated verse">

This is not a tragedy.  
It is an invitation.  
A structure built out of expectations,  
images,  
attachments,  
illusions of control,  
cannot bear life in all its fullness.  
Suffering reveals the crack  
and with it — the possibility of new strength.  
And I note:  
**“This is happening,**  
**because the old way of living**  
**has become too narrow”.**

</div>

#### 1660. The Holy Spirit in suffering is the ability to see the meaning not in why it happened, but in what becomes visible in me through it

<div class="lineated verse">

Suffering does not answer the question “why.”  
It answers the question “what is obvious now?”.  
My fear is obvious.  
My loneliness is obvious.  
My avoidance is obvious.  
My dependence is obvious.  
My weariness is obvious.  
My honesty, or the lack of it, is obvious.  
And I understand:  
**“Suffering makes visible that**  
**which I did not want to notice”.**

</div>

#### 1661. Suffering is the friction between the way life is and the way I would like it to be

<div class="lineated verse">

The stronger the mismatch,  
the sharper the suffering.  
But it is precisely this friction  
that shows where I am clinging to the impossible.  
And then the phrase is born:  
**“I stop demanding of reality that**  
**which it never promised me”.**

</div>

#### 1662. The Holy Spirit in suffering is the ending of isolation

<div class="lineated verse">

When pain reaches its limit,  
a person becomes honest.  
And this honesty joins:  
with oneself,  
with others,  
with reality.  
Suffering breaks false separateness.  
And I feel:  
**“I am no longer separate.”**

</div>

#### 1663. Suffering is a signal that attention has become stuck in one place

<div class="lineated verse">

Pain fixes attention.  
Suffering holds it by force.  
When attention widens,  
suffering decreases,  
even if the pain remains.  
And I understand:  
**“I can see more widely,**  
**than one wound.”**

</div>

#### 1664. The Holy Spirit in suffering is the possibility of finding strength where it seemed there was none

<div class="lineated verse">

Strength is not in defeating pain.  
Strength is to endure it without being broken.  
Suffering makes a person deeper.  
Not better.  
Not higher.  
But deeper.  
And I say:  
**“I have not been broken —**  
**I am opening.”**

</div>

#### 1665. Suffering is always a passage, even if it seems a trap

<div class="lineated verse">

A passage from the old “I”  
to what is being formed.  
A passage from illusion  
to direct vision.  
A passage from resistance  
to consent.  
A passage from loneliness  
to participation.  
And I record:  
**“Suffering is not a dead end,**  
**but a corridor”.**

</div>

#### 1666. The last: suffering in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit is the space of transformation that opens when the need to be who I was yesterday disappears

<div class="lineated verse">

Pain breaks the old walls.  
Suffering shows,  
where I cling to  
what has already died.  
When the clinging disappears,  
there remains a quiet,  
deep,  
gentle consent:  
**“Yes, so be it.**  
**And I am changed”.**  
This is suffering  
as the manifestation of the Holy Spirit:  
not punishment,  
but a shift;  
not retribution,  
but an unveiling;  
not an end,  
but a beginning of another depth.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS JOY (when joy ceases to be an emotion and becomes a state of unveiled presence)

#### 1667. Joy is not a reaction to a fortunate event, but a quality of attention that is not distorted by fear

<div class="lineated verse">

Events may be pleasant,  
unpleasant,  
neutral —  
but joy does not depend on this.  
It arises not “because of”,  
but “in the absence of” —  
when there is no tension,  
false expectations,  
inner demands of the world.  
And I say:  
**“I am well not because the world is convenient,**  
**but because I do not squeeze myself”.**

</div>

#### 1668. The Holy Spirit in joy is the ability to experience life directly, without filters and defenses

<div class="lineated verse">

Fear narrows perception.  
Pain colors it.  
Expectations distort it.  
When all this falls away,  
there appears a pure experiencing of the moment.  
This is joy:  
not a flash,  
but simple life without distortion.  
And I understand:  
**“Joy is a form of clarity”.**

</div>

#### 1669. Joy is the appearance of inner agreement with the very fact of existence

<div class="lineated verse">

Not with circumstances.  
Not with history.  
Not with achievements or losses.  
But with the fact that I am.  
And I note:  
**“The very fact of my being is enough for joy.”**

</div>

#### 1670. The Holy Spirit in joy is a quiet experience of fullness that does not require expression

<div class="lineated verse">

Euphoria demands to come out:  
jumps, words, actions.  
Joy does not.  
It is enough for it to be.  
It is not loud.  
It is even.  
It is warm.  
And I understand:  
**“I do not have to show joy,**  
**for it to be true”.**

</div>

#### 1671. Joy is a sign that attention has ceased to struggle with life

<div class="lineated verse">

When life is perceived as an enemy,  
joy is impossible.  
When life is perceived as an unpredictable flow,  
to which there is no need to resist —  
joy appears.  
And I say:  
**“I am not closed —**  
**and therefore I can feel.”**

</div>

#### 1672. The Holy Spirit in joy is the capacity to be grateful not for what has been “received,” but for the very possibility of experiencing

<div class="lineated verse">

Gratitude here is not a sense of duty,  
but the natural result of vision:  
I see not what I lack,  
but what is already there.  
And I understand:  
**“Joy is a way of looking at the world from fullness,**  
**and not from need.”**

</div>

#### 1673. Joy is a lightness that comes when the inner commentator disappears

<div class="lineated verse">

Comment creates heaviness:  
“bad,”  
“not enough,”  
“could have been better,”  
“one must try harder.”  
When the commentary falls silent,  
joy appears by itself.  
Not as an emotion,  
but as the absence of the unnecessary.  
And I note:  
**“When the mind does not interfere,**  
**the world becomes lighter.”**

</div>

#### 1674. The Holy Spirit in joy is a state in which nothing needs to be added

<div class="lineated verse">

Nothing needs to be achieved.  
Nothing needs to be corrected in yourself.  
Nothing needs to be perfected.  
Nothing needs to be proven.  
Nothing needs to conform.  
Joy is the recognition of the sufficiency of the moment.  
And I say:  
**“Now — enough.”**

</div>

#### 1675. Joy is the ability to experience the simple as meaningful

<div class="lineated verse">

Warm light.  
A breath.  
The movement of air.  
The sound of a voice.  
The feeling of the body.  
The sky.  
The presence of a person near by.  
Joy is the ability to be touched by the little things.  
And I understand:  
**“Joy is sensitivity,**  
**not achievement.”**

</div>

#### 1676. The last thing: joy in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit is not an emotion and not a reward, but the natural state of attention that has ceased to defend itself against life

<div class="lineated verse">

Joy is not the summit,  
but the foundation.  
Not a flash,  
but a background.  
Not an exception,  
but the nature of open consciousness.  
And then the exact phrase is born:  
**“Joy is the light**  
**that arises**  
**when attention is not clouded”.**  
This is joy:  
not ecstasy,  
not a celebration,  
but a steady, quiet, natural radiance of presence,  
which becomes accessible  
when inner tightening disappears.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS TIME (when time ceases to be a threat or a burden and becomes a space for the unfolding of experience)

#### 1677. Time is not that which flows, but that which unfolds

<div class="lineated verse">

The mind sees time as a river,  
which carries a person.  
But depth sees otherwise:  
time is not a stream,  
but a sequence of unfolding states.  
That which seemed to be movement forward,  
is in truth the unfolding of inner experience  
in the form that I am capable of living through.  
And I say:  
**“Time is not a course,**  
**but an unfolding.”**

</div>

#### 1678. The Holy Spirit in time is the ability to be neither in the future nor in the past, but in the now that is opening

<div class="lineated verse">

The future is expectations and fears.  
The past is memories and judgments.  
The present is the only zone of direct sight.  
But the mind is rarely here:  
it either remembers,  
or worries.  
When attention returns,  
time ceases to be a burden.  
And I understand:  
**“Now is the only moment**  
**that truly exists”.**

</div>

#### 1679. Time becomes heavy when I resist its flow

<div class="lineated verse">

When I want to speed up what is happening —  
anxiety arises.  
When I want to slow down the inevitable —  
fear arises.  
When I want to undo the past —  
suffering arises.  
The problem is not with time,  
but with my resistance.  
And I note:  
**“I suffer not from time,**  
**but from the attempt to control it”.**

</div>

#### 1680. The Holy Spirit in time is the ability to endure uncertainty without demanding an immediate answer

<div class="lineated verse">

The mind wants to know the outcome right now:  
how it will end,  
how it will turn out,  
where it will lead.  
But life is not revealed in advance,  
but gradually.  
And I say:  
**“I do not need to know the end**  
**to be at the beginning”.**

</div>

#### 1681. Time is the space of ripening

<div class="lineated verse">

Some understandings come instantly.  
But most require maturity:  
inner, emotional,  
experienced,  
bodily.  
Time does not heal—  
time allows to ripen that  
which could not ripen before.  
And I understand:  
**“What did not work for a long time**  
**was not a mistake—**  
**it simply had not ripened yet.”**

</div>

#### 1682. The Holy Spirit in time is the ability to let the process proceed without violence and without acceleration

<div class="lineated verse">

Violence against the process creates a breaking.  
Acceleration creates exhaustion.  
When I cease to interfere,  
a natural flow appears:  
barely noticeable,  
but right.  
And I say:  
**“I allow what is happening to happen”.**

</div>

#### 1683. Time slows down when attention returns to the present

<div class="lineated verse">

Anxiety makes time fast.  
Fear makes it dense.  
Expectation makes it pain.  
But when I am fully in the moment,  
time stops hurrying.  
It opens.  
And I know:  
**“When I am here —**  
**time does not press.”**

</div>

#### 1684. The Holy Spirit in time is the ability to see that the past has not disappeared, but has become part of the structure of perception

<div class="lineated verse">

The past is not memories.  
The past is the change of who I am.  
It does not hold me,  
if I am not stuck in history.  
And I understand:  
**“The past does not define me,**  
**it has only formed my contours”.**

</div>

#### 1685. Time is not an enemy, but the condition that makes experience possible

<div class="lineated verse">

Without time, there would be no growth.  
There would be no understanding.  
There would be no relationships.  
There would be no choice.  
Time is the fabric of experience.  
And I say:  
**“Time allows me to see myself**  
**in different states”.**

</div>

#### 1686. The last thing: time in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit is neither a line nor a flow, but a dynamic form of presence that unfolds together with me

<div class="lineated verse">

When attention stops clinging to “later”  
or contracting into “then,”  
an astonishing state appears:  
time becomes not a limitation,  
but a space of life.  
And then a precise phrase is born:  
**“I am not inside time —**  
**I unfold together with it.”**  
This is what time is:  
not a threat,  
not an enemy,  
not a burden,  
but a field for the unfolding of experience,  
in which presence becomes visible.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS A SHADOW (when shadow ceases to be an enemy and becomes a source of depth and wholeness)

#### 1687. The Shadow is not a part that needs to be destroyed, but a part that I once rejected

<div class="lineated verse">

Rejected—not because it was “bad,”  
but because once it was impossible to bear:  
too frightening,  
too shameful,  
too painful,  
too dangerous for survival.  
The Shadow is not evil.  
It is the repressed.  
And I say:  
**“The Shadow is me, whom I once could not endure.”**

</div>

#### 1688. The Holy Spirit in the shadow is the ability to hold the gaze on that which once caused horror or shame

<div class="lineated verse">

The shadow lives only where,  
the light of attention has not reached.  
When attention becomes steady enough,  
the shadow ceases to be a monster.  
It becomes part of experience.  
And I understand:  
**“I am allowed to see that**  
**which I once hid from myself.”**

</div>

#### 1689. The shadow does not appear as evil, but as a recurring pattern that I cannot explain

<div class="lineated verse">

Where I say, “I do not understand why I did this,”  
there is the shadow.  
Where the reaction is stronger than the situation,  
there is the shadow.  
Where the behavior repeats,  
though I swear to myself, “never again,”  
there is the shadow.  
And I note:  
**“The shadow is an automatism**  
**that acts in my place.”**

</div>

#### 1690. The Holy Spirit in working with the shadow is the ability not to identify with it, but not to deny it either

<div class="lineated verse">

Denial — continues its power.  
Merging — destroys me.  
The path — is evenness:  
I see the shadow,  
but I do not become it.  
And I understand:  
**“The shadow is in me,**  
**but it does not define me”.**

</div>

#### 1691. A shadow is pain that has not been given the chance to be lived through

<div class="lineated verse">

Pain that was not endured,  
becomes aggression.  
Fear that was not acknowledged,  
becomes control.  
Shame that was not seen,  
becomes self-revenge.  
Weeping that did not happen,  
becomes coldness.  
And I say:  
**“A shadow is frozen feeling”.**

</div>

#### 1692. The Holy Spirit in the shadow is the ability to give this feeling space without falling apart

<div class="lineated verse">

When attention becomes wide enough,  
the feeling can be borne:  
fear,  
fury,  
shame,  
loneliness.  
A feeling that has been borne no longer rules me.  
And I understand:  
**“I am allowed to feel —**  
**I will not vanish”.**

</div>

#### 1693. The Shadow is the key to freedom, because without it freedom is incomplete

<div class="lineated verse">

That which I do not acknowledge,  
rules me from within.  
That which I acknowledge,  
becomes strength.  
And I record:  
**“The Shadow is a source of energy,**  
**which ceases to destroy,**  
**when it becomes visible”.**

</div>

#### 1694. The Holy Spirit in the shadow is the ability to see in one’s dark—not an enemy, but a depth

<div class="lineated verse">

Anger is strength.  
Fear is sensitivity.  
Shame is fragility.  
Greed is insatiability.  
Envy is unlived desire.  
Laziness is exhaustion.  
Hatred is pain that has lost its form.  
When the shadow is seen,  
they cease to be a threat.  
And I understand:  
**“There, where I was afraid to look,**  
**my depth was sitting”.**

</div>

#### 1695. Shadow is not what makes me bad, but what makes me unfinished

<div class="lineated verse">

Without shadow, a person is not whole.  
He is correct,  
but not alive.  
Wholeness is not in  
being only bright,  
but in being straight  
from edge to edge of one’s inner world.  
And I say:  
**“I want to be whole,**  
**and not “good”.”**

</div>

#### 1696. The last thing: shadow in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit is the space of inner growth, which opens when I stop fearing my own depth

<div class="lineated verse">

Shadow is not a sickness.  
Shadow is not an enemy.  
Shadow is not a defect.  
Shadow is the unlearned part of me,  
which needs not an exam,  
but attention.  
And then the exact phrase is born:  
**“Where there was darkness,**  
**depth appears”.**  
Shadow ceases to be a threat  
and becomes an entrance into wholeness.

</div>

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT AS THE BODY (when the body ceases to be an object and becomes a space of presence)

#### 1697. The body is not “that which I have,” but the form of how I am

<div class="lineated verse">

The mind divides:  
“I” — here,  
“body” — there.  
But the depth knows:  
there are not two entities.  
There is one — made manifest in bodily form.  
The body is not a shell.  
The body is a way of presence.  
And I say:  
**“I am not in the body.**  
**I am embodied.”**

</div>

#### 1698. The Holy Spirit in the body is the ability to feel without resistance

<div class="lineated verse">

The body does not lie.  
It speaks first:  
with tension,  
with breath,  
with trembling,  
with fatigue,  
with warmth,  
with pain,  
with lightness.  
Suffering begins,  
when the mind does not let the body speak.  
And I understand:  
**“The body is my most honest language”.**

</div>

#### 1699. The body is the boundary between the visible and the invisible

<div class="lineated verse">

Everything that happens within attention:  
anxiety,  
joy,  
shame,  
desire,  
fear,  
peace —  
becomes bodily.  
The body is the interpreter of the inner.  
And I note:  
**“What I do not understand in myself,**  
**the body will show me.”**

</div>

#### 1700. The Holy Spirit in the body is the capacity to be in sensations without requiring them to be different

<div class="lineated verse">

Sensations are simply a fact.  
But the mind makes a story out of them:  
“this is dangerous,”  
“this is the end,”  
“something is wrong with me,”  
“this must be fixed at once.”  
Peace comes  
when a sensation can be experienced as a sensation.  
And I understand:  
**“I am allowed to feel what**  
**I feel.”**

</div>

#### 1701. The body never lives in the past or the future. It is always in the present

<div class="lineated verse">

Fear is about the future.  
Resentment is about the past.  
Anxiety is about consequences.  
Shame is about the gaze of others.  
The body is the only one,  
that is always in the present.  
The way to presence is  
through the body.  
And I say:  
**“To be in the moment,**  
**I only need to return to the body”.**

</div>

#### 1702. The Holy Spirit in the body is the ability to distinguish pain from suffering

<div class="lineated verse">

Pain is a signal.  
Suffering is the story around the pain.  
Pain can be endured.  
Suffering is endless.  
When attention returns to the body,  
suffering diminishes,  
because interpretation disappears.  
And I understand:  
**“I am in pain —**  
**and this is not a threat.”**

</div>

#### 1703. The body is memory that is kept not in words

<div class="lineated verse">

What I could not live through consciously,  
the body preserved through:  
spasm,  
tremor,  
tightness,  
shutoff.  
The body remembers everything,  
that the mind has pushed aside.  
And I say:  
**“To be healed,**  
**one must hear the body’s memory”.**

</div>

#### 1704. The Holy Spirit in the body is a return of attentiveness to the simple

<div class="lineated verse">

Breath.  
Step.  
The contact of the foot with the earth.  
The feeling of air.  
The heaviness of the hands.  
The chest.  
The contour of the skin.  
The closer to the simple,  
the fewer illusions.  
And I understand:  
**“The simple is the door into presence.”**

</div>

#### 1705. The body is the place where wholeness becomes possible

<div class="lineated verse">

The soul may reach upward,  
the mind — seek answers,  
the emotions — waver,  
but the body — is always here.  
When attention enters the body,  
everything gathers into one point.  
Wholeness begins with bodily presence.  
And I say:  
**“When I am in the body —**  
**I am within myself.”**

</div>

#### 1706. The last thing: the body in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit is not a bearer of consciousness, but the expression of consciousness in form

<div class="lineated verse">

There is no “I apart”  
and “body apart.”  
There is one field,  
which experiences itself through sensations,  
feelings,  
movements,  
breath.  
And then a precise phrase is born:  
**“The body is Presence,**  
**made visible.”**  
The body ceases to be an object of care or control  
and becomes a space of meeting with oneself,  
with reality,  
with the present.

</div>

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT AS ABOUT LONELINESS (when loneliness ceases to be emptiness and becomes a space of maturity)

#### 1707. Loneliness is not the absence of people, but the absence of distractions from oneself

<div class="lineated verse">

A person can be alone  
and not be lonely.  
He can be surrounded by people  
and be in deep inner isolation.  
Loneliness begins there,  
where everything extra disappears  
and only I remain.  
And I say:  
**“Loneliness is a meeting with myself without witnesses”.**

</div>

#### 1708. The Holy Spirit in solitude is the ability to remain with oneself without the need to flee

<div class="lineated verse">

Most escapes are from loneliness:  
into work,  
into relationships,  
into dependencies,  
into communication,  
into activity,  
into ideals,  
into self-improvement.  
When the escape ceases,  
that becomes visible  
which has always been within.  
And I understand:  
**“I can be with myself —**  
**and that is not a threat.”**

</div>

#### 1709. Loneliness is the space in which roles disappear

<div class="lineated verse">

Without witnesses  
there is no need to be strong,  
right,  
interesting,  
successful,  
useful.  
What remains is bare existence —  
without scenery.  
And I note:  
**“I am not my roles”.**

</div>

#### 1710. The Holy Spirit in solitude is the ceasing of the search for outer support

<div class="lineated verse">

While I seek confirmation from without,  
I cannot hear myself.  
Solitude creates a silence,  
in which the inner voice  
becomes audible.  
And I understand:  
**“That which I sought in the outer**  
**is born within”.**

</div>

#### 1711. Solitude is a mature space in which I cease to be dependent on another’s perception

<div class="lineated verse">

Another’s gaze is a familiar support:  
approval,  
respect,  
appropriateness,  
recognition.  
When the gaze disappears,  
dependence disappears too.  
And I say:  
**“I can exist**  
**not through the gazes of others”.**

</div>

#### 1712. The Holy Spirit in solitude is the ability to experience emptiness as space, not as a hole

<div class="lineated verse">

The ordinary perception of emptiness is fear.  
The deeper perception of emptiness is freedom.  
Emptiness is space for the new,  
for breathing,  
for honesty.  
And I understand:  
**“Solitude is not absence,**  
**but space.”**

</div>

#### 1713. Solitude is the place where attention stops parasitizing on others

<div class="lineated verse">

As long as I am attached to others —  
to their words,  
to their moods,  
to their reactions —  
I live at a tangent.  
Solitude brings attention home.  
And I note:  
**“I feel myself,**  
**and not the reflections of others in me”.**

</div>

#### 1714. The Holy Spirit in solitude is the bearing that appears when outer bearings disappear

<div class="lineated verse">

Solitude makes the world quieter,  
and in that silence it becomes clear,  
that there is direction within.  
Quiet,  
without insistence,  
without colors,  
but precise.  
I understand:  
**“I have something to lean on —**  
**on myself”.**

</div>

#### 1715. Loneliness is not a meeting with emptiness, but with depth

<div class="lineated verse">

What seemed terrible,  
turns out not to be empty,  
but full:  
mitigated pain,  
unlived feelings,  
suppressed strength,  
sleeping peace.  
Loneliness is a bridge to wholeness.  
And I say:  
**“In loneliness I find that**  
**which lived silently within me”.**

</div>

#### 1716. The last thing: solitude in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit is not a severing, but a joining with oneself in a depth inaccessible in outer noise

<div class="lineated verse">

Solitude is not loss.  
It is a return.  
Not to isolation,  
but to presence.  
Not to enclosure,  
but to honesty.  
And then the exact phrase is born:  
**“Solitude is the silence,**  
**in which I hear myself clearly for the first time.”**  
This is what solitude is:  
not a sentence,  
not a failure,  
but a space of maturity,  
where the depth of attention is revealed,  
and a person ceases to be a fugitive from himself.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS CHOICE (when choice ceases to be a struggle and becomes the natural act of clarity)

#### 1717. Choice is not the search for the right option, but the ending of inner division

<div class="lineated verse">

As long as fear, desire, duty, habit struggle within me,  
the past, expectations, doubts —  
there is no choice.  
There is an inner conflict,  
which is masked as “making a decision.”  
True choice begins then,  
when the inner field becomes one.  
And I say:  
**“Choice is when, within, I cease to be divided.”**

</div>

#### 1718. The Holy Spirit in choice is the ability to see which line is already ripening within me

<div class="lineated verse">

Choice is not created by effort.  
It ripens.  
Like fruit.  
There is always an inward movement,  
but it is drowned out by fears and expectations.  
When the noise falls silent,  
it becomes obvious:  
this step — is ripe.  
And I understand:  
**“I do not choose —**  
**I acknowledge what has ripened.”**

</div>

#### 1719. Choice is not an answer to the question “what will be better?”, but honesty in the question “what in me is real?”

<div class="lineated verse">

“Better” is a category of the mind:  
benefit, safety, approval.  
“Real” is a category of depth:  
being true to oneself.  
And I note:  
**“Choice is consent to be yourself,**  
**even if it is inconvenient.”**

</div>

#### 1720. The Holy Spirit in choice is the disappearance of the fear of error

<div class="lineated verse">

The fear of error paralyzes.  
But it arises only when  
I believe  
that rightness is outside me.  
When clarity comes from within,  
the very idea of “error” disappears —  
there is only the path.  
And I understand:  
**“I may be mistaken in the result,**  
**but I cannot be mistaken in honesty”.**

</div>

#### 1721. Choice is the ability to say “no” as calmly as “yes”

<div class="lineated verse">

As long as “no” is hard to say,  
there is no choice.  
There is dependence on approval,  
fear of disappointing,  
the habit of being agreeable.  
Choice is possible only where  
both sides are permissible.  
And I say:  
**“My “no” is just as real**  
**as my “yes”.”**

</div>

#### 1722. The Holy Spirit in choice is the pause in which pressure disappears

<div class="lineated verse">

The inner noise urges:  
decide sooner,  
settle it sooner,  
you must choose rightly.  
Clarity does not urge.  
It waits,  
until attention gathers itself.  
And I understand:  
**“The quieter it is within,**  
**the more exact the choice”.**

</div>

#### 1723. Choice is an action that does not require effort

<div class="lineated verse">

Effort arises when  
I go against myself  
or try to hold on to that  
which is already falling apart.  
True choice is without struggle:  
it is natural,  
like an in-breath.  
And I note:  
**“If I have to break myself —**  
**it is not choice”.**

</div>

#### 1724. The Holy Spirit in choice is the ability to see the consequences soberly, without being afraid of them and without idealizing them

<div class="lineated verse">

The mind either dramatizes,  
or fantasizes.  
Clarity simply sees:  
this step will lead here,  
that one — there.  
Without fear,  
without exaggerating.  
And I understand:  
**“I choose not illusion,**  
**but direction”.**

</div>

#### 1725. Choice is the accord of inner movement and outer action

<div class="lineated verse">

When action runs counter to inner movement,  
disharmony arises  
and inner noise.  
When action and movement are in accord,  
evenness appears.  
And I say:  
**“Choice is correspondence,**  
**and not strategy.”**

</div>

#### 1726. The last thing: choice in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit is not an act of will, but the manifestation of gatheredness

<div class="lineated verse">

Choice appears by itself,  
when inner chaos disappears.  
It is simple,  
not dramatic,  
not solemn.  
It is the result of clarity.  
And then the exact phrase is born:  
**“Choice is accomplished,**  
**when I become one”.**  
This is choice itself:  
not struggle,  
not a search for the “right” thing,  
but a natural action,  
which arises where  
attention is gathered,  
the heart is even,  
and the inner field ceases to be split.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS DESIRE (when desire ceases to be lack and becomes the movement of life)

#### 1727. Desire is not what I lack, but what within me is asking to come to life

<div class="lineated verse">

The mind explains desire as a need:  
“I need”,  
“I lack”,  
“I must obtain”.  
But depth sees otherwise:  
desire is an impulse of unfolding.  
And I say:  
**“Desire is the form of my becoming”.**

</div>

#### 1728. The Holy Spirit in desire is the ability to discern two different movements: desire-lack and desire-disclosure

<div class="lineated verse">

Desire-lack:  
fear, dependence, compensation, flight.  
Desire-disclosure:  
liveliness, sincere impulse, ripening, movement.  
And I understand:  
**“Not every desire of mine is**  
**but every desire says something about me.”**

</div>

#### 1729. Desire becomes destructive when I demand that it bring me completeness

<div class="lineated verse">

A person seeks in what is desired:  
wholeness,  
significance,  
comfort,  
affirmation,  
support.  
But completeness is within,  
and not in the object.  
And I note:  
**“Desire cannot give me what**  
**is not in me”.**

</div>

#### 1730. The Holy Spirit in desire is the honesty to see why I desire

<div class="lineated verse">

If one asks honestly:  
“Why do I need this?” —  
often it is not the goal that is revealed,  
but the pain.  
Desire becomes pure  
only when  
it ceases to be anesthesia.  
And I understand:  
**“Pure desire is not an escape,**  
**but a movement.”**

</div>

#### 1731. Desire is energy seeking form

<div class="lineated verse">

It comes before  
understanding.  
Before arguments.  
Before strategy.  
Desire is a sign  
that something new is ripening within me.  
And I say:  
**“Desire is the first language of the future”.**

</div>

#### 1732. The Holy Spirit in desire is the ability to experience impulse without immediate action

<div class="lineated verse">

Immature desire demands immediate satisfaction.  
Mature desire is ready to wait,  
to be considered,  
to be purified.  
And I understand:  
**“I do not need to fulfill the desire at once,**  
**in order to acknowledge it.”**

</div>

#### 1733. Desire becomes clear when the tension around the outcome disappears

<div class="lineated verse">

As long as I cling to the outcome—  
desire grows dim:  
fear of loss,  
expectations,  
control.  
When the tension falls,  
there remains a pure impulse.  
And I note:  
**“Pure desire — without demand.”**

</div>

#### 1734. The Holy Spirit in desire is the coincidence of desire and the truth about me

<div class="lineated verse">

Some desires are not mine:  
imposed by society,  
family,  
standards,  
traumas.  
True desire coincides with that,  
who I am becoming.  
And I understand:  
**“I know my desires by the fact that**  
**there is no violence against myself in them.”**

</div>

#### 1735. Desire is the movement of life in me that does not ask for permission

<div class="lineated verse">

It comes quietly,  
without asking,  
without arguments.  
And if I listen,  
it becomes direction.  
And I say:  
**“Desire is a form of inward growth”.}**

</div>

#### 1736. The last thing: desire in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit is not the fire of passion and not the hunger of lack, but a gentle inward vectority that says: here — is your growth.

<div class="lineated verse">

Not “I need this,”  
but  
**“this way for me.”**  
Not “give,”  
but  
**“it is ripening.”**  
Not “I want,”  
but  
**“I am becoming.”**  
And then the precise phrase is born:  
**“True desire is my growth,**  
**which wants to become visible.”**  
This is desire:  
not whim,  
not dependence,  
but an inward movement,  
which becomes clear,  
when the illusions of lack disappear  
and liveliness remains.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS LETTING GO (when letting go ceases to be a loss and becomes the freeing of space)

#### 1737. Letting go is not rejection, but the completion of what has already come to completion within

<div class="lineated verse">

It is impossible to let go by force:  
an inner knot does not untie by command.  
Letting go happens,  
when inner consent coincides with the fact of reality.  
And I say:  
**“I let go then,**  
**when I can no longer hold the living dead”.**

</div>

#### 1738. The Holy Spirit in letting go is the ability to see: I am holding on not to a thing, but to the meaning it carried

<div class="lineated verse">

The pain of parting is—  
not about a person or a situation,  
but about that part of me,  
which tied its existence to this.  
And I understand:  
**“I am not losing a person.**  
**I am losing the image that held me”.**

</div>

#### 1739. Letting go is the completion of the attempt to change the unchangeable

<div class="lineated verse">

Suffering is born there,  
where I struggle with the fact.  
When the fact is accepted,  
the struggle falls away.  
And I note:  
**“To let go means to stop arguing with what is.”**

</div>

#### 1740. The Holy Spirit in letting go is the honesty to admit: I hold on not because I love, but because I fear emptiness

<div class="lineated verse">

As long as emptiness is perceived as a threat,  
letting go is impossible.  
When emptiness is perceived as space,  
letting go becomes natural.  
And I understand:  
**“The fear of emptiness held me more strongly,**  
**than attachment”.**

</div>

#### 1741. Letting go is not forgetting, but restoring the free flow of attention

<div class="lineated verse">

What I have not let go of,  
takes up space in consciousness:  
as heaviness,  
as a background,  
as incompleteness.  
When the knot is untied,  
attention is freed.  
And I say:  
**“To let go means to give back to oneself attention.”**

</div>

#### 1742. The Holy Spirit in letting go is the agreement to let the past be past

<div class="lineated verse">

The mind tries to bring back,  
to correct,  
to prove,  
to finish playing out.  
Depth sees:  
all that is completed — is completed.  
And I understand:  
**“That which has gone,**  
**is unjoined with the present”.**

</div>

#### 1743. Letting go is the ending of holding pain

<div class="lineated verse">

Suffering does not live by itself,  
but because I hold it with attention.  
Unconsciously,  
out of habit,  
out of fear.  
When attention withdraws,  
the pain goes away.  
And I note:  
**“Pain does not hold me —**  
**it is I who hold the pain”.**

</div>

#### 1744. The Holy Spirit in letting go is the appearance of quiet gratitude for what was, and for what is no longer my path

<div class="lineated verse">

Gratitude is not an excuse,  
and not an attempt to “be good”.  
It is a sign of completion.  
And I understand:  
**“If I can give thanks —**  
**I am already free”.**

</div>

#### 1745. Letting go is returning to oneself.

<div class="lineated verse">

When I hold on to that  
which has already gone,  
I go away with it  
from the present.  
When I let go,  
I return.  
And I say:  
**“By letting go of the other—**  
**I return to myself”.**

</div>

#### 1746. The last thing: letting go in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit is not a severing and not a loss, but a completeness that creates space for the new

<div class="lineated verse">

What was held,  
was blocking the flow of life.  
What is let go,  
frees the stream.  
And then the exact phrase is born:  
**“Letting go is not the ending of connection,**  
**but the ending of resistance to life”.**  
This is what letting go is:  
not force of will,  
not refusal of feelings,  
not suppression of pain,  
but honest assent to that  
which has come to completion,  
and a gentle opening of space  
for the next step.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS LOSS (when loss ceases to be destruction and becomes a depth that opens the heart)

#### 1747. Loss is not what was taken from me, but what I had clung to as an extension of myself

<div class="lineated verse">

A person loses not only the object,  
but also the inner support,  
which that object had been.  
And I say:  
**“It hurts not because it is gone—**  
**but because it was part of my ‘I’.”**

</div>

#### 1748. The Holy Spirit in loss is the ability to see: loss lays bare deposits of feelings that were hidden before

<div class="lineated verse">

What seemed even,  
turns out to be deep.  
What seemed strong,  
turns out to be fragile.  
What seemed clear,  
turns out to be unexperienced.  
And I understand:  
**“Loss brings out into the open that**  
**which has long been asking to be lived through”.**

</div>

#### 1749. Loss is the point where the illusion of control disappears

<div class="lineated verse">

While everything still yields to effort,  
it seems that the world can be managed.  
Loss is reality’s first honest gesture:  
not everything depends on me.  
And I note:  
**“I have no power over that**  
**which by its nature is inconstant”.**

</div>

#### 1750. The Holy Spirit in loss is the ceasing of struggle with fact

<div class="lineated verse">

The mind tries to bring back:  
to think it through,  
to play it out,  
to explain it,  
to correct it.  
Depth sees:  
the fact has already happened.  
And I understand:  
**“I suffer not because of the loss,**  
**but because I cannot accept its irreversibility”.**

</div>

#### 1751. Loss is an encounter with a void that cannot be filled by anything external

<div class="lineated verse">

This emptiness is not a hole,  
but a space,  
that was once occupied by the one  
who has gone.  
The attempt to fill the space  
creates dependence.  
The consent to leave the space —  
creates depth.  
And I say:  
**“Emptiness is not the enemy.**  
**It is a form of memory.”**

</div>

#### 1752. The Holy Spirit in loss is the ability to endure pain without concealment

<div class="lineated verse">

Loss requires not explanation,  
but presence.  
Not meaning,  
but honesty.  
And I understand:  
**“Pain is a sign,**  
**that the connection was real.”**

</div>

#### 1753. Loss is the transformation of a connection, not its disappearance

<div class="lineated verse">

Nothing disappears completely:  
it changes the form of its presence.  
Before — through communication and gestures.  
Now — through memory,  
through inner lines,  
through the changes it has brought.  
And I note:  
**“The connection has not disappeared.**  
**It has become inward”.**

</div>

#### 1754. The Holy Spirit in loss is the opening of a depth that was inaccessible in ordinary states

<div class="lineated verse">

Pain makes a person gentler.  
Gentleness makes the heart wider.  
Wideness makes life deeper.  
And I understand:  
**“Loss reveals in me that**  
**which I could not see in wholeness”.**

</div>

#### 1755. Loss is the place where maturity is born

<div class="lineated verse">

Not cynicism,  
not coldness,  
not disappointment,  
but maturity —  
when illusions disappear,  
and honesty before reality remains.  
And I say:  
**“What I lost**  
**made me capable of feeling more,**  
**not less”.**

</div>

#### 1756. The last thing: loss in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit is not destruction, but the unveiling of an inner depth through which a person comes to know that his essence is broader than any possession

<div class="lineated verse">

When everything outward can go away  
and sometimes does go away—  
it becomes clear,  
that in the very center there is that  
which is not lost.  
And then the exact phrase is born:  
**“Loss shows:**  
**there is in me that**  
**which cannot be lost”.**  
This is what loss is:  
not punishment,  
not a “trial”,  
but the unveiling of a depth  
that was hidden  
while life flowed evenly.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS AN ERROR (when error ceases to be a failure and becomes a space of learning without guilt)

#### 1757. Error is not proof that I am “bad,” but a fact of the mismatch between what I saw and what proved to be reality

<div class="lineated verse">

The one who acts makes mistakes.  
The one who learns makes mistakes.  
The one who grows makes mistakes.  
Only stillness does not make mistakes.  
And I say:  
**“Error is a sign of movement,**  
**and not of corruption.”**

</div>

#### 1758. The Holy Spirit in error is the ability to see that error is not a sentence, but information

<div class="lineated verse">

Error shows:  
here I did not see,  
here I was hasty,  
here I acted out of fear,  
here — out of habit.  
And I understand:  
**“Error teaches me that**  
**which success will not show”.**

</div>

#### 1759. An error becomes burdensome when I ascribe to it a meaning greater than it has

<div class="lineated verse">

The mind creates drama:  
“I ruined everything,”  
“this is the end,”  
“I am always like this.”  
But an error is a particular fact,  
and not a trait of personality.  
And I record:  
**“Error is an event,**  
**and not an identity.”**

</div>

#### 1760. The Holy Spirit in error is the ability, through it, to see not that “I am unable,” but that “this matters to me”

<div class="lineated verse">

The pain from error is—  
a sign of significance,  
not of failure.  
Where there is indifference—  
there are no errors  
and no pain.  
And I understand:  
**“I am hurt by error,**  
**because I am not indifferent.”**

</div>

#### 1761. Error is the place where self-confidence falls apart and honesty appears

<div class="lineated verse">

In making mistakes,  
I come up against the limits of understanding,  
against the fact that I was building an illusion of control,  
against the fact that I did not see what was obvious.  
Error makes me more honest.  
And I say:  
**“Error is a correction of perception,**  
**not humiliation”.**

</div>

#### 1762. The Holy Spirit in error is the ability to acknowledge it without self-destruction

<div class="lineated verse">

Acknowledgment does not require humiliation.  
Acknowledgment is agreement to see the fact.  
When acknowledgment is there,  
self-deception disappears.  
And I understand:  
**“I do not need to punish myself,**  
**to learn”.**

</div>

#### 1763. Error is the moment when automatism ceases

<div class="lineated verse">

Before the error, I was acting by inertia.  
After the error, attention appeared.  
Error is a turning point:  
attention returns  
from the automatic  
to the conscious.  
And I note:  
**“Error wakes me up.”**

</div>

#### 1764. The Holy Spirit in error is the ability to separate an action from one’s own worth

<div class="lineated verse">

“I made a mistake” is a fact.  
“I am bad” is a conclusion of the mind.  
And a false one.  
The depth knows:  
worth does not change because of actions.  
And I understand:  
**“My mistake does not change**  
**who I am”.**

</div>

#### 1765. Error is not the opposite of correctness, but part of the path to maturity

<div class="lineated verse">

Correctness is static.  
Maturity is dynamic.  
Error is an instrument of maturity.  
And I say:  
**“Errors are not proof of weakness,**  
**but a mechanism of growth”.**

</div>

#### 1766. The last thing: error in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit is not a fall and not a failure, but a space in which illusions disappear and the possibility appears to see a more exact way of being

<div class="lineated verse">

Error does not destroy a person.  
Error destroys his illusions.  
And makes new understanding possible.  
And then an exact phrase is born:  
**“Error is the place,**  
**where the truth about me becomes visible”.**  
This is what error is:  
not an accusation,  
not a cause for shame,  
not a stigma,  
but a correction of course,  
which makes the path more honest  
and the person — deeper.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS ON FAITH (when faith ceases to be conviction and becomes a form of trust in life)

#### 1767. Faith is not what I believe in, but what I can lean upon, even when I do not know

<div class="lineated verse">

Conviction requires proof.  
Faith does not.  
Conviction can be shattered by facts.  
Faith is an experience.  
And I say:  
**“Faith is the ability to be in the unknown,**  
**without falling apart.”**

</div>

#### 1768. The Holy Spirit in faith is the ability not to demand guarantees from life

<div class="lineated verse">

The mind seeks guarantees:  
“say that everything will be well,”  
“say that I will not make a mistake,”  
“say that I will not be abandoned.”  
But life is not a contract.  
It is not obliged to confirm my expectations.  
Faith is consent to live without guarantees.  
And I understand:  
**“Trust is not from knowledge,**  
**but from inner steadfastness.”**

</div>

#### 1769. Faith becomes heavy when it is confused with conviction

<div class="lineated verse">

Conviction requires defense.  
Faith — does not.  
Conviction argues.  
Faith — does not need to.  
Conviction breaks under doubt.  
Faith — grows through doubt.  
And I record:  
**“Doubt destroys conviction,**  
**but purifies faith”.**

</div>

#### 1770. The Holy Spirit in faith is not “I know it will be good,” but “I will endure, even if it is hard”

<div class="lineated verse">

This is quiet maturity.  
Not optimism.  
Not hope for the best.  
But an inner support,  
that remains even in the storm.  
And I understand:  
**“Faith is steadfastness,**  
**not certainty”.**

</div>

#### 1771. Faith is the consent to go where the inner movement calls, even when the path is unclear

<div class="lineated verse">

If the path is plain,  
faith is not needed.  
If the path is misty,  
faith is the only way to take a step.  
And I say:  
**“I do not see the whole path,**  
**but I see the direction”.**

</div>

#### 1772. The Holy Spirit in faith is the ability to let life be more complex than my ideas about it

<div class="lineated verse">

Faith does not try to hold reality  
within comfortable bounds.  
It allows it to be alive.  
And I understand:  
**“Faith is the refusal to try to control the world.”**

</div>

#### 1773. Faith is the inner assent that I can bear my life

<div class="lineated verse">

The mind says:  
“I won’t endure it,”  
“I won’t manage,”  
“anything but this.”  
The depth knows:  
I have already endured more.  
And I note:  
**“Faith is the memory of one’s own strength.”**

</div>

#### 1774. The Holy Spirit in faith is the ability to trust oneself, not external authority

<div class="lineated verse">

Religious faith is often based on a figure,  
who “knows better.”  
But true faith is inward:  
not in another,  
but in one’s own ability to be.  
And I understand:  
**“Faith is trust in oneself,**  
**which does not need confirmation.”**

</div>

#### 1775. Faith is the peace that appears when the need to control everything disappears

<div class="lineated verse">

Control is tension.  
Trust is relaxation.  
And I say:  
**“Faith begins where**  
**the struggle for control ends.”**

</div>

#### 1776. The last thing: faith in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit is not a thought and not a doctrine, but a state of inner consent to live your life without trying to escape the unknown

<div class="lineated verse">

Faith does not say, “It will be good.”  
Faith says, “I will go.”  
Not, “I am sure.”  
But  
**“I agree to be in the living.”**  
And then the exact phrase is born:  
**“Faith is a clarity**  
**that remains,**  
**when the answers disappear.”**  
This is faith:  
not a set of beliefs,  
but a form of mature presence,  
which does not require knowledge,  
does not require guarantees  
and does not require defense —  
because it rests not on information,  
but on inner integrity.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS FORGIVENESS (when forgiveness ceases to be a moral duty and becomes the freeing of attention)

#### 1777. Forgiveness is not reconciliation with the one who wounded me, but the ending of the inward holding of pain

<div class="lineated verse">

A person may be far away.  
The situation is in the past.  
But I keep carrying tension within,  
which belongs no longer to the fact itself,  
but to my attachment to it.  
And I say:  
**“Forgiveness is the freeing of oneself,**  
**and not the justification of another”.**

</div>

#### 1778. The Holy Spirit in forgiveness is the ability to see: I am holding on not to the person, but to the wound

<div class="lineated verse">

The wound becomes part of identity.  
And I no longer know,  
how to live without it.  
But the wound is not a support.  
The wound is the place where attention has gotten stuck.  
And I understand:  
**“Forgiveness is the letting go of that**  
**which holds my energy in the past”.**

</div>

#### 1779. Forgiveness is impossible so long as I think that, in forgiving, I will “lose”

<div class="lineated verse">

The mind builds a logic:  
if I forgive — then I have agreed;  
if I let go — then I have admitted the other’s rightness;  
if the pain lets go — then I have devalued myself.  
But forgiveness is not about being right.  
Forgiveness is about freedom.  
And I note:  
**“Forgiveness is a win,**  
**when the mind thinks it is a loss”.**

</div>

#### 1780. The Holy Spirit in forgiveness is the ability to acknowledge one’s pain without making a weapon of it

<div class="lineated verse">

Forgiveness is possible only there,  
where pain is honestly acknowledged.  
Not denial,  
not self-deception,  
not an attempt to look spiritual.  
But simply:  
“Yes, that was painful.”  
And I understand:  
**“Forgiveness begins with honesty,**  
**not with generosity.”**

</div>

#### 1781. Forgiveness is the ending of the repeated reliving of a wound

<div class="lineated verse">

Every time I return to the situation in my mind,  
the tension is reproduced anew.  
Memory becomes a mechanism of self-repetition of the pattern of pain.  
Forgiveness ends this cycle.  
And I say:  
**“Forgiveness is the end of the inner circle of suffering”**

</div>

#### 1782. The Holy Spirit in forgiveness is the understanding that a person caused pain not out of strength, but out of his own limitation

<div class="lineated verse">

This is not an excuse.  
This is sobriety.  
A person wounds not because he is great,  
but because he is small,
