**“Presence is when nothing within seeks to leave.”**  
This is presence:  
not concentration,  
not attainment,  
but a simple, quiet form of being,  
in which I stop running away from the moment  
and for the first time am fully in it.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS REST (when rest ceases to be the absence of movement and becomes an inner non-strain)

#### 2007. Peace is not the stopping of the world, but the ceasing of inner resistance to the world

<div class="lineated verse">

Outer silence may be absent.  
Inner silence may arise.  
I see:  
**peace is not an environment, but a state.**

</div>

#### 2008. The Holy Spirit in rest is the ability to be within what is happening without trying to speed it up or slow it down

<div class="lineated verse">

When violence against reality disappears,  
rest arises.  
It is clear to me:  
**rest is agreement with the rhythm of life.**

</div>

#### 2009. Peace is the absence of inner tension between “as it is” and “as I want it to be”

<div class="lineated verse">

When this tension disappears,  
even movement feels smooth.  
I know:  
**peace is friction that has ceased.**

</div>

#### 2010. The Holy Spirit at rest is the ability to be in the moment without trying to keep it under control

<div class="lineated verse">

Control creates anxiety.  
Letting go creates peace.  
I see:  
**peace is life without inner control.**

</div>

#### 2011. Peace is a gentleness that appears when I stop struggling with myself

<div class="lineated verse">

The struggle for a “better state” is also tension.  
To give up the struggle is peace.  
I note:  
**peace is the absence of self-compulsion.**

</div>

#### 2012. The Holy Spirit in repose is the ability to notice movement within without the need to correct it at once

<div class="lineated verse">

Emotions may be.  
Thoughts may be.  
The body may be restless.  
And repose — still be present.  
I understand:  
**repose is independence from the inner weather.**

</div>

#### 2013. Peace is an inner center that does not depend on outward circumstances

<div class="lineated verse">

Noise does not destroy peace.  
It destroys only the attempt to “enter” into something.  
Peace is deeper.  
I see:  
**peace is steadiness without effort.**

</div>

#### 2014. The Holy Spirit in rest is the ceasing of the mental bustle about how it should be

<div class="lineated verse">

“Should” disappears —  
peace appears.  
It is clear to me:  
**peace is life without an imposed form.**

</div>

#### 2015. Peace is presence without strain

<div class="lineated verse">

Not slowing down,  
not shutting off,  
not stupor,  
but clarity that requires no effort.  
I say:  
**peace is wakeful gentleness.**

</div>

#### 2016. And lastly: peace in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit is not about the absence of sounds, not about a relaxed body, and not about steadily “positive” emotions, but about an inner lack of tension that arises when I no longer struggle:

<div class="lineated verse">

neither with myself,  
nor with feelings,  
nor with reality,  
nor with time.  
Peace is not an achievement.  
Peace is the ceasing of struggle.  
And the exact phrase is born:  
**peace is when there is nothing inside that needs to be held together.**  
This is peace:  
quiet,  
even,  
unforced,  
not requiring conditions,  
arising as a natural state,  
when resistance disappears.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS ACCEPTANCE (when acceptance ceases to be submissiveness and becomes a mature form of contact with reality)

#### 2017. Acceptance is not agreement and not approval. Acceptance is the recognition of a fact. A fact does not become better by struggling with it

<div class="lineated verse">

The fact becomes clearer.  
I see:  
**acceptance is contact with reality without distortion.**

</div>

#### 2018. The Holy Spirit in acceptance is the ending of the inner dispute with what has already happened

<div class="lineated verse">

The past cannot be changed.  
But inner resistance to the past is the source of pain.  
It is clear to me:  
**acceptance is the ending of a senseless struggle.**

</div>

#### 2019. Acceptance is a straight gaze

<div class="lineated verse">

When I stop covering reality with desires,  
fears,  
resentments,  
hopes,  
ideas of how it “should” be.  
I note:  
**acceptance is honesty before the fact.**

</div>

#### 2020. The Holy Spirit in acceptance is the ability to remain in contact with what is, not closing off, not justifying oneself, not running away

<div class="lineated verse">

And I understand:  
**acceptance is the maturity to stay with reality face to face.**

</div>

#### 2021. Acceptance is not passivity.

<div class="lineated verse">

Acceptance is the starting point of action.  
One cannot change that  
which I am not ready to see.  
I say:  
**acceptance is the beginning of true change.**

</div>

#### 2022. The Holy Spirit in acceptance is the ability to discern: I accept the fact, and do not reject myself

<div class="lineated verse">

Acceptance of the fact is not surrender.  
Acceptance of the fact is the acknowledgment of the map of the territory.  
It is clear to me:  
**acceptance is to see where I stand.**

</div>

#### 2023. Acceptance is the ending of self-deception

<div class="lineated verse">

I stop telling myself,  
that the world is different,  
people are different,  
I am different.  
And in this honesty, relief appears.  
I note:  
**acceptance is honesty without excuses.**

</div>

#### 2024. The Holy Spirit in acceptance is the gentleness that comes when the tension of struggle lets up

<div class="lineated verse">

The struggle with reality is always a losing one.  
Acceptance is the ending of inner violence.  
And I understand:  
**acceptance is peace without surrender.**

</div>

#### 2025. Acceptance is the ability to say honestly: “this is so”

<div class="lineated verse">

And not to add:  
“and this is unbearable,”  
“and this is the end,”  
“and it must be otherwise.”  
I say:  
**acceptance is a simple “yes” to the fact.**

</div>

#### 2026. And one last thing: acceptance in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit is not a yielding to fate, not a renunciation of strength, and not an attempt to be “spiritual,” but a mature state of inner straightness, in which the war with what already is disappears, and a clear space appears in which one can act, experience, feel, and live honestly

<div class="lineated verse">

Acceptance is not weakness.  
Acceptance is vision.  
And a precise phrase is born:  
**acceptance is the ceasing of resistance to reality,**  
**but not a renunciation of the possibility of changing it.**  
This is acceptance:  
steady,  
mature,  
firm and gentle at once,  
from which alone true movement is possible.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS ON RESPECT (when respect ceases to be politeness and becomes the recognition of another’s wholeness)

#### 2027. Respect is not about evaluating a person, but about recognizing his boundaries

<div class="lineated verse">

Not “I like you,”  
but “you are a separate whole.”  
I see:  
**respect is the recognition of the other as an autonomous phenomenon.**

</div>

#### 2028. The Holy Spirit in respect is the ability not to intrude where another has the right to be himself

<div class="lineated verse">

Not to save.  
Not to correct.  
Not to direct without being asked.  
It is clear to me:  
**respect is non-intrusion.**

</div>

#### 2029. Respect is the ability to see another not as an extension of one’s own expectations and not as a character in one’s inner script

<div class="lineated verse">

A person is not an instrument.  
And not a backdrop.  
I note:  
**respect is the freeing of the other from my interpretation.**

</div>

#### 2030. The Holy Spirit in respect is the ability to accept another’s choices without the urge to make them like my own

<div class="lineated verse">

Even if I do not agree.  
Even if it is hard for me.  
And I understand:  
**respect is the freedom of another to be himself**  
**without my control.**

</div>

#### 2031. Respect is maturity that understands: my path is mine, his path is his

<div class="lineated verse">

I do not know,  
what he needs to go through,  
to come to maturity.  
I say:  
**respect is the refusal to decide another’s life for him.**

</div>

#### 2032. The Holy Spirit in respect is the ability to hold boundaries without anger and without fear of losing relationships

<div class="lineated verse">

Boundaries are not a wall.  
Boundaries are a map.  
And I understand:  
**respect is clarity of boundaries without aggression.**

</div>

#### 2033. Respect is the vision that every person acts from his depths, trauma, experience, abilities, and limitations

<div class="lineated verse">

I am not its cause.  
I am not its solution.  
And it is not for me to remake it.  
I note:  
**respect is the sobriety to understand another’s inner reality.**

</div>

#### 2034. The Holy Spirit in respect is the ability not to belittle another by one’s own superiority, nor by one’s own goodness, nor by one’s own “knowing better”

<div class="lineated verse">

And I understand:  
**respect is equality,**  
**which does not need confirmation.**

</div>

#### 2035. Respect is an inner silence in which I do not judge a person by the form of his actions, but see in him a subject, not an object

<div class="lineated verse">

I say:  
**respect is an attitude toward a person as toward a center,**  
**and not as toward a function.**

</div>

#### 2036. And one last thing: respect in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit is not politeness, not courtesy, not “proper” behavior, and not a social mask, but a deep acknowledgment of another’s wholeness, another’s freedom, another’s path, and another’s inner autonomy, without any attempt to control, judge, or appropriate

<div class="lineated verse">

Respect is freedom on both sides.  
And a precise phrase is born:  
**respect is the recognition of the other as a world,**  
**which does not have to coincide with me.**  
This is what respect is:  
even,  
calm,  
deep,  
not requiring explanation,  
and not depending on sympathy, agreement  
or closeness.

</div>

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT AS OF SORROW (when sorrow ceases to be destruction and becomes a space of deep attunement with life)

#### 2037. Grief is not destruction. Grief is living through the mismatch between “was” and “is no more.”

<div class="lineated verse">

Pain arises not from loss,  
but from the collision of fact and attachment.  
I see:  
**grief is honesty before irreversibility.**

</div>

#### 2038. The Holy Spirit in grief is the ability to endure a reality that can no longer be changed

<div class="lineated verse">

To endure means not to run away from it  
and not to hasten its passing through.  
It is clear to me:  
**grief is a form of inner maturity,**  
**which acknowledges the irreversible.**

</div>

#### 2039. Sorrow does not require strength. Sorrow requires honesty

<div class="lineated verse">

Strength tries to suppress.  
Honesty makes it possible to live through it.  
I note:  
**grief is the space where self-deception disappears.**

</div>

#### 2040. The Holy Spirit in grief is the gentleness that arises when resistance to reality is exhausted

<div class="lineated verse">

Pain remains,  
but the struggle against it ceases.  
And I understand:  
**grief is pain without struggle.**

</div>

#### 2041. Grief is not weakness and not a mistake. Grief is the way the psyche and the body align themselves with the new state of things.

<div class="lineated verse">

Not to break.  
To align oneself.  
I say:  
**grief is a process of adapting to absence.**

</div>

#### 2042. The Holy Spirit in sorrow is the ability to be in pain without being destroyed by it

<div class="lineated verse">

When pain is there,  
but I am greater than it.  
I can see:  
**sorrow is contact with the depths,**  
**which are opened only in extremity.**

</div>

#### 2043. Grief is both loss and clarification

<div class="lineated verse">

Loss is in the world of form.  
Clarification is in the world of meaning.  
I state:  
**grief is the moment when value becomes visible.**

</div>

#### 2044. The Holy Spirit in grief is a silence that asks for no words, no explanations, no consolations

<div class="lineated verse">

It is precisely silence that allows grief to pass.  
And I understand:  
**grief is the place where words become superfluous,**  
**and presence is the only thing that works.**

</div>

#### 2045. Grief is not something to be “overcome.”

<div class="lineated verse">

Grief is something to be lived through,  
so that what you carry forward is no longer pain,  
but clarity.  
When the living-through is complete,  
the pain dissolves,  
and the clarity remains.  
I say:  
**grief is a passage,**  
**not a state.**

</div>

#### 2046. And one last thing: grief in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit is not destruction, not punishment, and not a “lesson,” but a deep process of readjustment, in which a person stops clinging to what is no longer there, and learns to stand in what is.

<div class="lineated verse">

Grief is a depth,  
that is temporarily laid bare.  
And when that bareness is lived through honestly,  
a person comes out of grief  
not stronger,  
but more real.  
And the exact phrase is born:  
**grief is the place where life becomes truth.**  
This is grief:  
quiet,  
heavy,  
honest,  
not demanding haste,  
but not holding a person forever.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS ON LONELINESS (when loneliness ceases to be emptiness and becomes the space of the self)

**2047. Loneliness is not the absence of people. Loneliness is the absence of distractions from oneself.**

I see:

**loneliness is direct contact with one’s own depth.**

**2048. The Holy Spirit in solitude —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the ability to remain with oneself  
without trying to fill the emptiness by any means:  
with communication, entertainment, work, meaning.  
It is clear to me:  
**solitude is the space where a person stops running from himself.**

</div>

**2049. Loneliness lays bare that**

<div class="lineated verse">

which is usually hidden in the bustle.  
Not because loneliness is “worse,”  
but because it is quieter there.  
I note:  
**loneliness is a silence in which the real is heard.**

</div>

**2050. The Holy Spirit in solitude —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the capacity to endure emptiness,  
without turning it into tragedy.  
Emptiness is not an enemy.  
Emptiness is space.  
And I understand:  
**solitude is freedom without supports.**

</div>

**2051. Loneliness is not a sign of being unnecessary.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Loneliness is a sign of one’s own inner territory.  
When a person is left alone,  
he discovers boundaries  
that are unnoticed in the crowd.  
I say:  
**loneliness is a return to oneself.**

</div>

**2052. The Holy Spirit in solitude —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability to see the difference  
between “I am alone” and “I am lonely.”  
“I am alone” — is about a state.  
“I am lonely” — is about the pain of unfulfilled expectations.  
I can see:  
**solitude is a state,**  
**and solitude-pain is an interpretation.**

</div>

**2053. Loneliness is the maturity to dwell within oneself**

<div class="lineated verse">

without dependence on another’s presence  
or another’s affirmation.  
I record:  
**loneliness is a form of inner autonomy.**

</div>

**2054. The Holy Spirit in solitude —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the ability not to idealize others  
and not to devalue oneself.  
When a person is alone,  
the masks fall away,  
the roles disappear,  
and what truly is remains.  
And I understand:  
**solitude is truth without adornment.**

</div>

**2055. Loneliness is a space,**

<div class="lineated verse">

in which the inner voice becomes more audible.  
Not wiser —  
more audible.  
I say:  
**loneliness is tuning in to one’s own frequency.**

</div>

**2056. And the last thing: loneliness in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

this is not a void to be filled,

not a problem to be solved,

and not a state to be gotten rid of,

but the space of selfhood,

where supports disappear,

roles disappear,

external confirmations disappear,

and a person remains,

as he is.

Loneliness is not punishment.

Loneliness is a form of clarity.

And a precise phrase is born:

**loneliness is the space**

**in which a person ceases to be a figure among figures**

**and becomes himself.**

This is what loneliness is:

quiet,

steady,

stripping bare,

and always—honest.

### OF THE HOLY SPIRIT AS WEAKNESS (when weakness ceases to be a flaw and becomes a form of transparency before life)

**2047. Weakness is not the absence of strength.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Weakness is the moment when strength ceases to conceal the true state.  
I see:  
**weakness is truth, spoken without defense.**

</div>

**2048. The Holy Spirit in weakness —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability not to pretend to be strong,  
when there is no support within.  
Pretending exhausts.  
Honesty sets free.  
It is clear to me:  
**weakness is a return to the reality of one’s inner state.**

</div>

**2049. Weakness makes a person vulnerable,**

<div class="lineated verse">

but it is precisely vulnerability  
that makes deep contact with oneself possible  
and with others.  
I note:  
**weakness is the stripping away of the layer of impossibility of being seen.**

</div>

**2050. The Holy Spirit in weakness —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the silence in which the need to prove  
that I am “all right” disappears.  
There is no need to be “all right”.  
One needs to be honest.  
And I understand:  
**weakness is the place where the inner spectacle comes to an end.**

</div>

**2051. Weakness is not failure.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Weakness is the point at which a person stops fighting himself  
and admits:  
“here is my boundary.”  
To acknowledge a boundary is maturity.  
I say:  
**weakness is the acknowledgment of one’s limits without humiliation.**

</div>

**2052. The Holy Spirit in weakness —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the ability to let go of the role of the strong,  
with which one often tries to shield pain,  
fear, or confusion.  
The image of strength is armor.  
Weakness is the laying aside of armor.  
I see:  
**weakness is a form of inner nakedness,**  
**which makes a person alive, not conceptual.**

</div>

**2053. Weakness is the moment in which the illusions of control disappear.**

<div class="lineated verse">

It is here that true resilience appears—  
not from effort,  
but from honesty.  
I record:  
**weakness is transparency,**  
**which does not hold itself together by force.**

</div>

**2054. The Holy Spirit in weakness —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability to allow yourself to collapse as far  
as one collapses.  
But no deeper.  
And I understand:  
**weakness is trust that reality will bear me as I am.**

</div>

**2055. Weakness is the space where a person sees,**

<div class="lineated verse">

that his value does not disappear,  
when strength disappears.  
Value is deeper than strength.  
Strength is only an instrument.  
I say:  
**weakness is the moment when it becomes visible,**  
**that I am not my strength.**

</div>

**2056. And one last thing: weakness in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

is not a breach,

not a defect,

not a “lower state,”

and not something to be gotten rid of at once,

but a space of inner honesty,

in which masks disappear,

roles disappear,

the need to meet expectations disappears,

and there remains a person

as a living, feeling being,

not obliged to be perfect.

Weakness is not a fall.

Weakness is an exit from rigidity.

And a precise phrase is born:

**weakness is the place where a person stops holding themselves together,**

**and for the first time can be themselves.**

That is weakness:

not negative,

not positive,

but simply — transparent.

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS FREEDOM (when freedom ceases to be a choice and becomes an inner unconditionedness)

**2057. Freedom is not the number of choices.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Freedom is the absence of inner compulsion.  
One can have thousands of choices  
and not be free.  
One can have one —  
and be free.  
I see:  
**freedom is a state, not a set of possibilities.**

</div>

**2058. The Holy Spirit in freedom —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the ending of inner pressure:  
“must,”  
“ought,”  
“right,”  
“that is the custom.”  
When these voices fall silent,  
a space of choice appears,  
which does not seem like choice —  
it seems like a natural act.  
It is clear to me:  
**freedom is the absence of inner violence.**

</div>

**2059. Freedom is the ability to act not from fear.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Not to avoid punishment,  
not to seek approval,  
not to run from anxiety.  
When fear ceases to be the guide,  
action becomes steady.  
I record:  
**freedom is action not from fear,**  
**but from clarity.**

</div>

**2060. The Holy Spirit in freedom —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the ability to see one’s own causes:  
where I am moved by habit,  
where — by impulse,  
where — by compensatory pain,  
where — by honest desire.  
And I understand:  
**freedom is the transparency of motives.**

</div>

**2061. Freedom is not “I do what I want.”**

<div class="lineated verse">

Freedom is “I do not do what controls me.”  
Most unfree actions are automatic.  
Free ones are always conscious.  
I say:  
**freedom is the ending of the authority of automatisms.**

</div>

**2062. The Holy Spirit in freedom —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is an inner balance,  
in which choice is not colored by anxiety,  
not burdened by a sense of duty,  
and not smeared with the desire to conform.  
The silence of expectations is a space of freedom.  
I see it:  
**freedom is the silence of other voices within.**

</div>

**2063. Freedom is the honesty to see oneself**

<div class="lineated verse">

without illusions:  
one’s envy,  
one’s fear,  
one’s dependence,  
one’s weakness,  
one’s pain.  
Not denying —  
and not justifying.  
I note:  
**freedom is sobriety in relation to oneself.**

</div>

**2064. The Holy Spirit in freedom —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the ability to say “no,”  
when a boundary is crossed,  
and “yes,”  
when the action is ripe.  
And both —  
without aggression  
and without guilt.  
And I understand:  
**freedom is clarity of boundaries without fear of losing relationships.**

</div>

**2065. Freedom is the absence of an inner need**

<div class="lineated verse">

to be someone defined:  
comfortable,  
successful,  
strong,  
right,  
spiritual.  
When the need to conform to an image disappears,  
there appears action,  
which coincides with reality.  
I say:  
**freedom is life without a role.**

</div>

**2066. And lastly: freedom in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

it is not an ideal,

not a spiritual status,

and not a victory over circumstances,

but an inner unconditionedness,

in which action comes out of clarity,

and not out of pressure;

out of honesty,

and not out of fear;

out of understanding,

and not out of habit;

out of presence,

and not out of inner fuss.

Freedom is not the number of options.

Freedom is the quality of consciousness.

And the precise phrase is born:

**freedom is when nothing within makes me be other than myself.**

This is freedom:

even,

quiet,

without effort,

without pathos,

and always — transparent.

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS LOVE (when love ceases to be an emotion and becomes a form of inner wholeness)

**2067. Love is not a feeling.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Feelings come and go.  
Love remains.  
I see:  
**love is a state in which I am not divided within.**

</div>

**2068. The Holy Spirit in love —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the silence in which inner conflict disappears:  
between “what I am”  
and “what I must be.”  
When the conflict disappears,  
a steadiness arises, like warmth.  
It is clear to me:  
**love is harmony with oneself.**

</div>

**2069. Love is not an urge,**

<div class="lineated verse">

not a dependency,  
not a need to be needed,  
not a fear of losing.  
These are forms of attachment,  
and attachment is not love.  
I note:  
**love is the absence of grasping.**

</div>

**2070. The Holy Spirit in love —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability to see a person whole:  
not through a role,  
not through expectation,  
not through fear,  
not through projection.  
And I understand:  
**love is clear seeing of another without distorting them with oneself.**

</div>

**2071. Love is not “I feel good with you,”**

<div class="lineated verse">

not “I need you,”  
not “I cannot live without you.”  
Love is a state  
in which I do not become less  
to gain someone’s presence.  
I say:  
**love is a space**  
**in which no one makes himself less.**

</div>

**2072. The Holy Spirit in love —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability to be with a person,  
without demanding that he fill my emptiness.  
Love does not make the other an instrument.  
I see:  
**love is the freedom of two sides,**  
**and not mutual exploitation.**

</div>

**2073. Love is not an act, but acts grow out of it.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Not “how should I behave,”  
but “how could I do otherwise.”  
I note:  
**love is naturalness, not effort.**

</div>

**2074. The Holy Spirit in love —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the ability to remain open,  
without dissolving,  
and protected,  
without closing oneself off.  
And I understand:  
**love is boundaries without fear**  
**and openness without dependence.**

</div>

**2075. Love is the quality of presence.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Not what I feel,  
but the way I am.  
It is a form of being,  
in which there is no inner rupture  
and no need to hide.  
I say:  
**love is when, within, there is nothing that needs to be proved.**

</div>

**2076. And lastly: love in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is not attachment,  
not passion,  
not dependence,  
not exchange,  
not a promise,  
and not a feeling that can be summoned or lost.  
Love is a state of inner wholeness,  
in which I am not against myself,  
not in conflict with myself,  
and out of this lack of conflict  
I can be with another  
so that he remains himself  
and I remain myself.  
Love is the maturity of form.  
And the exact phrase is born:  
**love is a space in which no one must become another**  
**in order to be accepted.**  
This is love:  
steady,  
quiet,  
honest,  
not demanding reciprocity,  
and always — without violence.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS FORGIVENESS (when forgiveness ceases to be an act of will and becomes the consequence of vision)

**2077. Forgiveness is not an action.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Forgiveness is a state,  
in which offense is no longer being held.  
That which held it — has vanished.  
That which clung — has weakened.  
I see:  
**forgiveness is not to “forgive,”**  
**but to stop holding on.**

</div>

**2078. The Holy Spirit in forgiveness —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability to see:  
that another’s actions were the result of his state,  
and not of my qualities.  
Not “they did this to me,”  
but “the person acted out of his pain, immaturity, fear.”  
It is clear to me:  
**forgiveness comes when I stop taking another’s behavior personally.**

</div>

**2079. Forgiveness does not cancel the fact.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Forgiveness cancels the interpretation of the fact as a personal blow.  
The fact remains.  
The pain may have been there.  
But reproach disappears.  
I record:  
**forgiveness is the ungluing of fact and personal meaning.**

</div>

**2080. The Holy Spirit in forgiveness —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability to see another not through his error,  
but through his wholeness,  
which is broader than any one of his deeds.  
And I understand:  
**forgiveness is the restoration of the scale of vision.**

</div>

**2081. Forgiveness is not, “I generously let you go.”**

<div class="lineated verse">

Forgiveness is, “you no longer fill my consciousness.”  
I say:  
**forgiveness is the freeing of space within,**  
**which was occupied by offense.**

</div>

**2082. The Holy Spirit in forgiveness —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ceasing of the inward demand:  
“he must understand,”  
“he must apologize,”  
“he must mend his ways.”  
When expectation disappears,  
resentment disappears as well.  
It is clear to me:  
**forgiveness is freedom from unrealizable expectations.**

</div>

**2083. Forgiveness is not about the other.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Forgiveness is about me.  
Resentment is holding on.  
Forgiveness is the cessation of holding on.  
I state:  
**forgiveness is the restoration of one’s own wholeness.**

</div>

**2084. The Holy Spirit in forgiveness —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability to see,  
that my pain was real,  
but it does not have to become my identity.  
And I understand:  
**forgiveness is keeping hold of your pain,**  
**but refusing to build a life around it.**

</div>

**2085. Forgiveness appears when**

<div class="lineated verse">

I stop demanding of the world  
the justice  
it never promised.  
This is not surrender.  
This is sobriety.  
I say:  
**forgiveness is the maturity to look at reality without the illusion of a just world.**  
**2086**  
And lastly: forgiveness in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —  
it is not a process of suppressing resentment,  
not a moral feat,  
and not violence against oneself,  
when I force myself to “be above it.”  
Forgiveness is the natural consequence of seeing:  
when I see another’s motives,  
the limitations of his state,  
the blindness of his moment,  
and understand  
that the pain caused is the result of human unawareness,  
and not a malicious design against me.  
Forgiveness is the dissolution of the personal meaning of pain.  
And the exact phrase is born:  
**forgiveness is the moment when I see the cause of pain more clearly than the pain itself.**  
This is forgiveness:  
steady,  
non-heroic,  
nonviolent,  
not directed at another,  
but only — toward freeing space within.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS DIGNITY (when dignity ceases to be self-respect and becomes inner consistency)

**2087. Dignity is not pride and not status.**

<div class="lineated verse">

It is the ability to remain true to oneself  
even then,  
when it is inconvenient,  
unprofitable  
or frightening.  
I see:  
**dignity is inner straightness.**

</div>

**2088. The Holy Spirit in dignity —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability not to betray oneself  
for the sake of another’s comfort,  
approval,  
or fear of being rejected.  
It is clear to me:  
**dignity is fidelity to one’s own truth.**

</div>

**2089. Dignity is not how I look.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Dignity is that,  
what I will not allow myself to do,  
if it destroys my integrity.  
I set down:  
**dignity is a boundary,**  
**that is not for sale.**

</div>

**2090. The Holy Spirit in dignity —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability to say “no,”  
without explaining oneself,  
when “yes” would mean renouncing oneself.  
And I understand:  
**dignity is the ability to hold a boundary**  
**without aggression and without guilt.**

</div>

**2091. Dignity is the absence of inner duality:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when what I feel  
matches what I allow myself to do.  
I say:  
**dignity is harmony between the inner and the outer.**

</div>

**2092. The Holy Spirit in dignity —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability not to be shattered by another’s opinion.  
Opinion is another’s view.  
Dignity is the steadiness of one’s own point.  
It is clear to me:  
**dignity is independence from external judgments.**

</div>

**2093. Dignity is the ability to be vulnerable**

<div class="lineated verse">

without self-abasement  
and to be strong  
without superiority.  
Both come from the same point.  
I state:  
**dignity is balance,**  
**which requires no role.**

</div>

**2094. The Holy Spirit in dignity —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability not to take revenge,  
not to humiliate,  
not to play at power.  
Power without dignity is violence.  
Power with dignity is clarity.  
And I understand:  
**dignity is a form of pure power,**  
**not directed against another.**

</div>

**2095. Dignity is an inner standard,**

<div class="lineated verse">

that is not imposed from outside  
and does not depend on circumstances.  
It is not “I must be better.”  
It is “I do not want to be worse than myself.”  
I say:  
**dignity is a form of self-simplicity.**

</div>

**2096. And the last thing: dignity in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is not a social status,  
not a moral virtue,  
and not an image to be maintained,  
but an inner coherence,  
in which a person’s actions coincide with his truth,  
words coincide with his intention,  
boundaries coincide with reality,  
and choices do not destroy the self.  
Dignity is wholeness,  
and not display.  
And the exact phrase is born:  
**dignity is when I do not do that,**  
**after which I become less myself.**  
This is what dignity is:  
even,  
quiet,  
without pose,  
without heroism,  
and always—honest.

</div>

### OF THE HOLY SPIRIT AS TRUTH (when truth ceases to be knowledge and becomes an inner unsulliedness)

**2097. Truth is not what is “right.”**

<div class="lineated verse">

Truth is what is uncorrupted.  
And I see:  
**truth is the absence of inner falsehood.**

</div>

**2098. The Holy Spirit in truth —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability to look at reality  
without embellishment  
and without smoothing it into a desired image.  
Without the “how it should be.”  
Only the “how it is.”  
It is clear to me:  
**truth is direct contact with fact.**

</div>

**2099. Truth is not a set of knowledge.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Sometimes to know less is more honest,  
than to have answers for everything.  
I record:  
**truth is the acknowledgment of the boundary of one’s understanding.**

</div>

**2100. The Holy Spirit in truth —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability to see one’s own motives:  
where I lie to myself,  
where I manipulate,  
where I avoid,  
where I pass off fear as principle.  
And I understand:  
**truth is transparency in regard to one’s own movements.**

</div>

**2101. Truth does not require proof.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Proofs are an instrument of dispute.  
Truth is a state beyond dispute.  
I say:  
**truth is clarity, not victory in argument.**

</div>

**2102. The Holy Spirit in truth —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the absence of inner conflict between knowing and action.  
When I know one thing,  
say another,  
do a third —  
this is not the absence of truth,  
but the absence of coherence.  
It is clear to me:  
**truth is the agreement of the inner and the outer.**

</div>

**2103. Truth is not what “hurts.”**

<div class="lineated verse">

Pain is only an indicator of  
the illusion collapsing.  
Truth in itself is calm.  
I record:  
**truth is the silence after a shattered illusion.**

</div>

**2104. The Holy Spirit in truth —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability not to flee from reality  
into concept,  
into explanations,  
into self-justifications.  
And I understand:  
**truth is straightness without interpretive crutches.**  
**2105.**  
Truth is not final knowledge,  
but the ability to endure reality as it is,  
as it manifests itself now.  
The future may clarify.  
The past — distort.  
But the moment is always exact.  
I say:  
**truth is the honesty of the moment.**

</div>

**2106. And lastly: truth in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

it is not a system of views,

not a worldview,

not a doctrine,

not an absolute formula,

and not a statement about “how everything is arranged.”

Truth is a state of inner un-falsifiedness,

in which a person stops replacing reality with his own desires,

stops justifying his actions with false motives,

stops pretending before himself,

and for the first time looks straight ahead.

Truth is honesty,

multiplied by clarity,

multiplied by the absence of fear.

And the exact phrase is born:

**truth is what remains**

**when you stop lying to yourself.**

This is truth:

steady,

quiet,

honest,

not requiring proof,

and always simple.

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS MEANING (when meaning ceases to be a search and becomes a relation to life)

**2107. Meaning is not the answer to the question “why.”**

<div class="lineated verse">

Meaning is a state in which action coincides  
with a person’s inner structure.  
I see:  
**meaning is coincidence, not explanation.**

</div>

**2108. The Holy Spirit in the sense —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability to see a direction,  
that is not imposed by fear,  
not dictated by expectations,  
and not determined by what is “right.”  
It is clear to me:  
**meaning is a direction that arises without external pressure.**

</div>

**2109. Meaning is not something that is found.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Meaning is what is born in the process of action.  
It does not precede the path—  
it arises as the step is taken.  
I record:  
**meaning is the quality of motion, not its goal.**

</div>

**2110. The Holy Spirit in the sense —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the ability to discern:  
where my action is living,  
and where it is mechanical.  
Mechanical action contains no meaning  
even if it is “important.”  
And I understand:  
**meaning is the livingness within action.**

</div>

**2111. Meaning does not arise in attempts to “find one’s purpose”.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Such a purpose is always a fantasy,  
created by the mind for reassurance.  
True meaning is born much more humbly:  
in the exactness of the step that matched me.  
I say:  
**meaning is the coincidence of oneself and action**  
**in one moment of time.**

</div>

**2112. The Holy Spirit in the sense —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability to be attentive to what,  
what within responds,  
and what is empty.  
Response is alive.  
Emptiness is mechanical.  
I see it:  
**meaning is a response recognized without compulsion.**

</div>

**2113. Meaning is not a result,**

<div class="lineated verse">

which must be obtained.  
Meaning is the feeling that the path is right.  
If the path is true,  
then meaning is present  
even in difficulty.  
I note:  
**meaning is the inward rightness of the step,**  
**and not outward success.**

</div>

**2114. The Holy Spirit in meaning —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability not to force the moments,  
when there is no meaning.  
The absence of meaning is not a failure.  
It is a period of regrouping.  
And I understand:  
**meaning is a rhythm, not a constant quantity.**

</div>

**2115. Meaning is the experience of correspondence:**

<div class="lineated verse">

between me and the action,  
between the action and the situation,  
between the situation and time.  
When everything has come into alignment —  
silence arises,  
which says:  
“So it should be.”  
I say:  
**meaning is the silence of assent.**

</div>

**2116. And lastly: meaning in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is not a formula found,  
not an explanation of one’s path,  
and not an answer that gives confidence.  
Meaning is a form of inner harmony with life,  
which cannot be received from outside,  
but can be known within:  
as a response,  
as a correspondence,  
as a feeling that the step is right,  
which does not require proof.  
Meaning is not a goal.  
Meaning is a way of being.  
And the exact phrase is born:  
**meaning is the feeling that I am in my place**  
**and in my movement.**  
This is meaning:  
not abstract,  
not exalted,  
not imposed,  
but inner,  
quiet,  
one’s own.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS COURAGE (when courage ceases to be overcoming and becomes the ability to remain in truth even in fear)

**2117. Courage is not the absence of fear.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Courage is the ability not to let fear  
change my decisions.  
I see:  
**courage is action without surrender to fear.**

</div>

**2118. The Holy Spirit in courage —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability to acknowledge fear,  
without turning it into a guide for action.  
Fear is seen.  
But it does not rule.  
It is clear to me:  
**courage is when fear is present,**  
**but does not determine.**

</div>

**2119. Courage is the honesty to remain oneself**

<div class="lineated verse">

in a situation where it is easier to become someone else:  
convenient,  
quiet,  
false,  
accommodating.  
I note:  
**courage is inner consistency in the face of pressure.**

</div>

**2120. The Holy Spirit in courage —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability not to flee:  
not into fantasy,  
not into excuses,  
not into illusions,  
not into aggression.  
And I understand:  
**courage is an encounter with reality without trying to go around it.**

</div>

**2121. Courage is not a shove,**

<div class="lineated verse">

not a flash of strength,  
not a one-time effort.  
Courage is steadiness:  
step by step,  
breath by breath,  
without losing oneself.  
I say:  
**courage is a quiet form of steadfastness.**

</div>

**2122. The Holy Spirit in courage —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability to endure uncertainty.  
Fear feeds on the unknown.  
Courage — by being present in it.  
I can see:  
**courage is the ability to be in the unknown without panic.**

</div>

**2123. Courage is the ability not to trade truth**

<div class="lineated verse">

for quick comfort:  
not to betray oneself,  
in order to avoid tension.  
I note:  
**courage is fidelity to oneself in the moment of pressure.**

</div>

**2124. The Holy Spirit in courage —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability not to confuse strength with aggression.  
Aggression is a reaction.  
Strength is clarity.  
And I understand:  
**courage is strength without destruction.**

</div>

**2125. Courage is humility before the fact**

<div class="lineated verse">

that I may be afraid,  
it may be painful,  
it may be hard —  
and still a step is possible.  
Not in spite of fear,  
but beside it.  
I say:  
**courage is a step taken together with fear,**  
**and not instead of it.**

</div>

**2126. And lastly: courage in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is not a feat,  
not a display of endurance,  
and not victory over fear.  
Courage is inner honesty,  
which makes it possible to remain in truth,  
not to hide,  
not to pretend,  
and not to make choices  
that destroy integrity,  
even then,  
when fear is real,  
when the risk is palpable,  
and when the unknown is great.  
Courage is the maturity to remain oneself  
where it is easier not to be oneself.  
And the precise phrase is born:  
**courage is the ability not to betray oneself**  
**in the presence of fear.**  
This is courage:  
steady,  
quiet,  
unbreakable in its honesty,  
and not requiring outward demonstration.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS JOY (when joy ceases to be an emotion and becomes a form of inner liveliness)

**2127. Joy is not excitement and not euphoria.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Joy is an inner movement,  
in which life is felt as flowing.  
I see:  
**joy is aliveness without a cause.**

</div>

**2128. The Holy Spirit in joy —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability to feel life as it is,  
as it is,  
without demanding  
that it be better.  
It is clear to me:  
**joy is the absence of the inner condition, “I will be happy if…”.**

</div>

**2129. Joy is not a reward for success.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Joy is a return to naturalness,  
which was blocked by tension.  
When tension dissolves —  
joy appears.  
I note:  
**joy is a side effect of inner relaxation.**

</div>

**2130. The Holy Spirit in joy —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability to be open to the moment,  
without trying to hold it  
or repeat it.  
And I understand:  
**joy is openness,**  
**and not possession.**

</div>

**2131. Joy is the experience of fullness:**

<div class="lineated verse">

not “much”,  
but “enough”.  
Not greed,  
but contentment with the simple fact of existence.  
I say:  
**joy is consent to life in the form**  
**in which it comes.**

</div>

**2132. The Holy Spirit in joy —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability to feel lightness,  
which does not cancel out difficulties,  
but is not reduced to them.  
Lightness is not superficiality.  
Lightness is spaciousness.  
It is clear to Me:  
**joy is space within,**  
**and not an external stimulus.**

</div>

**2133. Joy is not an emotional flash.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Emotions flare up and fade away.  
Joy is not a flash,  
but a background.  
I note:  
**joy is a quiet inner radiance,**  
**which does not have to be bright.**

</div>

**2134. The Holy Spirit in joy —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the absence of inner tension,  
which makes life seem heavy.  
When the tension lets go,  
even simple things come alive.  
And I understand:  
**joy is the ability to see the living in the ordinary.**

</div>

**2135. Joy is a form of gratitude,**

<div class="lineated verse">

which does not need an object.  
Not, “I am grateful for something,”  
but, “I am grateful that I am.”  
I say:  
**joy is gratitude for existence.**

</div>

**2136. And lastly: joy in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is not an emotion,  
not a flash,  
not a reaction,  
and not a state that must be held onto.  
Joy is a form of inner vitality,  
that comes to light,  
when inner tension disappears,  
when there is no resistance to life,  
when presence becomes natural,  
and when a person does not try to replace reality  
with images of what it should be.  
Joy is a transparency  
through which life flows.  
And a precise phrase is born:  
**joy is the feeling**  
**that nothing hinders living.**  
This is joy:  
gentle,  
even,  
natural,  
not requiring a reason  
and not seeking to make an impression.

</div>

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT AS FAITHFULNESS (when faithfulness ceases to be a duty and becomes an inner accord with oneself)

**2137. Faithfulness is not about promises to others.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Faithfulness is about being consistent before yourself.  
I see:  
**faithfulness is honesty without witnesses.**

</div>

**2138. The Holy Spirit in faithfulness —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the ability to hold to the chosen direction,  
if that direction is in agreement with me,  
and not with the expectations of others.  
It is clear to Me:  
**faithfulness is steadfastness in that,**  
**which is mine, and not imposed.**

</div>

**2139. Fidelity is not clinging.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Fidelity is a deep, consistent movement:  
when my decisions today  
do not betray my yesterday-Self.  
I note:  
**fidelity is inner continuity.**

</div>

**2140. The Holy Spirit in faithfulness —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the ability to say “no,”  
when “yes” would mean renouncing oneself.  
Faithfulness is not compliance.  
It is a boundary.  
And I understand:  
**faithfulness is the defense of one’s inner structure.**

</div>

**2141. Fidelity is not loyalty to a person.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Fidelity is loyalty to the essence.  
I say:  
**fidelity is the ability to stand with that**  
**which is experienced within as truth.**

</div>

**2142. The Holy Spirit in faithfulness —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the absence of the need to justify one’s choices  
or prove them to anyone.  
If a choice rests on depth —  
it is clear without explanations.  
I see:  
**faithfulness is clarity without display.**

</div>

**2143. Faithfulness is not about relationships with others.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Faithfulness is the foundation,  
on which any relationship is built.  
Without faithfulness to oneself  
true faithfulness to another is impossible.  
I record:  
**faithfulness is honesty,**  
**which I do not betray for the sake of comfort.**

</div>

**2144. The Holy Spirit in faithfulness —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability to remain on the chosen path  
not out of fear of changing direction,  
but because the path is in accord with me.  
And I understand:  
**faithfulness is the absence of inner doubt about the direction,**  
**when the direction is true.**

</div>

**2145. Fidelity is the ability not to scatter oneself**

<div class="lineated verse">

across endless outer impulses,  
which demand attention,  
but do not correspond to me.  
I say:  
**fidelity is the holding of one’s center.**

</div>

**2146. And lastly: fidelity in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is not a vow,  
not a duty,  
not an obligation to someone or something.  
Fidelity is inner coherence,  
which makes a person whole:  
his words are an extension of his intention,  
his actions are an extension of his choice,  
his choices are an extension of his essence.  
Fidelity is not about another.  
Fidelity is a form of selfhood.  
And a precise phrase is born:  
**fidelity is when I do not take steps**  
**that diminish me.**  
This is fidelity:  
even,  
steady,  
few in words,  
and always—honest.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS PEACE (when peace ceases to be a feeling and becomes an inner immutability)

**2147. Peace is not the absence of problems.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Problems may be.  
Peace remains.  
I see:  
**peace is stability without strain.**

</div>

**2148. The Holy Spirit in the world —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability not to create needless conflict within  
about that  
which has already happened.  
Not to add resistance  
to the fact.  
It is clear to me:  
**peace is the absence of an inner “no” to what is.**

</div>

**2149. Peace is not relaxation.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Relaxation is bodily.  
Peace is deeper.  
Peace is the feeling,  
that I coincide with the moment.  
I note:  
**peace is alignment with reality.**

</div>

**2150. The Holy Spirit in the world —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the cessation of the inner race:  
“to make it in time,”  
“not to be late,”  
“not to lose,”  
“not to make a mistake.”  
When the race ceases —  
there remains space.  
And I understand:  
**peace is an inner expanse,**  
**which does not depend on the speed of outer life.**

</div>

**2151. Peace is not positivity and not optimism.**

<div class="lineated verse">

It is not the faith that everything will be well.  
It is the recognition,  
that even if it is bad,  
there is a point in me,  
that does not collapse.  
I say:  
**peace is the knowing of one’s inner support.**

</div>

**2152. The Holy Spirit in the world —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability not to spin oneself out in thoughts into anxiety.  
Anxiety is acceleration.  
Peace is the absence of acceleration.  
It is clear to me:  
**peace is the wheel of inner noise brought to a stop.**

</div>

**2153. Peace is not indifference.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Indifference is avoidance.  
Peace is presence.  
Presence without struggle.  
I note:  
**peace is silence without disconnection.**

</div>

**2154. The Holy Spirit in the world —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the ability to experience strong emotions  
without destroying the inner center.  
Emotions come and go.  
The center — remains.  
And I understand:  
**peace — is the steadiness of the center amid the waves.**

</div>

**2155. Peace is the absence of inner conflict**

<div class="lineated verse">

between “what is” and “what should be.”  
When conflict disappears —  
evenness arises.  
I say:  
**peace is inner wholeness, not torn apart.**

</div>

**2156. And lastly: peace in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

this is not a state of rest,

not the absence of pain,

not deliverance from outward troubles,

and not the result of a meditative technique.

Peace is an inner unchangeableness

that does not waver with every outward impulse,

that does not fall apart under the pressure of circumstances,

and that does not depend on whether

I am comfortable now.

Peace is not the absence of waves.

Peace is the stillness of the deep,

on which the waves leave no trace.

And a precise phrase is born:

**peace is when nothing within argues with life.**

This is peace:

even,

quiet,

deep,

always accessible,

when inner noise ceases to imitate reality.

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT AS HUMILITY (when humility ceases to be submissiveness and becomes a clear vision of one’s place in reality)

**2157. Humility is not “I am less.”**

<div class="lineated verse">

Nor is it “I am worse.”  
Humility is “I am no more than I am.”  
I see:  
**humility is the absence of self-inflation.**

</div>

**2158. The Holy Spirit in humility —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability to acknowledge the boundaries:  
of ability,  
of knowledge,  
of strength,  
of influence.  
Boundaries are not weakness.  
Boundaries are a map.  
It is clear to me:  
**humility is the honest knowledge of limits,**  
**without shame and without struggle.**

</div>

**2159. Humility is the renunciation of the illusion of control.**

<div class="lineated verse">

I do not govern the universe.  
I do not govern others.  
I do not govern even all my own states.  
And when this illusion lets go,  
silence arises.  
I note:  
**humility is sobriety.**

</div>

**2160. The Holy Spirit in humility —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the ability to be in reality as it is,  
just as it is,  
without trying to force it into your own schemes.  
And I understand:  
**humility is the absence of inner violence against reality.**

</div>

**2161. Humility is not weakness and not passivity.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Humility is clarity:  
what I can change,  
and what I cannot.  
And the difference between them ceases to be tormenting.  
I say:  
**humility is understanding one’s sphere of action.**

</div>

**2162. The Holy Spirit in humility —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability to stop demanding guarantees from life.  
Guarantees are an illusion of control.  
Humility is trust in the movement.  
I can see:  
**humility is the readiness to live without the safety nets of thoughts.**

</div>

**2163. Humility is the ability to step back,**

<div class="lineated verse">

when movement is unnecessary,  
and to move forward,  
when the time is ripe.  
No resistance,  
no forcing.  
I record:  
**humility is agreement with the rhythm.**

</div>

**2164. The Holy Spirit in humility —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the absence of posing.  
A humble person does not display humility.  
He simply does not resist reality,  
and does not ascribe to himself that  
which is not there.  
And I understand:  
**humility is transparency without a role.**

</div>

**2165. Humility is the ability to say:**

<div class="lineated verse">

“I do not know,”  
when I truly do not know.  
It is the highest honesty,  
which opens the door to understanding.  
I say:  
**humility is the refusal of false certainty.**

</div>

**2166. And lastly: humility in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is not submission,  
not self-abasement,  
and not a refusal of action.  
Humility is a state of inner clarity,  
in which a person stops exaggerating his own role,  
stops struggling with what is beyond his power,  
stops demanding that the world conform to his expectations,  
and begins to live in agreement with reality,  
rather than with a fantasy about it.  
Humility is maturity.  
And this exact phrase is born:  
**humility is the exact sense of one’s own size**  
**within the scale of reality.**  
That is humility:  
quiet,  
unnoticed,  
without pose,  
without superiority,  
and always — honest.  
If you want,  
I will continue —  
about how the Holy Spirit manifests in memory,  
when memory ceases to be an archive  
and becomes a way of recognizing oneself in one’s own experience.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS MEMORY (when memory ceases to be an archive and becomes a way of recognizing oneself in one’s own experience)

**2167. Memory is not a storehouse of events.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Memory is a way,  
by which consciousness creates continuity between “I was” and “I am”.  
I see:  
**memory is an instrument of continuity,**  
**not a catalog of facts.**

</div>

**2168. The Holy Spirit in memory —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the ability to see in the past  
not a sequence of failures and victories,  
but the trajectory of becoming.  
It is clear to me:  
**memory is a mirror of the path, not a list of grievances.**

</div>

**2169. Memory is not the restoration of accuracy.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Memory is the restoration of meaning.  
Consciousness never stores facts in full,  
but always stores interpretations,  
which helped one live.  
I record:  
**memory is meaning fixed in time.**

</div>

**2170. The Holy Spirit in memory —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the ability to see:  
I always interpreted reality  
in accordance with the level of understanding  
that was available then.  
And I understand:  
**memory is the story of growth,**  
**not the fixation of shortcomings.**

</div>

**2171. Memory is not punishment.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Memory is navigation.  
It is not made for the painful repetition of pain,  
but so that one may see the trajectory:  
where I was moving,  
what patterns repeated,  
where I became myself,  
and where — I was lost.  
I say:  
**memory is a map,**  
**and not a stone that must be carried.**

</div>

**2172. The Holy Spirit in memory —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the ability to see the past  
without self-blame  
and without self-glorification.  
Neither to overstate,  
nor to understate.  
I see:  
**memory — is the sober gaze upon what was,**  
**without any attempt to correct it after the fact.**

</div>

**2173. Memory is not what holds a person.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Memory is what shows what was holding.  
Fears, motives, expectations —  
all becomes clearer,  
when I look back without accusation.  
I note:  
**memory is an understanding of causes,**  
**and not being stuck in consequences.**

</div>

**2174. The Holy Spirit in memory —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability to let the past be past.  
Not to rewrite.  
Not to erase.  
Not to punish oneself.  
Simply to let it take its place.  
And I understand:  
**memory is agreement with that**  
**which can no longer be changed.**

</div>

**2175. Memory is a dialogue with my former self.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Not in order to correct him,  
but to understand,  
who I have become today.  
I say:  
**memory is a way of recognizing oneself across time.**

</div>

**2176. And lastly: memory in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is not an archive,  
not a set of facts,  
not an instrument of nostalgia,  
and not a source of shame.  
Memory is the structure of experience,  
which allows a person to understand his trajectory,  
to see patterns,  
to discern what is unhealed,  
to recognize maturity,  
and to accept today’s step  
not as an isolated act,  
but as a continuation of a long path.  
Memory is not a chain.  
Memory is context.  
And the exact phrase is born:  
**memory is a way to see**  
**that I did not arise by chance.**  
This is memory:  
steady,  
quiet,  
honest,  
without a condemning shade,  
and always — about clarification,  
not about holding on.

</div>

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT AS SILENCE (when silence ceases to be the absence of sound and becomes a space of clarity)

**2177. Silence is not a sound, nor the absence of it.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Silence is the ceasing of inner haste.  
I see:  
**silence is stillness within motion.**

</div>

**2178. The Holy Spirit in stillness —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability to hear that  
which remains after  
thoughts cease to sound.  
Not a voice.  
Not a message.  
But simple clarity:  
“so it is.”  
It is clear to me:  
**silence is vision without commentary.**

</div>

**2179. Silence is not the cessation of thoughts.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Thoughts may continue.  
But the inner noise disappears.  
I note:  
**silence is when thoughts no longer lead me.**

</div>

**2180. The Holy Spirit in silence —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability not to interfere with the flow of experience:  
not to judge,  
not to try to fix,  
not to avoid.  
And I understand:  
**silence is presence without reaction.**

</div>

**2181. Silence is honesty,**

<div class="lineated verse">

which does not need words.  
When words no longer improve understanding,  
silence becomes more exact than speech.  
I say:  
**silence is precision without formulation.**

</div>

**2182. The Holy Spirit in silence —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is that space,  
in which the need to be someone disappears:  
right,  
spiritual,  
intelligent,  
convincing.  
And in this disappearance, freedom appears.  
I see this:  
**silence is a state without a role.**

</div>

**2183. Silence is the ability to perceive reality directly,**

<div class="lineated verse">

without filters and without trying to improve it.  
Not “what does it mean?”  
but “how is it?”.  
I note:  
**silence is directness.**

</div>

**2184. The Holy Spirit in silence is—**

<div class="lineated verse">

the absence of inner compulsion to speak,  
to act,  
to move,  
to explain.  
And I understand:  
**silence is rest from the need to be the author of one’s own experience.**

</div>

**2185. Silence is a place,**

<div class="lineated verse">

in which knowledge arises without effort.  
Not through analysis.  
Not through logic.  
But through simple recognition.  
I say:  
**silence is transparency,**  
**through which clarity passes.**

</div>

**2186. And lastly: silence in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

it is not a pause,

not meditation,

and not the absence of stimuli.

Silence is a state of consciousness,

in which inner distortions no longer cover over reality.

It is a space,

where thought loses its tension,

where self-awareness does not require confirmation,

and where clarity arises not as a conclusion,

but as a natural way of being present.

Silence is not emptiness.

Silence is an environment,

in which everything is seen.

And the exact phrase is born:

**silence is the place where understanding does not need words.**

This is silence:

even,

deep,

alive,

not bound to sound,

and always opening a space for clarity.

### OF THE HOLY SPIRIT AS A VISION (when vision ceases to be a glance and becomes the discernment of essence)

**2187. Vision is not what I look at.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Vision is what becomes evident,  
when there are no disturbances within.  
I see:  
vision is recognition, not observation.

</div>

**2188. The Holy Spirit in vision —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability to distinguish fact from interpretation.  
Fact is simple.  
Interpretation is loud.  
It is clear to me:  
vision is direct contact with what is,  
without mental framing.

</div>

**2189. Vision is not analysis.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Analysis breaks things apart.  
Vision joins.  
I note:  
to see is to recognize the structure as a whole,  
not to gather the elements.

</div>

**2190. The Holy Spirit in vision —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ending of automatic meanings,  
which are laid upon experience faster than I have time to notice.  
When automatism ceases —  
clarity appears.  
And I understand:  
vision is the space between stimulus and interpretation.

</div>

**2191. Vision is not understanding in the usual sense.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Understanding is from the mind.  
Vision is from directness.  
I say:  
vision is the immediate recognition of the structure of the moment.

</div>

**2192. The Holy Spirit in vision —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability to notice the true motive,  
standing behind the action:  
fear,  
pain,  
desire,  
avoidance,  
response.  
And I understand:  
vision is the recognition of the source of movement.

</div>

**2193. Vision is not “to see the truth about the world.”**

<div class="lineated verse">

Vision is to see the truth of the moment.  
Truth is always concrete.  
I record:  
vision is accuracy toward what is happening now.

</div>

**2194. The Holy Spirit in vision —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability not to confuse what I want to see,  
with what is.  
Desire always colors.  
Vision — does not.  
And I understand:  
vision is perception, freed from desire.

</div>

**2195. Vision is when the inner noise stops prompting conclusions.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Not “this means that”,  
but “this is how it is.”  
I say:  
vision is recognized simplicity.

</div>

**2196. And last: vision in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

this is not clairvoyance,

not an intuitive guess,

and not the ability to penetrate “into the depths of the hidden.”

Vision is the recognition of the essence of what is happening

without the distortions created by:

fear,

desire,

attachment,

the illusion of control,

past traumas,

expectations,

role,

self-image.

When the distortions are dissolved,

vision arises of itself,

as a natural property of clear consciousness.

And then the exact phrase is born:

**vision is the ability to see what is,**

**and not what I think about it.**

This is vision:

quiet,

simple,

honest,

not dramatic,

and always directed toward reality,

and not toward its conceptual shadow.

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS REMEMBRANCE OF ONESELF (when “to remember oneself” ceases to be a practice and becomes a natural return into Presence)

**2197. Remembrance of oneself is not an action.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Remembrance is recognition.  
I see:  
**remembrance is the recollection that I am already here.**

</div>

**2198. The Holy Spirit in remembrance —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is not a reminder to “be mindful,”  
but the vanishing of the inner mist,  
which keeps one from seeing:  
I never leave myself,  
I only lose sight of it.  
It is clear to me:  
**remembrance is clarification,**  
**and not concentration.**

</div>

**2199. Remembrance is not effort.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Effort is when I pull myself “into myself.”  
Remembrance is when the interfering effort ceases.  
I note:  
**remembrance arises when the tension of holding a role ceases.**

</div>

**2200. The Holy Spirit in remembrance —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is clarity without commentary:  
“I AM.”  
Not as a concept.  
Not as a mantra.  
Not as identity.  
Simply as the fact of presence.  
And I understand:  
**remembrance is the recognition of one’s own presence**  
**without words about it.**

</div>

**2201. Mindfulness is when attention ceases to be scattered.**

<div class="lineated verse">

It is not drawn tight “into itself.”  
It simply stops leaking away.  
I say:  
**mindfulness is the absence of leakage of attention.**

</div>

**2202. The Holy Spirit in remembrance —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the cessation of inner running:  
“what next?,”  
“what is needed?,”  
“what is to be done?,”  
“what is right?.”  
When this running stops,  
recognition arises:  
I am — before the task, before the choice, before the response.  
It becomes clear to me:  
**remembrance is silence,**  
**in which I do not lose myself.**

</div>

**2203. Remembrance is not a fixed state.**

<div class="lineated verse">

It is a return,  
that happens again and again.  
Not as an achievement.  
As a natural rhythm.  
I record:  
**remembrance is a return without accusation for forgetting.**

</div>

**2204. The Holy Spirit in remembrance —**

this is the absence of inner control over the process of “being oneself.”

I do not “hold myself in presence.”

I simply stop interfering with being present.

And I understand:

**remembrance is the natural self-manifestation of consciousness,**

**when the mind ceases to fill all space with itself.**

**2205. Remembrance is when even in action,**

<div class="lineated verse">

even in conversation,  
even in anxiety  
there remains a slight feeling:  
“there is a point here that has not entered into automatism”.  
I say:  
**remembrance is a residual clarity,**  
**that does not dissolve completely into action.**

</div>

**2206. And the last thing: remembrance of oneself in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

this is not a practice of holding attention,

not a spiritual technique,

not “self-work.”

Remembrance is the property of clear consciousness

to return naturally to the point of presence,

when automatism ceases,

habitual reactivity,

inner haste,

and the urge to play a role

into which I had temporarily immersed myself.

Remembrance is not activity.

Remembrance is recognition.

And the exact phrase is born:

**remembrance is a return to the place**

**from which I never departed.**

This is remembrance:

quiet,

simple,

unstrained,

requiring no effort,

and always arising naturally,

as soon as inner preoccupation with oneself disappears.

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS WILL (when will ceases to be effort and becomes the orientation of being)

**2207. Will is not “I can force myself.”**

<div class="lineated verse">

Will is “I have stopped breaking myself.”  
I see:  
**will is agreement with one’s own movement,**  
**and not pressure upon oneself.**

</div>

**2208. The Holy Spirit in the will —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability to feel the direction,  
which is already coming from within,  
and not to silence it with fear, doubt, or role.  
It is clear to me:  
**the will is a recognized “yes.”**

</div>

**2209. Will is not about overcoming.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Overcoming is a struggle with oneself.  
Will is when struggle ceases,  
and clear action remains.  
I note:  
**will is action without inner conflict.**

</div>

**2210. The Holy Spirit in the will —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the absence of division between desire and step.  
When the desire is one thing, the action another —  
the will falls apart.  
When desire and action coincide —  
the will is simple.  
And I understand:  
**will is the joinedness of motive and step.**

</div>

**2211. Will is not “I pulled myself together.”**

<div class="lineated verse">

It is “I no longer need to pull myself together.”  
I say:  
**will is the maturity of the motive.**  
When the motive is mature —  
action is natural.  
When it is immature —  
action requires effort.

</div>

**2212. The Holy Spirit in the will —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability to distinguish:  
where I act out of fear,  
where out of avoidance,  
where out of role,  
where out of habit,  
and where — out of a true impulse.  
It is clear to me:  
**the will is a true impulse,**  
**purified of distortions.**

</div>

**2213. Will is not persistence.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Persistence is the continuation of action in defiance of reality.  
Will is action in accord with reality.  
I state:  
**will is sobriety,**  
**which allows one to step in a way adequate to the moment.**

</div>

**2214. The Holy Spirit in the will —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability to act without dramatization,  
without inner self-boosting,  
without the story about “strength of character.”  
And I understand:  
**the will is action without theater.**

</div>

**2215. Will is when a step is born of clarity,**

<div class="lineated verse">

and not from an emotional surge  
and not from the pressure of circumstances.  
I say:  
**will is direction,**  
**which remains even after**  
**emotion has quieted.**

</div>

**2216. And lastly: the will in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

it is not effort,

not self-compulsion,

not a symbol of “strength of spirit,”

and not an attempt to overcome weakness.

The will is the natural direction of inward movement,

which becomes evident,

when the noise of contradictions ceases,

when the mind no longer demands guarantees,

when desire no longer argues with reality,

and when I stop playing the part of one

who must be strong.

The will is not force.

The will is alignment.

And the exact phrase is born:

**the will is a clear “yes,”**

**which does not require violence against oneself.**

This is the will:

even,

clear,

genuine,

not born of effort,

and always — reflecting the true direction of my movement.

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS LOVE (when love ceases to be feelings and becomes a state of undividedness)

**2217. Love is not warmth toward another.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Love is the absence of dividing tension.  
I see:  
**love is the absence of an inner wall.**

</div>

**2218. The Holy Spirit in love —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability to look at another  
without projecting fear, resentment, the past, or expectation.  
It is clear to me:  
**love is the pure perception of the other,**  
**not distorted by defenses.**

</div>

**2219. Love is not sympathy.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Sympathy is an emotional coloring.  
Love is the absence of inner resistance.  
I note:  
**love is the relaxedness of contact.**

</div>

**2220. The Holy Spirit in love —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ceasing of inward movement  
“to assess — to compare — to defend oneself — to demand.”  
When these movements disappear,  
there remains a steady experience of the presence of the other.  
And I understand:  
**love is not a relationship,**  
**but the space in which relationship is possible.**

</div>

**2221. Love is not attachment.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Attachment is the fear of loss.  
Love is the absence of fear in contact.  
I say:  
**love is contact without tension.**

</div>

**2222. The Holy Spirit in love —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is clarity:  
the other is not an enemy,  
not a tool,  
not an extension of me,  
not a project,  
not a threat.  
The other is simply other.  
It is clear to me:  
**love is the acceptance of the other as other.**

</div>

**2223. Love is not self-sacrifice.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Self-sacrifice is an attempt to earn recognition.  
Love is the absence of bargain.  
I record:  
**love is the absence of conditions**  
**for the existence of another beside you.**

</div>

**2224. The Holy Spirit in love —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the cessation of inward constriction,  
which usually arises in contact:  
tension,  
self-defense,  
control.  
When the constriction disappears —  
the other is perceived as a whole.  
And I understand:  
**love is expansiveness,**  
**in which the other can be held without distortion.**

</div>

**2225. Love is not the state of “I feel good.”**

<div class="lineated verse">

It is the state of “there is no wall between us.”  
I say:  
**love is not an emotion,**  
**but a structure of contact.**

</div>

**2226. And lastly: love in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

it is not a feeling,

not altruism,

not all-forgiveness,

and not a spiritual virtue.

Love is a state of inner undividedness,

when the other is experienced not as a threat and not as an object,

but as part of a shared space of presence.

Love is evenness in contact,

absence of fear,

absence of mutual distortion,

absence of the schemes “I against”,

“I for”,

“I over”,

“I for the sake of”.

Love is precision in the perception of the other.

And a precise phrase is born:

**love is when I do not separate the other**

**by inner tension.**

This is love:

quiet,

simple,

honest,

without pathos,

without religious coloring,

and always — a manifestation of the wholeness of perception.

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS TRUTH (when truth ceases to be an opinion and becomes correspondence with reality)

**2227. Truth is not what I think.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Truth is what is,  
when my thoughts no longer try to “improve” reality.  
I see:  
**truth is the absence of distortion of fact.**

</div>

**2228. The Holy Spirit in truth —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability to see reality as it is,  
not as  
I need it  
to be,  
to be.  
It is clear to me:  
**truth is the straightness of perception without desire.**

</div>

**2229. Truth is not “I am right.”**

<div class="lineated verse">

Truth is “I am aligned with what is happening.”  
I record:  
**truth is the consonance of perception with fact.**

</div>

**2230. The Holy Spirit in truth —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ending of the inner need  
to defend one’s point of view.  
When defense disappears —  
clarity remains.  
And I understand:  
**truth is the absence of inner defense.**

</div>

**2231. Truth is not a set of knowledge.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Knowledge can be false,  
outdated,  
incomplete,  
interpreted.  
Truth is a way of seeing.  
I say:  
**truth is a quality of perception,**  
**and not the content of thoughts.**

</div>

**2232. The Holy Spirit in truth —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the ability to discern:  
what is happening,  
and what I am telling about what is happening.  
I can see:  
**truth is the discernment between fact and narrative.**

</div>

**2233. Truth is not rigidity.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Truth is gentleness,  
because it does not require proof.  
What requires proof is opinion.  
Truth does not need it.  
I state:  
**truth is naturalness,**  
**which does not need confirmation.**

</div>

**2234. The Holy Spirit in truth —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the ability to stop avoiding the unpleasant.  
Lies are always a means of defense.  
Truth is the renunciation of defense.  
And I understand:  
**truth is the courage to look straight on.**

</div>

**2235. Truth is not “how it should be.”**

<div class="lineated verse">

Truth is “how it is.”  
Everything else is an interpretation.  
I say:  
**truth is a correspondence with reality without improvements.**

</div>

**2236. And the last thing: truth in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

it is not a concept,

not a doctrine,

not a statement about the structure of the world,

and not a moral category.

Truth is a state of inner wholeness,

in which a person does not distort reality

for the sake of protection,

gaining advantage,

maintaining an image,

satisfying desire

or avoiding pain.

Truth is direct contact with fact.

Without ideas,

without adornment,

without excuses.

And there is born an exact phrase:

**truth is when I do not interfere with reality being itself.**

This is truth:

even,

quiet,

not grandiose,

not aggressive,

and always — freeing from inner tension.

### On the Holy Spirit as Freedom (when freedom ceases to be a choice and becomes the absence of inner compulsion)

**2237. Freedom is not a multitude of options.**

<div class="lineated verse">
