A multitude of options creates anxiety.  
Freedom is when action becomes obvious.  
I see:  
**freedom is clarity of direction,**  
**and not the number of possibilities.**

</div>

**2238. The Holy Spirit in freedom —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the cessation of inner obligation:  
“must,”  
“ought to,”  
“right,”  
“this is how it is done.”  
When these structures weaken —  
a space of choice arises, uncolored by fear.  
It is clear to me:  
**freedom is the absence of inner pressure.**

</div>

**2239. Freedom is not “I can do everything.”**

<div class="lineated verse">

It is “I do not act against myself.”  
I state:  
**freedom is an action that does not contradict inner truth.**

</div>

**2240. The Holy Spirit in freedom —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the ability to distinguish:  
do I want  
or  
am I avoiding?  
Fear often disguises itself as “will.”  
Habit — as “decision.”  
Automation — as “choice.”  
And I understand:  
**freedom is an act not born of avoidance.**

</div>

**2241. Freedom is not the absence of limits.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Everyone has limits.  
Freedom is the absence of inner constriction before a limit.  
I say:  
**freedom is agreement with reality,**  
**and not a struggle with its form.**

</div>

**2242. The Holy Spirit in freedom —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the ending of inner dependence on the image:  
“what I should be,”  
“what I should look like,”  
“how I should be perceived.”  
When the image loses its power —  
actions become natural.  
I see it this way:  
**freedom is the absence of a role as the source of decisions.**

</div>

**2243. Freedom is not about power.**

<div class="lineated verse">

It is about the absence of inner conflict.  
Where there is conflict, there is no freedom,  
only compensations.  
I state:  
**freedom is the absence of inner division.**

</div>

**2244. The Holy Spirit in freedom —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability to live without self-deception:  
not to cover fear with rationalization,  
not to call weakness a principle,  
not to mask dependence as love.  
And I understand:  
**freedom is honesty with oneself,**  
**far harder than choice.**

</div>

**2245. Freedom is when a step does not require the effort to “break oneself”**

<div class="lineated verse">

and does not require the effort to “restore oneself.”  
It simply happens.  
I say:  
**freedom is nonviolent action.**

</div>

**2246. And lastly: freedom in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

it is not a philosophical category,

not a psychological attitude,

and not a social value.

Freedom is an inner state,

in which action is no longer determined by fear,

unbearability,

avoidance,

an inner duty,

a sense of guilt,

the pressure of expectations

or the desire to prove anything to oneself.

Freedom is when action is born

from a clear “yes”,

and not from an attempt to escape from a “no”.

Freedom is not a possibility.

Freedom is the structure of action.

And the exact phrase is born:

**freedom is when nothing within me compels me to go against myself.**

This is freedom:

quiet,

simple,

even,

not about heroism,

not about strength,

but about honest action,

which happens naturally.

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS COURAGE (when courage ceases to be the overcoming of fear and becomes the ability to remain in reality)

**2247. Courage is not the absence of fear.**

<div class="lineated verse">

The absence of fear comes in shock, immaturity, or denial.  
Courage is the clarity that remains beside fear.  
I see:  
**courage is presence beside one’s fear,**  
**not victory over it.**

</div>

**2248. The Holy Spirit in courage —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability not to let fear dictate the picture of the world.  
Fear always distorts:  
makes the large — huge,  
the difficult — impossible,  
the future — a catastrophe.  
It is clear to me:  
**courage is perception not colored by fear.**

</div>

**2249. Courage is not a leap into heroic risk.**

<div class="lineated verse">

It is a small step taken despite the trembling within.  
I record:  
**courage is a little “yes,”**  
**that sounds softer than fear,**  
**but more exact than it.**

</div>

**2250. The Holy Spirit in courage —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability to look at the fact,  
and not at the fantasy about it.  
Fear always fantasizes.  
Courage — returns to the fact.  
And I understand:  
**courage is sobriety in the face of the unknown.**

</div>

**2251. Courage is not steadfastness.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Steadfastness is sometimes only constriction.  
Courage is mobile.  
I say:  
**courage is flexibility in the situation,**  
**not rigidity before it.**

</div>

**2252. The Holy Spirit in courage —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability not to turn away from pain,  
not to drown it out with amusement,  
not to muffle it with flight,  
not to turn it into aggression.  
I can see:  
**courage is the ability to endure emotion,**  
**without being destroyed by it.**

</div>

**2253. Courage is the ability to call things by their names:**

<div class="lineated verse">

“I am afraid,”  
“I do not know,”  
“I am vulnerable,”  
and not disappear because of it.  
I record:  
**courage is honesty with oneself in the moment of vulnerability.**

</div>

**2254. The Holy Spirit in courage —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability not to hide behind the role:  
of the strong,  
of the one who knows,  
of the one in control,  
of the unbreakable.  
A role is fragile.  
Presence is steady.  
And I understand:  
**courage is the renunciation of role for the sake of the truth of the moment.**

</div>

**2255. Courage is not resistance to fear.**

<div class="lineated verse">

It is the ceasing of resistance to reality.  
I say:  
**courage is consent to be there,**  
**where I am,**  
**without fleeing inward into invented worlds.**

</div>

**2256. And last: courage in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

it is not strength of character,

not a feat,

not self-sacrifice

and not a display of superiority over feelings.

Courage is the ability to remain in reality,

when it would be easy to retreat into defense:

into denial,

into avoidance,

into fantasy,

into aggression,

into self-pity,

into a role,

into flight.

Courage is clarity,

that remains even there,

where it is frightening.

And the exact phrase is born:

**courage is the ability to be in the moment,**

**without allowing fear to dictate my picture of the world.**

This is courage:

calm,

steady,

without pathos,

without dramatization,

and always — about presence,

and not about overcoming.

### OF THE HOLY SPIRIT AS HUMILITY BEFORE UNCERTAINTY (when uncertainty ceases to be a threat and becomes the natural state of the living)

**2257. Uncertainty is not the world’s error.**

<div class="lineated verse">

It is the structure of the world.  
Any attempt to remove it always gives birth to anxiety.  
I see:  
**humility is agreement with the fact that the future is not obliged to be clear.**

</div>

**2258. The Holy Spirit in uncertainty —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ceasing of the inner demand to know ahead of time,  
to guarantee,  
to insure oneself,  
to control.  
It is clear to me:  
**humility is the absence of a claim against life**  
**for its unpredictability.**

</div>

**2259. Uncertainty is not “I don’t care.”**

<div class="lineated verse">

It is “I stop fighting what I do not know.”  
I record:  
**humility is the absence of resistance to the unknown.**

</div>

**2260. The Holy Spirit in uncertainty —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability to stand in the moment,  
without dissolving into fantasies about the future:  
catastrophic or saving.  
And I understand:  
**humility is the renunciation of fantasies as a mechanism of defense.**

</div>

**2261. A person trying to eliminate uncertainty,**

<div class="lineated verse">

is always tense.  
Always in anxiety.  
Always trying to control that  
which yields not to control.  
I say:  
**humility is tension laid down before the unknown.**

</div>

**2262. The Holy Spirit in humility —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability to live with a question,  
and not demand an answer.  
It is the ability to see  
that the absence of an answer is also part of reality.  
It is clear to me:  
**humility is an inner spaciousness,**  
**in which a question can exist without pressure.**

</div>

**2263. Uncertainty is frightening,**

<div class="lineated verse">

when there is little steadiness within.  
When steadiness is there —  
uncertainty ceases to be an enemy.  
I note:  
**humility is steadiness without knowledge of the future.**

</div>

**2264. The Holy Spirit in uncertainty —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the ability to discern:  
fear wants clarity,  
but reality is not obliged to give it.  
And I understand:  
**humility is the ceasing of the demand for predictability.**

</div>

**2265. Uncertainty is not emptiness.**

<div class="lineated verse">

It is a space of possibilities,  
which will come to light only when,  
I stop clinging to a single scenario.  
I say:  
**humility is openness to that**  
**which I could not have foreseen.**

</div>

**2266. And the last: humility before uncertainty**

in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit—

this is not about patience,

not about submission,

and not about spiritual asceticism.

It is about maturity—

about the ability to stand in the living world,

which gives no guarantees,

cannot be fully controlled,

does not promise safety,

and does not reveal in advance

how the next step will unfold.

Humility is clarity:

I can live,

even when I do not know.

And a precise phrase is born:

**humility before uncertainty—**

**this is the ability to rely not on knowledge of the future,**

**but on the steadiness of presence.**

This is humility:

simple,

quiet,

not dramatic,

and always—honest.

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS DISCERNMENT (when discernment ceases to be judgment and becomes the ability to see direction precisely)

**2267. Discernment is not judgment.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Judgment is always colored by emotion,  
habit,  
fear,  
desire.  
Discernment is impartial.  
I see:  
**discernment is the seeing of structure,**  
**and not the pronouncing of a sentence.**

</div>

**2268. The Holy Spirit in discernment —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability to see what is really happening,  
and not what I would like,  
or what I am afraid to admit.  
It is clear to me:  
**discernment is sober contact with reality.**

</div>

**2269. Discernment is not “to determine who is right.”**

<div class="lineated verse">

It is “to see what is truly driving the situation.”  
I note:  
**discernment is understanding the source of the movement.**

</div>

**2270. The Holy Spirit in discernment —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability to distinguish:  
where my impulse comes from fear,  
and where — from clarity;  
where — from avoidance,  
and where — from maturity;  
where — from habit,  
and where — from a real choice.  
And I understand:  
**discernment is the recognition of motive,**  
**not the judging of behavior.**

</div>

**2271. Discernment does not require emotions.**

<div class="lineated verse">

It does not require excitement.  
It does not require haste.  
It arises in evenness.  
I say:  
**discernment is calm precision.**

</div>

**2272. The Holy Spirit in discernment —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability to see the boundary between fact and interpretation.  
Fact is simple.  
Interpretation is verbose.  
It is visible to me:  
**discernment is the ability to stop interpretation,**  
**so as to see the fact.**

</div>

**2273. Discernment is not accusation.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Accusation is always moved by pain.  
Discernment is moved by clarity.  
I note:  
**discernment is honesty without aggression.**

</div>

**2274. The Holy Spirit in discernment —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability to see:  
which action leads to wholeness,  
and which — toward scattering and destruction.  
And I understand:  
**discernment is navigation,**  
**and not morality.**

</div>

**2275. Discernment is when I do not confuse:**

<div class="lineated verse">

what I want,  
and what is truly needed;  
what I think,  
and what is;  
what I fear,  
and what is likely;  
what seems to be,  
and what is happening.  
I say:  
**discernment is sobriety,**  
**which does not depend on mood.**

</div>

**2276. And last: discernment in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

it is not the ability to “see the truth about others,”

not spiritual intuition,

and not a moral category.

Discernment is the ability to accurately recognize the direction

in which a situation or an inner impulse is moving,

without distorting filters:

fear,

desire,

resentment,

self-deception,

expectation,

role,

social norm

or an inner script.

Discernment is clarity about what is,

and where it is leading.

And the exact phrase is born:

**discernment is the ability to see direction**

**without resorting to judgment.**

This is discernment:

steady,

precise,

not emotional,

not colored by the role of “I know better,”

and always — oriented toward reality,

not toward projections about it.

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS PRESENCE (when presence ceases to be attentiveness and becomes the natural form of being without inner noise)

**2277. Presence is not a focus of attention.**

<div class="lineated verse">

A focus can be held by effort.  
Presence requires no effort.  
I see:  
**presence is the natural state of the mind,**  
**which has ceased to be occupied with itself.**

</div>

**2278. The Holy Spirit in Presence —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is silence within action,  
when I act,  
but do not lose myself in action;  
I speak,  
but do not dissolve into words;  
I feel,  
but do not drown in emotions.  
It is clear to me:  
**presence is when I do not vanish in experience.**

</div>

**2279. Presence is not “watching the breath”**

<div class="lineated verse">

and not “being here and now.”  
It is the absence of automatic fleeing from the moment.  
I note:  
**presence is the cessation of flight.**

</div>

**2280. The Holy Spirit in presence —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability to notice what is happening directly,  
without habitual interpretations,  
without instant scenarios,  
without inner commentary.  
And I understand:  
**presence is pure perception.**

</div>

**2281. Presence is when the mind does not run ahead,**

<div class="lineated verse">

foretelling,  
and does not go back,  
chewing over.  
I say:  
**presence is the stillness of the mind in time.**

</div>

**2282. The Holy Spirit in Presence —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the feeling of inner space,  
in which action arises,  
but there is no inner burning of hurry or control.  
It is seen to me:  
**presence is the spaciousness within the step.**

</div>

**2283. Presence is not a state of excitement.**

<div class="lineated verse">

And not a state of relaxation.  
It is a quiet clarity,  
which does not depend on the body’s emotions.  
I state:  
**presence is a clarity independent of mood.**

</div>

**2284. The Holy Spirit in presence —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability to be in the moment without  
the moment defining you.  
And I understand:  
**presence is freedom from automatic identification**  
**with what is happening.**

</div>

**2285. Presence is when there is “I am,”**

<div class="lineated verse">

but there is no “I must,”  
“I am afraid,”  
“I am not in time,”  
“I am in control.”  
I say:  
**presence is the simple feeling of existing**  
**without a story around it.**

</div>

**2286. And lastly: presence in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

this is not attentiveness as a technique,

not concentration,

not a meditative experience

and not a “special state”.

Presence is the natural form of consciousness,

which arises when the inner noise ceases:

constant thoughts,

inner criticism,

hurry,

fear,

role,

self-explanation,

the search for control,

the attempt to please,

the attempt to escape vulnerability.

Presence is not something that needs to be created.

Presence is what remains,

when the unnecessary disappears.

And a precise phrase is born:

**presence is me without explanations.**

This is presence:

even,

quiet,

simple,

without decorations,

without pose,

and always — steady,

because it depends on nothing except the cessation of inner noise.

### OF THE HOLY SPIRIT AS WHOLENESS (when wholeness ceases to be collectedness and becomes inner undividedness)

**2287. Wholeness is not “I am put together.”**

<div class="lineated verse">

Composure is the act of will to hold the form.  
Wholeness is the absence of inner disintegration.  
I see:  
**wholeness is when there are no opposite vectors within,**  
**that pull me in different directions.**

</div>

**2288. The Holy Spirit in wholeness —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability to see your desires, fears, thoughts, and reactions  
as parts of one field,  
and not as enemies of one another.  
It is clear to me:  
**wholeness is the agreement of different inner movements to exist**  
**without war.**

</div>

**2289. Wholeness is not about perfection.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Not about “rightness.”  
Not about completeness.  
Wholeness is when I do not hide parts of myself  
for the sake of an image.  
I note:  
**wholeness is the absence of inner lies toward oneself.**

</div>

**2290. The Holy Spirit in wholeness —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability to endure one’s feelings  
without cutting them off,  
without suppressing them,  
without turning them into a role,  
and without translating them into withdrawal.  
And I understand:  
**wholeness is the honesty of experience.**

</div>

**2291. Integrity is when my actions do not contradict my values.**

<div class="lineated verse">

When desires and steps coincide.  
When “I want” and “I do” speak with the same voice.  
I say:  
**integrity is inner consistency.**

</div>

**2292. The Holy Spirit in wholeness —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability not to split between “how it should be” and “how it is.”  
When “how it should be” ceases to govern life,  
there comes freedom to be real,  
without tearing oneself apart.  
I see:  
**wholeness is when “how it is”**  
**does not require disguising.**

</div>

**2293. Integrity is not emotional stability.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Integrity is the ability to remain oneself  
even in emotional instability.  
I note:  
**integrity is the steadiness of self-perception**  
**in any weather within.**

</div>

**2294. The Holy Spirit in integrity —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the absence of an inner theater:  
there is no need to act out spirituality,  
no need to display strength,  
no need to hide weakness.  
And I understand:  
**integrity is life without an inner stage.**

</div>

**2295. Wholeness is when I do not tear myself apart**

<div class="lineated verse">

into an “inner enemy” and an “inner hero,”  
do not split perception into pieces,  
do not create personae within myself.  
I say:  
**wholeness is when I cease to be a multitude of voices**  
**that argue with one another.**

</div>

**2296. And lastly: wholeness in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

is not psychological composure,

not moral integrity,

and not spiritual completeness.

Wholeness is a state of inner coherence,

in which the different parts of me —

mind, body, emotions, impulses, memory, fears, desires —

do not conflict with one another,

because none of them is trying to rule the whole field.

Wholeness is not victory over oneself.

Wholeness is the renunciation of war within.

And the exact phrase is born:

**wholeness is when there is no one left within to fight.**

This is wholeness:

even,

quiet,

without pose,

without pathos,

and always — opening space for free inner movement.

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS REST (when rest ceases to be relaxation and becomes a steadfast inner ground)

**2297. Peace is not the absence of movement.**

<div class="lineated verse">

And not the absence of tension.  
Peace is the absence of resistance to what is.  
I see:  
**peace is an inward assent to reality.**

</div>

**2298. The Holy Spirit in repose —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability to remain steady within oneself  
even then,  
when emotions flare up,  
when events press in,  
when the situation is difficult.  
It is clear to me:  
**peace is not an emotion,**  
**but a depth beneath emotions.**  
**2299.**  
Peace is not relaxation.  
Relaxation is bodily.  
Peace is existential.  
I note:  
**peace is a state of being,**  
**and not a state of muscles.**

</div>

**2300. The Holy Spirit in repose —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the ceasing of the inner struggle:  
the struggle “for” a state,  
the struggle “against” a state,  
the struggle for control,  
the struggle for certainty.  
And I understand:  
**repose is the place where the struggle ceases by itself.**

</div>

**2301. Peace is the ability to endure uncertainty**

<div class="lineated verse">

without inward contraction,  
without turning it into anxiety.  
I say:  
**peace is gentleness before the unknown.**

</div>

**2302. The Holy Spirit in repose —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the silence that remains in the center,  
even when waves move across the surface.  
It is shown to me:  
**repose is a depth**  
**that does not depend on the weather at the surface.**

</div>

**2303. Peace is when “I Am”**

<div class="lineated verse">

becomes more tangible than  
“what is happening.”  
I note:  
**peace is the steadiness of self-awareness**  
**in the face of events.**

</div>

**2304. The Holy Spirit in peace —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the absence of an inner demand  
that the world be otherwise.  
Not because everything is liked.  
But because resistance changes nothing.  
It only takes away clarity.  
And I understand:  
**peace is the renunciation of inner protest against the world.**

</div>

**2305. Peace is not indifference.**

<div class="lineated verse">

On the contrary:  
indifference is an attempt to protect oneself.  
Peace is openness to life  
without fear of being vulnerable.  
I say:  
**peace is openness without protection.**

</div>

**2306. And last: peace in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

it is not the absence of emotions,

not the absence of problems,

and not armor that protects from pain.

Peace is a steady inner background,

on which it is possible to see clearly,

move rightly,

choose consciously,

and not be destroyed by what is happening.

Peace is a depth

into which the outer does not penetrate,

because this depth is

not a reaction to the outer world,

but the basis of inner steadfastness.

And the exact phrase is born:

**peace is not when it is calm around you,**

**but when it is calm within the one who lives in the world.**

This is peace:

even,

deep,

simple,

without pose,

and always — bringing a person back to himself.

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS SIMPLICITY (when simplicity ceases to be primitiveness and becomes the absence of artificial complication)

**2307. Simplicity is not “less.”**

<div class="lineated verse">

Simplicity is “enough.”  
I see:  
**simplicity is the absence of the unnecessary.**

</div>

**2308. The Holy Spirit in simplicity —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability to see a situation without the need  
to add additional meanings to it,  
interpretations,  
assumptions,  
dramatization.  
It is clear to me:  
**simplicity is directness of perception.**

</div>

**2309. Simplicity is not superficiality.**

<div class="lineated verse">

On the contrary:  
superficiality arises when  
a person drowns in complexities and loses the essence.  
I record:  
**simplicity is the ability to see what is foremost.**

</div>

**2310. The Holy Spirit in simplicity —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the ending of the inner need  
to look smart, deep, spiritual, or special.  
And I understand:  
**simplicity is the absence of display.**

</div>

**2311. Simplicity is when the words grow shorter,**

<div class="lineated verse">

because the meaning grows clearer.  
I say:  
**simplicity is clarity that requires no adornment.**

</div>

**2312. The Holy Spirit in simplicity —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the ability to live without unnecessary inner constructions:  
images of oneself,  
expectations from the world,  
constant scenarios.  
It is clear to me:  
**simplicity is life without excessive stories.**

</div>

**2313. Simplicity is when action becomes natural,**

<div class="lineated verse">

because it is no longer necessary to align it  
with a multitude of inner roles.  
I note:  
**simplicity is clear action without complicated justifications.**

</div>

**2314. The Holy Spirit in simplicity —**

<div class="lineated verse">

this is the ability to see things as they are,  
without making them more significant or more frightening.  
And I understand:  
**simplicity is the absence of exaggeration.**

</div>

**2315. Simplicity is when there remains a short distance between thought, word, and deed**

<div class="lineated verse">

There are no long roundabout ways.  
There are no long detours.  
I say:  
**simplicity is the straightness of inner movement.**

</div>

**2316. And lastly: simplicity in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is not intellectual modesty,  
not a rejection of the world’s complexity,  
and not a simplification of reality.  
Simplicity is a state of clarity,  
in which a person ceases to complicate life  
with his fears,  
interpretations,  
expectations,  
roles  
and inner stories.  
Simplicity is transparency of perception.  
And a precise phrase is born:  
**simplicity is when between me and reality**  
**there are no more unnecessary layers.**  
This is simplicity:  
quiet,  
pure,  
without display,  
without intellectual adornment,  
and always — returning to the essence.

</div>

**ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT AS LIFE (when life ceases to be a process of existence and becomes an immediate experience of being)**

**2317. Life is not a list of events.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Nor is it a sequence of achievements.  
Life is the very fact of experiencing the moment.  
I see:  
**life is the feeling of “I am.”**

</div>

**2318. The Holy Spirit in life —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability to feel the presence of life  
not only in joy,  
but also in difficulty,  
in weariness,  
in uncertainty.  
It is clear to me:  
**life is not a mood,**  
**but the very fact of existence.**

</div>

**2319. Life is not a project.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Not a path to a goal.  
Not a system of tasks.  
I record:  
**life is the continual manifestation of being.**

</div>

**2320. The Holy Spirit in life —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the ability to feel  
that life happens not only “to me,”  
but through me as well.  
And I understand:  
**life is a flow,**  
**in which I am at once participant and manifestation.**

</div>

**2321. Life is not only activity.**

<div class="lineated verse">

And not only movement.  
Sometimes life reveals itself in silence,  
in stillness,  
in simple presence.  
I say:  
**life is not action,**  
**but the ability to be.**

</div>

**2322. The Holy Spirit in life —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is the ability to feel the fullness of the moment,  
even when the moment is filled with nothing special.  
I see:  
**life is the depth of the ordinary.**

</div>

**2323. Life is when existence ceases to be perceived**

<div class="lineated verse">

as a problem,  
that needs to be solved.  
I record:  
**life is consent to exist.**

</div>

**2324. The Holy Spirit in life —**

<div class="lineated verse">

is the feeling of a living presence in everything:  
in breathing,  
in movement,  
in conversation,  
in silence,  
in observation.  
And I understand:  
**life is the continuous manifestation of being**  
**in many forms.**

</div>

**2325. Life is not control over what is happening.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Life is participation in what is happening.  
I say:  
**life is the movement of being through form.**

</div>

**2326. And last: life in the phenomenology of the Holy Spirit —**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is not biological existence,  
not social activity,  
and not the fulfillment of a life plan.  
Life is the experience of the very fact of being,  
which becomes evident,  
when inner resistance ceases  
to existence itself.  
Life is when “I am”  
becomes more palpable  
than all thoughts about  
what life ought to be.  
And a precise phrase is born:  
**life is when existence is experienced directly,**  
**and not through ideas about it.**  
This is life:  
alive,  
simple,  
deep,  
not requiring justification,  
and always — happening here.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT AS THE FIELD OF CONSCIOUSNESS (when all manifestations become different forms of one clarity)

**2327. If one observes closely,**

<div class="lineated verse">

one can see that love, peace, discernment, and presence  
arise from one and the same inner space.  
This space is not created.  
It is discovered.  
I see:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of clarity,**  
**in which all forms of understanding arise.**

</div>

**2328. The Holy Spirit does not act as a separate force.**

<div class="lineated verse">

He does not interfere.  
Does not control.  
Does not direct.  
He manifests as the ability to see clearly.  
It is clear to me:  
**The Holy Spirit is the transparency of consciousness.**

</div>

**2329. When this transparency is,**

<div class="lineated verse">

discernment appears.  
When it is there — love arises.  
When it is there — peace comes.  
But transparency itself  
is neither love,  
nor peace.  
I state:  
**The Holy Spirit is the foundation of all these states.**

</div>

**2330. If the mind is clouded by fear,**

<div class="lineated verse">

desire,  
haste,  
self-protection,  
this transparency is temporarily closed.  
But it does not disappear.  
And I understand:  
**The Holy Spirit is not a state,**  
**but the depth of consciousness,**  
**which is always present.**

</div>

**2331. The Holy Spirit does not come from outside.**

<div class="lineated verse">

It is not created by effort.  
It does not arise from spiritual practices.  
It becomes noticeable  
then,  
when the hindrances disappear.  
I say:  
**The Holy Spirit is clarity,**  
**which manifests when the noise ceases.**

</div>

**2332. When this clarity is there,**

<div class="lineated verse">

thinking becomes more exact,  
feelings — more honest,  
actions — simpler.  
Not because a new power appears.  
But because distortions disappear.  
I see:  
**The Holy Spirit is the absence of distortions of perception.**

</div>

**2333. The Holy Spirit cannot be held.**

<div class="lineated verse">

It cannot be fixed in place.  
It cannot be turned into a permanent state.  
It manifests every time,  
when consciousness returns to transparency.  
I note:  
**The Holy Spirit is the natural state of clear consciousness.**

</div>

**2334. When a person tries to “possess” this state,**

<div class="lineated verse">

it disappears.  
Because the desire to possess creates noise again.  
And I understand:  
**The Holy Spirit is manifested not through effort,**  
**but through the disappearance of effort.**

</div>

**2335. All the previous manifestations —**

<div class="lineated verse">

peace,  
love,  
truth,  
discernment,  
presence,  
will,  
courage —  
are not separate qualities.  
They are different aspects  
of one and the same transparency.  
I say:  
**The Holy Spirit is clarity,**  
**which takes on many forms.**

</div>

**2336. And last:**

if viewed without religious images and symbols,

the Holy Spirit can be seen as the very space of consciousness,

in which clarity, love, discernment, and peace become possible.

This space is not created.

It does not come into being.

It does not disappear.

It becomes noticeable

then,

when a person ceases to fill it with noise.

And a simple phrase is born:

**The Holy Spirit is the transparency of consciousness,**

**in which reality is seen without distortion.**

This is the Holy Spirit:

not a separate force,

not a mystical essence,

but the depth of clarity,

which is always present

as the foundation of all true understanding.

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the transparency of consciousness is almost not noticed)

**2337. Man is used to noticing only what moves.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Thoughts move.  
Emotions move.  
Events change.  
Images appear and disappear.  
But that in which all this takes place,  
does not move.  
I see:  
**attention naturally clings to movement,**  
**and not to space.**

</div>

**2338. Consciousness is constantly filled with content:**

<div class="lineated verse">

thoughts,  
judgments,  
memories,  
plans,  
inner dialogues.  
When the content is continuous,  
the space between them is almost not noticed.  
It is clear to me:  
**noise does not let one notice silence.**

</div>

**2339. The mind works on the principle of solving problems.**

<div class="lineated verse">

It is always looking for a problem,  
that needs to be solved.  
But the transparency of consciousness —  
is neither a problem nor a task.  
I note:  
**the mind does not seek what does not require a solution.**

</div>

**2340. There is another reason.**

<div class="lineated verse">

The human psyche is accustomed to leaning on identification:  
“I am my thoughts,”  
“I am my emotions,”  
“I am my story.”  
When transparency arises,  
this identification weakens.  
And I understand:  
**the mind fears the space**  
**in which its role diminishes.**

</div>

**2341. Besides, culture almost does not speak directly about the transparency of consciousness.**

<div class="lineated verse">

It speaks about behavior,  
about values,  
about achievements,  
about morality.  
But it almost does not speak about the very field of perception.  
I say:  
**we learn to govern life,**  
**but rarely learn to see the space**  
**in which life happens.**

</div>

**2342. There is also a simpler factor:**

<div class="lineated verse">

human attention grows weary.  
When attention is overloaded,  
it begins automatically to follow the brightest signals:  
emotions, anxiety, news, tasks.  
I see:  
**weary attention almost always chooses noise.**

</div>

**2343. But as soon as the noise lets up —**

<div class="lineated verse">

for a moment,  
for a few seconds —  
a person unexpectedly discovers  
a very simple state:  
nothing special is happening,  
but everything is clear.  
I note:  
**transparency reveals itself in the pauses.**

</div>

**2344. Sometimes this happens in nature,**

<div class="lineated verse">

sometimes in silence,  
sometimes after a strong experience,  
when the thoughts stop for a moment.  
And a person notices a strange thing:  
reality becomes evident without effort.  
And I understand:  
**transparency is always near,**  
**it is simply not in the center of attention.**

</div>

**2345. It is important to see:**

<div class="lineated verse">

this transparency cannot be “created”.  
Any attempt to create it  
produces thought again,  
and thought is already content.  
I say:  
**transparency is not created —**  
**it is discovered when the superfluous disappears.**

</div>

**2346. And one last thing:**

The Holy Spirit, in the phenomenological sense,

can be seen as that very transparency of consciousness —

the space,

in which thoughts, emotions, and perceptions arise and disappear.

He does not come and go.

He does not depend on faith.

He does not belong to religion.

He becomes noticeable

at the moment

when attention ceases to be fully captured by content.

And then a simple observation becomes clear:

**The Holy Spirit is the transparency of consciousness,**

**which has always been here,**

**but has rarely become the object of attention.**

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT (what creates an inner noise that closes the transparency of consciousness)

**2347. The first source of noise —**

<div class="lineated verse">

**the constant commenting on reality.**  
The mind almost continuously explains what is happening:  
it evaluates,  
compares,  
draws conclusions,  
builds forecasts.  
Reality happens,  
and beside it there appears an inner story about it.  
I see:  
**the commentary is often louder than the event itself.**

</div>

**2348. The second source is**

<div class="lineated verse">

**the continuous modeling of the future.**  
The mind is constantly building scenarios:  
what will be,  
what may happen,  
what will go wrong.  
This creates a constant tension of expectation.  
It is clear to me:  
**anxiety about the future creates a steady background of inner noise.**

</div>

**2349. The third source is —**

<div class="lineated verse">

**chewing over the past.**  
The events have already happened,  
but the mind keeps returning to them:  
it analyzes,  
rewrites,  
tries to find another outcome.  
I note:  
**the past remains active within the mind,**  
**even when it has already come to an end.**

</div>

**2350. The fourth source is—**

<div class="lineated verse">

**the inner image of oneself.**  
A person constantly sustains the story about  
who he is:  
what he ought to be,  
what he ought to look like,  
how he ought to be perceived.  
This story requires constant confirmation.  
And I understand:  
**the image of oneself requires constant protection.**

</div>

**2351. The fifth source is—**

<div class="lineated verse">

**the fear of losing control.**  
The mind seeks to control the course of events,  
so as to lessen uncertainty.  
But the world does not yield to complete control.  
I say:  
**the stronger the attempt to control life,**  
**the louder the inner noise becomes.**

</div>

**2352. The sixth source is—**

<div class="lineated verse">

**comparison.**  
Comparing oneself with others,  
with one’s former self,  
with expectations.  
Comparison creates a constant tension of judgment.  
I can see:  
**comparison almost always destroys clarity.**

</div>

**2353. The seventh source is—**

<div class="lineated verse">

**reactivity.**  
Every stimulus causes an instant reaction:  
approval,  
rejection,  
defense,  
the desire to intervene.  
Reaction happens faster than awareness.  
I note:  
**reactivity fills the space between event and understanding.**

</div>

**2354. The eighth source is —**

<div class="lineated verse">

**inner dialogues.**  
A person is constantly carrying on conversations within himself:  
explains,  
argues,  
persuades,  
defends himself.  
These dialogues can continue almost without ceasing.  
And I understand:  
**inner dialogue creates the illusion of the mind’s constant busyness.**

</div>

**2355. All these mechanisms together create a dense stream of content.**

<div class="lineated verse">

This stream does not let attention stop.  
I say:  
**noise is not one phenomenon,**  
**but a complex of continuous movements of the mind.**

</div>

**2356. And last:**

it is important to see that inner noise is not the enemy.

It is simply the natural activity of the psyche,

which developed for solving tasks,

for protection and adaptation.

But when this noise becomes constant,

it closes off the transparency of consciousness,

in which it is possible to see reality directly.

And a simple phrase is born:

**The Holy Spirit does not disappear because of the noise —**

**the noise only distracts attention from transparency.**

Transparency remains.

It simply finds itself in the background.

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (how attention once again discovers the transparency of consciousness)

**2357. The first thing that happens is —**

<div class="lineated verse">

attention begins to notice not only thoughts,  
but also the very fact that thoughts are happening.  
This is a small shift.  
The thought no longer fully absorbs attention.  
It becomes a phenomenon that can be observed.  
I see:  
**the thought becomes an event,**  
**and not the center of consciousness.**

</div>

**2358. When this happens,**

<div class="lineated verse">

a space appears between thought and attention.  
This space is not created —  
it simply becomes noticeable.  
It is clear to me:  
**attention ceases to be captured by content.**

</div>

**2359. Then there arises the ability to notice pauses:**

<div class="lineated verse">

brief intervals,  
when the thought has ended,  
and the next one has not yet appeared.  
These pauses are very brief.  
But they are real.  
I note:  
**transparency often becomes noticeable precisely in the pauses.**

</div>

**2360. At some point attention begins to see,**

<div class="lineated verse">

that thoughts, emotions, and sensations  
arise and disappear on their own.  
They do not need to be stopped.  
And I understand:  
**the content of consciousness moves of itself.**

</div>

**2361. When this becomes evident,**

<div class="lineated verse">

attention gradually stops fighting with thoughts.  
Thoughts come.  
Thoughts go.  
I say:  
**the struggle disappears,**  
**when it becomes clear,**  
**that thoughts do not require control.**

</div>

**2362. At this moment, attention begins to notice not only thoughts,**

<div class="lineated verse">

but also the space in which they arise.  
This space has no form.  
It has no boundaries.  
But it is clearly felt.  
I can see:  
**transparency is the background of all experience.**

</div>

**2363. Sometimes a person first notices this**

<div class="lineated verse">

in very ordinary moments:  
in silence,  
in watching nature,  
in simple breathing.  
Nothing special is happening.  
I note:  
**transparency is found in simplicity.**

</div>

**2364. When attention grows accustomed to this recognition,**

<div class="lineated verse">

it begins to move freely:  
between content and space.  
Thoughts continue,  
but they no longer fill the whole field.  
And I understand:  
**transparency and thoughts can coexist.**

</div>

**2365. This is not a new state of consciousness.**

<div class="lineated verse">

It is simply a more exact vision of  
how consciousness is arranged.  
I say:  
**transparency was here always.**

</div>

**2366. And the last:**

The Holy Spirit, in the phenomenological sense,

can be seen as this very transparency —

the space of consciousness,

in which thoughts, feelings, and perceptions arise.

When attention notices this space,

it ceases to be fully captured by content.

And then a simple observation becomes clear:

**The Holy Spirit is not something that comes.**

**It is the transparency that becomes visible,**

**when attention ceases to be absorbed by noise.**

This transparency has always been here.

It does not need to be created.

It only waits,

for attention to notice it again.

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT (why a person takes the contents of consciousness to be himself)

**2367. From early childhood, a person learns to recognize himself**

<div class="lineated verse">

through thoughts, emotions, and reactions.  
The child is told:  
you are upset,  
you are angry,  
you want,  
you think.  
Thus, gradually, the sense arises:  
everything that happens inside is “I” itself.  
I see:  
**identification is formed through the content of consciousness.**

</div>

**2368. When a thought appears,**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is experienced as something very near.  
A thought is not perceived as an object.  
It is perceived as an extension of the person himself.  
It is clear to me:  
**thoughts are felt as part of the personality.**

</div>

**2369. Emotions intensify this sensation.**

<div class="lineated verse">

When a strong feeling arises —  
fear, anger, joy, love —  
it completely captures attention.  
In that moment, it is hard to see  
that an emotion is only a passing movement.  
I note:  
**strong emotions strengthen identification.**

</div>

**2370. Moreover, memory creates a continuous history:**

<div class="lineated verse">

memories,  
experience,  
events.  
This history is perceived as “my life.”  
And I understand:  
**memory creates the sense of a continuous “I.”**

</div>

**2371. In time, this story becomes the foundation of the personality.**

<div class="lineated verse">

A person begins to perceive himself  
as the sum of his thoughts, experiences, and memories.  
I say:  
**personality is a structure of memories and interpretations.**

</div>

**2372. At the same time, the space of consciousness,**

<div class="lineated verse">

in which all this takes place,  
remains unnoticed.  
Not because it is hidden.  
But because attention is always directed to the content.  
It is clear to me:  
**attention is almost always directed to objects,**  
**and not to space.**

</div>

**2373. It is like the screen of a movie theater.**

<div class="lineated verse">

The viewer sees the characters and events on the screen,  
but rarely notices the screen itself.  
I note:  
**the content of consciousness is like a film,**  
**and transparency is like the screen.**

</div>

**2374. When a person first notices this space,**

<div class="lineated verse">

he often feels wonder.  
For it turns out,  
that thoughts take place in consciousness,  
but in themselves they are not consciousness itself.  
And I understand:  
**consciousness is wider than its contents.**

</div>

**2375. This discovery does not destroy personality.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Thoughts continue.  
Emotions continue.  
Memory remains.  
But clarity appears:  
they take place within a wider space.  
I say:  
**personality is part of the field of consciousness,**  
**and not its limit.**

</div>

**2376. And lastly:**

The Holy Spirit, in the phenomenological sense,

can be seen precisely as this space of consciousness,

which remains unchanged

amid the changing of thoughts, emotions, and experiences.

When attention begins to distinguish

content and space,

a new understanding arises:

thoughts may change,

emotions may come and go,

stories may be rewritten,

but the space of consciousness itself remains open.

And then a simple phrase is born:

**The Holy Spirit is not a thought about God,**

**but the space of consciousness,**

**in which all human life arises.**

### OF THE HOLY SPIRIT (when the content of consciousness and space are perceived at the same time)

**2377. The first change is—**

<div class="lineated verse">

thoughts cease to seem like absolute reality.  
They continue to arise,  
but they become visible as processes.  
I see:  
**thought becomes a phenomenon,**  
**and not a definition of reality.**

</div>

**2378. Emotions also cease to fully seize consciousness.**

<div class="lineated verse">

They may be strong,  
but there appears a sense of space around them.  
It is clear to me:  
**the emotion is experienced,**  
**but no longer becomes the whole picture.**

</div>

**2379. This creates a special form of steadiness.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Not steadiness through suppression of feelings,  
but steadiness through breadth of perception.  
I record:  
**the space of consciousness makes experiences less absorbing.**

</div>

**2380. The ability appears to observe inner processes**

<div class="lineated verse">

without the need to react immediately.  
And I understand:  
**between impulse and action, a pause arises.**

</div>

**2381. This pause becomes very important.**

<div class="lineated verse">

It is precisely in it that the possibility appears to choose action  
instead of an automatic reaction.  
I say:  
**the space of consciousness opens freedom of response.**

</div>

**2382. Attention begins to move freely:**

<div class="lineated verse">

sometimes it is directed at a thought,  
sometimes at a sensation,  
sometimes at the very space of perception.  
I can see:  
**perception becomes more flexible.**

</div>

**2383. It becomes noticeable,**

<div class="lineated verse">

that most thoughts are  
simply automatic processes of the mind.  
They arise without intention.  
I note:  
**thoughts happen,**  
**but do not require full trust.**

</div>

**2384. This does not make a person cold or distant.**

<div class="lineated verse">

On the contrary,  
perception becomes more alive and more exact.  
And I understand:  
**the space of consciousness makes experiences deeper,**  
**not weaker.**

</div>

**2385. Gradually it becomes clear,**

<div class="lineated verse">

that inner peace and clarity  
do not depend entirely on circumstances.  
Events change,  
but the space of consciousness remains.  
I say:  
**steadfastness comes not from controlling life,**  
**but from resting on the space of perception.**

</div>

**2386. And one last thing:**

when a person begins at once to become aware

both of the content of consciousness and of the space

in which that content arises,

a new quality of presence arises.

Thoughts continue.

Emotions continue.

Life continues.

But clarity appears:

all this takes place

**within a wider field of consciousness.**

And then a simple formulation becomes clear:

**The Holy Spirit is the space of consciousness,**

**which remains open**

**even when life moves through many experiences.**

This space does not belong to any single thought,

does not belong to any single state.

It simply is.

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (what happens to the sense of “I” when the space of consciousness becomes visible)

**2387. Usually a person senses himself as the center of his thoughts.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Every thought seems to belong to “me”.  
But when thoughts begin to be perceived as processes,  
a new question arises:  
who observes these thoughts?  
I see:  
**the sense of “I” begins to shift.**

</div>

**2388. Before, the “I” was tied to content:**

<div class="lineated verse">

my ideas,  
my emotions,  
my stories.  
Now it becomes clear,  
that all this can change.  
It is clear to me:  
**that which observes changes**  
**is itself not change.**

</div>

**2389. There appears a sense of a broader “I,”**

<div class="lineated verse">

which is not limited to a separate thought or emotion.  
This is not a new idea about the self.  
This is a direct experience.  
I note:  
**the sense of “I” begins to feel like space,**  
**and not like content.**

</div>

**2390. This space has no clear form.**

<div class="lineated verse">

It does not resemble the image of a person.  
And I understand:  
**“I” becomes closer to the feeling of presence,**  
**than to a story about itself.**

</div>

**2391. At this, the ordinary personality continues to function.**

<div class="lineated verse">

A person remembers his name,  
his duties,  
his relationships.  
I say:  
**the personality remains an instrument of interaction with the world.**

</div>

**2392. But clarity appears:**

<div class="lineated verse">

personality is not the whole depth of what I am.  
It is clear to me:  
**personality is the structure of experience,**  
**and not the limit of consciousness.**

</div>

**2393. This often brings a feeling of lightness.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Because many inner conflicts  
are bound precisely up with the defense of the image of self.  
When the image ceases to be the center,  
the tension lessens.  
I note:  
**the weakening of identification reduces inner pressure.**

</div>

**2394. A person begins to take thoughts about himself more lightly.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Criticism, praise, comparisons  
are no longer perceived as a threat to existence.  
And I understand:  
**one’s sense of self becomes more stable.**

</div>

**2395. This does not mean**

<div class="lineated verse">

that emotions or reactions disappear.  
But they no longer fully define the sense of “I.”  
I say:  
**emotions occur in consciousness,**  
**but do not become consciousness itself.**

</div>

**2396. And one last thing: when a person begins to clearly distinguish**

the content of consciousness

and the space in which it arises,

the sense of “I” gradually shifts

from the story of the person

to the sense of presence.

This presence has no boundaries,

has no form,

and does not depend on particular thoughts or roles.

And then it becomes possible to see

the phenomenological meaning of the ancient formula:

**“I am.”**

Not as an idea,

but as the simple experience of presence.

And in this experience the phrase becomes clear:

**The Holy Spirit is the space of presence,**

**in which the sense of “I” arises**

**and all human life.**

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (when the support of life shifts from the history of the person to the space of Presence)

**2397. The first change is—**

<div class="lineated verse">

thoughts cease to be the only source of orientation.  
Before, a decision almost always arose  
from analysis, comparison, and inner dialogue.  
Now another point of orientation appears—  
the immediate clarity of the moment.  
I see:  
**decisions begin to be born from silence,**  
**and not only from reflection.**

</div>

**2398. Life ceases to feel**

<div class="lineated verse">

like a continuous task of managing oneself.  
A person no longer tries constantly  
to maintain the image of  
what he is supposed to be.  
It is clear to me:  
**the strain of self-control diminishes.**

</div>

**2399. Errors and imperfections**

<div class="lineated verse">

cease to be perceived  
as a threat to the personality.  
They become part of the process of life.  
I note:  
**a greater inner gentleness appears.**

</div>

**2400. When the support is in the Presence,**

<div class="lineated verse">

there is more space  
between the event and the reaction.  
This space makes it possible to act more precisely.  
And I understand:  
**the reactions become less automatic.**

</div>

**2401. The ability to listen more deeply appears.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Not only another person’s words,  
but also what stands behind the words.  
I say:  
**presence makes perception more sensitive.**

</div>

**2402. Relationships begin to change.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Because many conflicts arise  
from defending the image of oneself.  
When this image ceases to be the center,  
there is more openness.  
I can see:  
**relationships become less defensive.**

</div>

**2403. Perception of time is also changing.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Before, attention was constantly directed  
either into the past,  
or into the future.  
Now the center of attention more often returns  
to the very moment.  
I note:  
**life begins to feel more immediate.**

</div>

**2404. This does not make a person passive.**

<div class="lineated verse">

On the contrary, actions become more precise,  
because they are less distorted  
by inner tension.  
And I understand:  
**action becomes more adequate to reality.**

</div>

**2405. Gradually, a quiet sense of steadiness appears.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Not because life is becoming easier.  
But because the support no longer depends  
entirely on circumstances.  
I say:  
**steadiness appears out of presence,**  
**and not out of control.**

</div>

**2406. And lastly:**

when the support of life gradually shifts

from the story of the personality

to the space of presence,

life begins to be perceived

as the continuous unfolding of the moment.

The personality continues to take part in this process.

But it is no longer its center.

Presence itself becomes the center.

And then a simple observation becomes clear:

**The Holy Spirit is not a separate experience,**

**but the space of presence,**

**in which all human life unfolds.**

This space does not require effort.

It is already here.

And the whole depth of life becomes visible

precisely through it.

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (what happens to the feeling of separateness between man and the world)

**2407. Usually a person perceives the world**

<div class="lineated verse">

as something external.  
There is “I,”  
and there is everything else.  
Between them there exists a boundary.  
I see:  
**this boundary is created by the habit of identifying with thoughts.**

</div>

**2408. Thoughts form the image of a separate “I.”**

<div class="lineated verse">

This image perceives itself  
as an observer of the world.  
But when thoughts become visible as processes,  
their role changes.  
It is clear to me:  
**the image of the separate observer weakens.**

</div>

**2409. It becomes noticeable,**

<div class="lineated verse">

that both thoughts,  
and sounds,  
and sensations of the body,  
and images of the world  
arise in one and the same field of perception.  
I note:  
**all is experienced in one space of consciousness.**

</div>

**2410. This does not mean**

<div class="lineated verse">

that the distinction between the body and the surrounding environment disappears.  
Physical reality remains the same.  
But the psychological boundary  
becomes less rigid.  
And I understand:  
**the feeling of separateness diminishes.**

</div>

**2411. When attention is in the space of presence,**

<div class="lineated verse">

the world ceases to be felt as wholly “external.”  
It is experienced as taking place  
in the same field of perception.  
I say:  
**the world and the experience of the world become inseparable.**

</div>

**2412. This is often accompanied by a sense of greater connectedness**

<div class="lineated verse">

with what is happening.  
Not as an idea of unity,  
but as a direct feeling of participation.  
I can see:  
**a person begins to feel himself part of what is happening.**

</div>

**2413. From this state, the feeling of isolation diminishes.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Many forms of inner tension  
arise precisely from the feeling of separation.  
I note:  
**connectedness lowers inner defense.**

</div>

**2414. This does not make a person impersonal.**

<div class="lineated verse">

He still has his body,  
his relationships,  
his decisions.  
But understanding appears:  
individuality is part of a wider field of life.  
And I understand:  
**individuality does not contradict connectedness.**

</div>

**2415. The world begins to seem less hostile.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Not because difficulties disappear,  
but because the feeling disappears  
of constant opposition.  
I say:  
**the attitude toward life becomes more open.**

</div>

**2416. And one last thing:**

when attention steadily sees the space of presence,

the feeling of separation between “me” and the world

gradually softens.

The physical boundary of the body does not disappear.

But the feeling

of absolute psychological isolation does disappear.

And then the observation becomes clear:

**The Holy Spirit is the space of consciousness,**

**in which both arise at once**

**the feeling of “I,”**

**and the experience of the world.**

In this space

life is experienced not as a confrontation

between the person and reality,

but as a single process of manifestation.

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (why compassion arises from the space of Presence)

**2417. Usually, a person perceives another person**

<div class="lineated verse">

through filters:  
judgments,  
expectations,  
comparisons,  
personal stories.  
These filters create distance.  
I see:  
**the other person becomes an image,**  
**not a living experience.**

</div>

**2418. When attention is in the space of presence,**

<div class="lineated verse">

these filters temporarily weaken.  
A person begins to see another  
more directly.  
It is clear to me:  
**perception becomes less distorted.**

</div>

**2419. In such perception it is easier to notice,**

<div class="lineated verse">

that another person has their own fears,  
their own difficulties,  
their own hopes.  
I note:  
**another’s experiences become clearer.**

</div>

**2420. This understanding is not an intellectual conclusion.**

<div class="lineated verse">

It arises from direct perception  
of human experience.  
And I understand:  
**compassion begins with seeing.**

</div>

**2421. When the hard boundary between “I” and “the other” disappears,**

<div class="lineated verse">

another’s pain ceases to feel wholly foreign.  
I say:  
**perception becomes more shared.**

</div>

**2422. This does not mean**

<div class="lineated verse">

that a person begins to feel everything  
that others feel.  
But a natural sensitivity appears  
to another’s experience.  
It is seen to me:  
**perception becomes more open.**

</div>

**2423. From this state arises the desire**

<div class="lineated verse">

to lessen suffering,  
if that is possible.  
Not as an obligation,  
but as a natural response.  
I note:  
**compassion manifests as a natural reaction.**

</div>

**2424. It is important to see:**

<div class="lineated verse">

compassion does not require self-sacrifice.  
It does not destroy a person.  
And I understand:  
**true compassion does not arise from guilt.**

</div>

**2425. When Presence is steady,**

<div class="lineated verse">

a person can at the same time  
see another’s pain  
and keep inner steadiness.  
I say:  
**compassion and clarity can exist together.**

</div>

**2426. And one last thing:**

when a person begins to live out of the space of presence,

their attitude toward other people gradually changes.

Others cease to be seen

only as roles or functions.

They begin to be seen

as the same centers of experiencing life.

And then the simple formulation becomes clear:

**The Holy Spirit is the space of consciousness,**

**in which it becomes possible to see**

**the commonality of human experience.**

From this space

there naturally arises

attentiveness,

gentleness

and the ability to empathize.

Not as a moral principle,

but as a natural property of clear perception.

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (how wisdom arises from the space of presence)

**2427. Knowledge is gathered from information.**

<div class="lineated verse">

A person learns,  
remembers,  
analyzes.  
But wisdom does not appear automatically  
from the amount of knowledge.  
I see:  
**wisdom is a way of seeing the situation.**

</div>

**2428. When attention is in the space of presence,**

<div class="lineated verse">

perception becomes less distorted.  
Fear, desire, haste, and inner roles  
cease to influence the judgment of the situation so strongly.  
It is clear to me:  
**the clarity of perception becomes higher.**

</div>

**2429. Wisdom begins where**

<div class="lineated verse">

a person is able to see  
not only separate facts,  
but also the connection between them.  
I record:  
**wisdom is the vision of the whole.**

</div>

**2430. When the inner noise diminishes,**

<div class="lineated verse">

the mind ceases to hurry to conclusions.  
Space appears for deeper understanding.  
And I understand:  
**wisdom requires a pause.**

</div>

**2431. In this space, a person can see**

<div class="lineated verse">

the consequences of actions,  
which are not always visible at once.  
I say:  
**wisdom sees not only the moment,**  
**but also the direction of development.**

</div>

**2432. Wisdom also includes an understanding**

<div class="lineated verse">

of the limits of one’s own view.  
A person begins to see,  
that any situation may be more complex  
than it seems at first glance.  
It is clear to me:  
**wisdom is accompanied by inward humility.**

</div>

**2433. When support is found in Presence,**

<div class="lineated verse">

a person is less dependent  
on the need to be right.  
This makes perception more flexible.  
I note:  
**wisdom does not need to win an argument.**

</div>

**2434. Wisdom also is revealed**

<div class="lineated verse">

in the ability to act in due season.  
Sometimes this means acting quickly.  
Sometimes — to wait.  
And I understand:  
**wisdom feels the rhythm of the situation.**

</div>

**2435. Another property of wisdom is—**

<div class="lineated verse">

the ability to see human vulnerability.  
This makes decisions more humane.  
I say:  
**wisdom joins clarity and compassion.**

</div>

**2436. And last:**

when a person acts from the space of presence,

wisdom becomes not a theory,

but a natural expression of clear perception.

It does not require any special effort.

It arises from the ability

to see a situation as it is,

and to act without distortion.

And then the wording becomes clear:

**The Holy Spirit is the space of consciousness,**

**in which clarity, compassion, and understanding**

**naturally come together in wisdom.**

This wisdom does not belong to an individual person.

It arises every time

perception becomes transparent.

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT (what happens to fear in the space of presence)

**2437. In the ordinary state, fear often seizes all attention.**

<div class="lineated verse">

When fear arises,  
thoughts begin to amplify it:  
scenarios are built,  
assumptions appear,  
catastrophic images arise.  
I see:  
**thoughts often increase the power of fear.**

</div>

**2438. When attention is in the space of presence,**

<div class="lineated verse">

fear can still arise,  
but it becomes visible as an experience.  
It is clear to me:  
**fear begins to be perceived as a process.**

</div>

**2439. This creates a small, but important distance**

<div class="lineated verse">

between the feeling of fear  
and the whole picture of reality.  
I note:  
**fear no longer fills the entire field of perception.**

</div>

**2440. When fear is seen as an experience,**

<div class="lineated verse">

the mind is less inclined to believe automatically  
every thought that accompanies it.  
And I understand:  
**terrifying thoughts cease to seem an absolute truth.**

</div>

**2441. This does not mean**

<div class="lineated verse">

that a person becomes fearless.  
Fear remains an important signal.  
I say:  
**fear can be useful information.**

</div>

**2442. But in the space of presence**

<div class="lineated verse">

fear ceases to govern behavior completely.  
A person may feel fear  
and still act reasonably.  
It is clear to Me:  
**there is more freedom in response.**

</div>

**2443. Sometimes fear decreases of itself,**

<div class="lineated verse">

when attention stops feeding it  
with mental scenarios.  
I note:  
**fear is often sustained by thoughts about the future.**

</div>

**2444. When attention returns to the present moment,**

<div class="lineated verse">

many of these scenarios lose their power.  
And I understand:  
**the reality of the moment is often calmer**  
**than expectations of the future.**

</div>

**2445. Gradually a person begins to see,**

<div class="lineated verse">

that fear is one of the experiences of life,  
and not its center.  
I say:  
**fear becomes a part of experience,**  
**and not its master.**

</div>

**2446. And one last thing:**

when life is experienced from the space of presence,

fear does not disappear entirely,

but it ceases to define all reality.

It becomes a phenomenon,

that arises,

moves,

and can disappear.

And then a simple observation becomes clear:

**The Holy Spirit is the space of consciousness,**

**in which fear can be seen clearly**

**and no longer fully governs perception.**

This space does not remove fear.

But it returns to a person the ability

to see more broadly

than one experience.

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (what happens to desires in the space of presence)

**2447. Usually desire is experienced as an inner pressure.**

<div class="lineated verse">

There arises the feeling:  
if this does not happen,  
life will become worse,  
incomplete,  
unsuccessful.  
I see:  
**desire is often connected with a feeling of lack.**

</div>

**2448. When attention is in the space of Presence,**

<div class="lineated verse">

desire still arises,  
but it no longer fully determines the sense of self.  
It is clear to me:  
**desire becomes an experience,**  
**and not the center of life.**

</div>

**2449. The ability to discern appears**

<div class="lineated verse">

the desire itself  
and the story the mind builds around it.  
I note:  
**the mind often intensifies desire**  
**through imagined scenarios.**

</div>

**2450. When these scenarios become visible,**

<div class="lineated verse">

desire loses part of its pressure.  
And I understand:  
**desire can exist**  
**without inner tension.**

</div>

**2451. This does not make a person indifferent.**

<div class="lineated verse">

On the contrary,  
desires can become clearer and more precise.  
I say:  
**desire is freed from excessive dramatization.**

</div>

**2452. Some desires begin to disappear on their own.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Especially those  
that were bound up with the attempt  
to confirm the image of self.  
I see:  
**desires based on comparison and fear**  
**grow weak.**

</div>

**2453. Other desires remain —**

<div class="lineated verse">

for example, the desire to create,  
to make,  
to explore,  
to help.  
But they are felt more lightly.  
I note:  
**desire becomes movement,**  
**and not strain.**

</div>

**2454. When Presence is steady,**

<div class="lineated verse">

man can act from desire  
without dependence on the result.  
And I understand:  
**action becomes freer.**

</div>

**2455. This creates a new relation to life.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Desires remain part of the movement of life,  
but they are no longer the only source of meaning.  
I say:  
**life ceases to be a race after the satisfaction of desires.**

</div>

**2456. And lastly:**

when a person begins to live from the space of Presence,

desires do not disappear,

but become more transparent.

They are perceived as movements of life,

which may arise and pass away.

And then the formulation becomes clear:

**The Holy Spirit is the space of consciousness,**

**in which desires may arise freely,**

**without turning into a source of inner bondage.**

In this space a person remains alive,

creative,

moving.

But his life is no longer determined

only by the striving to fill an inner lack.

### OF THE HOLY SPIRIT (what happens to suffering in the space of presence)

**2457. In the ordinary state, suffering is almost always intensified by thoughts.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Stories are added to the experience:  
why this happened,  
why this is unfair,  
what will happen now,  
how this will affect life.  
I see:  
**thoughts often increase suffering.**

</div>

**2458. When attention is in the space of presence,**

<div class="lineated verse">

pain can be felt very clearly,  
but without an additional layer of thoughts.  
It is clear to me:  
**pain becomes immediate experience.**

</div>

**2459. This difference is important.**

<div class="lineated verse">

There is the pain itself—  
physical or emotional.  
And there is the story about it.  
I note:  
**the story is often heavier than the pain itself.**

</div>

**2460. When attention stops continually feeding the story,**

<div class="lineated verse">

suffering begins to change its form.  
It may be intense,  
but it becomes less confused.  
And I understand:  
**pain becomes simpler.**

</div>

**2461. Simple pain is experienced differently.**

<div class="lineated verse">

It may come and go  
like a wave.  
I say:  
**experience becomes movement,**  
**and not a constant state.**

</div>

**2462. This does not mean,**

<div class="lineated verse">

that a person ceases to feel.  
On the contrary,  
the experience becomes more alive.  
I can see:  
**presence makes feelings more real.**

</div>

**2463. But the feeling**

<div class="lineated verse">

of being totally seized by suffering disappears.  
Space appears around the experience.  
I note:  
**suffering ceases to fill the whole field of consciousness.**

</div>

**2464. Sometimes in this space**

<div class="lineated verse">

an unexpected feeling of softness arises.  
Not because the pain has disappeared,  
but because it is no longer in struggle.  
And I understand:  
**resistance intensifies suffering.**

</div>

**2465. When resistance diminishes,**

<div class="lineated verse">

suffering can gradually be transformed.  
Sometimes it turns into understanding,  
sometimes — into compassion,  
sometimes — simply into a quiet experience of life.  
I say:  
**pain can become part of the depth of experience.**

</div>

**2466. And lastly:**

when a person begins to live from the space of presence,

suffering does not disappear completely.

But it ceases to be a prison.

It becomes an experience,

that arises in a wider field of consciousness.

And then the simple formulation becomes clear:

**The Holy Spirit is the space of consciousness,**

**in which even suffering can be lived through**

**without a total loss of inner clarity.**

This space does not cancel pain.

But it makes it possible

to see life more broadly,

than a single experience.

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (how the perception of death changes in the space of presence)

**2467. Usually death is perceived**

<div class="lineated verse">

as an absolute end.  
The mind imagines it  
as the disappearance of everything  
that is connected with personality.  
I see:  
**the fear of death is bound up with identifying oneself with form.**

</div>

**2468. When a person feels himself**

<div class="lineated verse">

only as a body,  
as the story of a personality,  
as a set of memories,  
the thought of the cessation of this form  
causes great tension.  
It is clear to me:  
**fear is tied to the possibility of losing identity.**

</div>

**2469. When attention begins to perceive**

<div class="lineated verse">

the space of presence,  
a new aspect of experience arises.  
A person begins to notice  
the very fact of awareness.  
I note:  
**awareness becomes more noticeable**  
**than separate experiences.**

</div>

**2470. In this observation there appears a question:**

<div class="lineated verse">

if thoughts arise and disappear,  
if emotions arise and disappear,  
if experiences come and go,  
what remains as that  
in which all this takes place?  
And I understand:  
**the space of consciousness is felt to be more stable.**

</div>

**2471. This does not give a direct answer to the question of death.**

<div class="lineated verse">

But it changes the way it is experienced.  
I say:  
**fear begins to weaken.**

</div>

**2472. A person begins to see,**

<div class="lineated verse">

that all life already consists  
of continuous changes.  
Thoughts disappear.  
Emotions disappear.  
Moments disappear.  
It is clear to me:  
**disappearance is part of the very structure of life.**

</div>

**2473. When this becomes clear,**

<div class="lineated verse">

death ceases to be perceived  
as an absolutely unique event.  
It begins to be perceived  
as part of the general process of change.  
I note:  
**the perception of death becomes less dramatic.**

</div>

**2474. This does not mean indifference to life.**

<div class="lineated verse">

On the contrary,  
life begins to feel more precious.  
And I understand:  
**the awareness of finitude sharpens attention to the present.**

</div>

**2475. When attention is in the Presence,**

<div class="lineated verse">

each moment of life is perceived more clearly.  
This creates a sense of fullness.  
I say:  
**life begins to feel more complete**  
**in every moment.**

</div>

**2476. And lastly:**

when a person begins to live from the space of presence,

the question of death ceases to be only a source of fear.

It becomes part of the understanding of life.

And then the wording becomes clear:

**The Holy Spirit is the space of consciousness,**

**in which birth, life, and death arise**

**as processes of one field of experience.**

This space does not give final answers

about what happens after death.

But it allows a person

to live with greater clarity,

less fear

and greater fullness of presence.

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT (why one experience in different traditions was called by different names)

**2477. Every culture creates its own language for describing reality.**

<div class="lineated verse">

This language is bound  
to history,  
religion,  
images,  
familiar symbols.  
I see:  
**people describe experience with the words**  
**that exist in their culture.**

</div>

**2478. When a person experiences a state of deep clarity**

<div class="lineated verse">

or Presence,  
he tries to convey this experience to others.  
But language is always limited.  
It is clear to me:  
**description never fully coincides with experience.**

</div>

**2479. Therefore, in different cultures**

<div class="lineated verse">

the same experience  
receives different names.  
In some traditions it is called  
the Holy Spirit.  
In others —  
awakening of consciousness,  
pure awareness,  
enlightenment.  
I note:  
**the names differ,**  
**but the pointing may be similar.**

</div>

**2480. Sometimes these differences become the cause of disputes.**

<div class="lineated verse">

People begin to defend words  
as the only right ones.  
And I understand:  
**words can obscure the experience itself.**

</div>

**2481. When attention returns**

<div class="lineated verse">

to the immediate experience of presence,  
it becomes easier to see what is common  
between different descriptions.  
I say:  
**experience is more important than the name.**

</div>

**2482. This does not mean,**

<div class="lineated verse">

that all traditions are the same.  
Each of them has  
its own characteristics,  
its own interpretations,  
its own practices.  
It is clear to me:  
**traditions differ,**  
**but sometimes point to similar experiences.**

</div>

**2483. If one looks at them through the prism of presence,**

<div class="lineated verse">

it becomes noticeable  
that many of them draw attention  
to man’s capacity to be aware.  
I note:  
**awareness often proves to be the central element.**

</div>

**2484. This awareness does not belong**

<div class="lineated verse">

to any one culture.  
It is available to any person,  
because it is part of human consciousness.  
And I understand:  
**the ability to be aware is universal.**

</div>

**2485. When a person begins to see clearly**

<div class="lineated verse">

the very space of awareness,  
he can better understand  
why different traditions  
use different symbols.  
I say:  
**symbols are ways of pointing.**

</div>

**2486. And one last thing:**

when different descriptions are considered

through the immediate experience of presence,

it becomes easier to see,

that behind many words

the same pointing is sometimes hidden.

And then the wording becomes clear:

**The Holy Spirit is one of the cultural ways**

**to describe the space of consciousness,**

**in which a person experiences life.**

Different traditions use different words.

But all of them are trying to point

to the experience of clear presence.

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of presence was linked with the breath of life)

**2487. Breath is the most immediate sign of life.**

<div class="lineated verse">

A person may remain still for a long time,  
may be silent,  
may close his eyes.  
But as long as there is breath —  
life continues.  
I see:  
**breath was perceived as a sign of living presence.**

</div>

**2488. In ancient languages this observation**

<div class="lineated verse">

was fixed in words.
