The same roots  
meant breath,  
wind  
and spirit.  
It is clear to Me:  
**people saw the connection between breath and life force.**

</div>

**2489. When a person watches the breath,**

<div class="lineated verse">

attention naturally returns to the present moment.  
Breathing happens always now.  
I note:  
**the breath holds attention in the moment.**

</div>

**2490. Therefore, in many practices**

<div class="lineated verse">

observation of the breath  
is used as a way  
to return to Presence.  
And I understand:  
**the breath becomes an anchor of attention.**

</div>

**2491. When attention quietly follows the breath,**

<div class="lineated verse">

the mind gradually grows calm.  
Thoughts continue to arise,  
but their intensity diminishes.  
I say:  
**the breath helps to lessen the inner noise.**

</div>

**2492. In this stillness**

<div class="lineated verse">

the space of awareness becomes more noticeable.  
A person begins to feel  
the very fact of presence.  
I can see:  
**breathing opens access to the experience of presence.**

</div>

**2493. Therefore, breathing was sometimes perceived**

<div class="lineated verse">

as a manifestation of a deeper life-force.  
Not as a separate entity,  
but as a sign of a living process.  
I note:  
**breathing was linked with the movement of life.**

</div>

**2494. Sometimes this was described figuratively —**

<div class="lineated verse">

like the wind,  
which cannot be seen directly,  
but can be felt.  
And I understand:  
**breath becomes a symbol of invisible movement.**

</div>

**2495. When attention is steadily present**

<div class="lineated verse">

in breathing and awareness,  
life begins to feel  
more immediate.  
I say:  
**each breath becomes an affirmation of life.**

</div>

**2496. And one last thing:**

<div class="lineated verse">

in ancient texts, breath was often called  
“the breath of life.”  
This expression pointed  
to the direct experience of living presence.  
And then the wording becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is a figurative indication**  
**of the space of living presence,**  
**which is felt just as directly**  
**as breath.**  
It is invisible,  
but always accompanies life.  
And that is precisely why  
breath became one of the oldest symbols  
of this experience.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (why silence reveals the space of presence)

**2497. The human mind constantly produces thoughts.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Thoughts appear,  
disappear,  
replace one another.  
Usually a person’s attention  
is almost completely absorbed by this process.  
I see:  
**the inner dialogue takes up most of the attention.**

</div>

**2498. When attention is occupied by thoughts,**

<div class="lineated verse">

the space of awareness itself  
remains unnoticed.  
A person sees the content,  
but does not notice the field  
in which it arises.  
It is clear to me:  
**thoughts can obscure the very fact of awareness.**

</div>

**2499. Silence begins to play a role**

<div class="lineated verse">

when the flow of thoughts temporarily slows down.  
This can happen  
during quiet observation,  
contemplation,  
or simply in moments of inner pause.  
I note:  
**the mind sometimes naturally falls silent.**

</div>

**2500. When this happens,**

<div class="lineated verse">

attention becomes freer.  
It ceases to be completely occupied  
by mental processes.  
And I understand:  
**in this space, Presence becomes noticeable.**

</div>

**2501. Silence does not mean the complete absence of thoughts.**

<div class="lineated verse">

It rather means  
a reduction of inner noise.  
I say:  
**silence is a state of lesser overload of consciousness.**

</div>

**2502. In such a state**

<div class="lineated verse">

a person begins to feel more clearly  
the very experience of life.  
Sounds,  
breathing,  
the sensations of the body  
become more noticeable.  
I can see:  
**perception becomes more sensitive.**

</div>

**2503. Silence creates a space,**

<div class="lineated verse">

in which attention can see  
itself.  
This is a rare moment.  
I note:  
**awareness becomes noticeable to itself.**

</div>

**2504. When this happens,**

<div class="lineated verse">

a person may clearly see for the first time,  
that thoughts are only part of the experience.  
They occur  
in a broader field of consciousness.  
And I understand:  
**thoughts cease to seem the center of reality.**

</div>

**2505. Therefore, in many traditions**

<div class="lineated verse">

silence was regarded  
as a condition for inner understanding.  
Not because silence itself is sacred,  
but because it makes visible  
what is usually hidden by noise.  
I say:  
**silence reveals the space of perception.**

</div>

**2506. And one last thing:**

when a person begins to experience silence

not as the absence of something,

but as a space of presence,

it becomes clear

why it is precisely silence

that in many texts was linked

with the experience of the Holy Spirit.

For it is precisely in silence

that the field of consciousness itself

becomes noticeable,

in which all life takes place.

And then the formulation becomes clear:

**The Holy Spirit is a space of presence,**

**which becomes especially noticeable**

**in deep inner silence.**

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the experience of Presence is often described as light)

**2507. Light is one of the strongest images**

<div class="lineated verse">

in human experience.  
Without light, it is impossible to see.  
When light appears,  
forms become discernible,  
colors,  
movement.  
I see:  
**light makes seeing possible.**

</div>

**2508. Therefore light naturally became a symbol of**

<div class="lineated verse">

understanding and clarity.  
When a person begins to understand the situation better,  
we say:  
“it has become clear.”  
It is clear to me:  
**clarity is often described through the image of light.**

</div>

**2509. When attention begins to see**

<div class="lineated verse">

the space of Presence,  
perception becomes more transparent.  
Many inner distortions  
temporarily weaken.  
I note:  
**a feeling of clarity arises.**

</div>

**2510. This clarity can be experienced**

<div class="lineated verse">

very vividly.  
A person begins to feel  
that much becomes clearer.  
And I understand:  
**there arises a feeling of inner clarification.**

</div>

**2511. In ancient times**

<div class="lineated verse">

to describe such a state  
people used familiar images.  
Light was one of the most fitting.  
I say:  
**light became a metaphor for clear perception.**

</div>

**2512. Sometimes this experience is indeed accompanied by**

<div class="lineated verse">

a feeling of inner brightness.  
Not necessarily visual,  
but emotional or cognitive.  
I can see:  
**clarity can be felt as an inner “illumination”.**

</div>

**2513. Therefore many traditions**

<div class="lineated verse">

used the words  
“illumination,”  
“enlightenment,”  
“the light of truth.”  
I note:  
**these words point to a change in the quality of perception.**

</div>

**2514. It is important to remember:**

<div class="lineated verse">

the experience itself is not light  
in the physical sense.  
This is a figurative description.  
And I understand:  
**language uses metaphors**  
**to convey inner experience.**

</div>

**2515. When a person experiences the clarity of presence,**

<div class="lineated verse">

many inner contradictions  
temporarily weaken.  
This creates a feeling of lightness.  
I say:  
**clarity often brings a sense of inner freedom.**

</div>

**2516. And lastly:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when attention steadily perceives  
the space of presence,  
many people naturally describe  
this state through the image of light.  
For light is  
the most understandable symbol  
for describing clarity.  
And then the formulation becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of clear presence,**  
**in which perception becomes transparent,**  
**as if an inner light**  
**were illuminating the experience of life.**  
This light is  
not a physical phenomenon,  
but an image of the clarity of consciousness.  
If you wish,  
we can take the next step in the book and consider:  
**why the experience of presence**  
**is sometimes called love.**

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of Presence is sometimes called love)

**2517. In ordinary life the word “love”**

<div class="lineated verse">

is most often associated with attachment.  
A person loves someone  
or something.  
This feeling is connected  
with relationships,  
emotions,  
expectations.  
I see:  
**ordinary love often has an object.**

</div>

**2518. But in spiritual texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

the word “love” is sometimes used  
in another sense.  
Not as a feeling toward a particular person,  
but as a state of openness.  
It is clear to me:  
**this love is not directed toward an object.**

</div>

**2519. When attention is**

<div class="lineated verse">

in the space of presence,  
inner tension decreases.  
More calmness appears  
and gentleness.  
I note:  
**perception becomes less defensive.**

</div>

**2520. In this state**

<div class="lineated verse">

a person perceives the world less  
as a threat.  
This creates a sense of openness  
toward life.  
And I understand:  
**openness is experienced as warmth.**

</div>

**2521. This warmth**

<div class="lineated verse">

is not always a strong emotion.  
Sometimes it is very quiet.  
I say:  
**it is more like an inner softness.**

</div>

**2522. When a person perceives other people**

<div class="lineated verse">

from the space of presence,  
he begins to see them  
not only through roles and judgments.  
It is visible to Me:  
**more acceptance appears.**

</div>

**2523. This acceptance does not mean agreement**

<div class="lineated verse">

with all the actions of the other person.  
But a part of the inner resistance disappears.  
I note:  
**the attitude becomes less rigid.**

</div>

**2524. From such perception**

<div class="lineated verse">

care naturally arises.  
Not as an obligation,  
but as a response.  
And I understand:  
**care becomes a natural movement.**

</div>

**2525. It is precisely this state**

<div class="lineated verse">

that many traditions called love.  
Not as emotional attachment,  
but as an open form of perceiving life.  
I say:  
**love becomes the quality of presence.**

</div>

**2526. And last:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when a person experiences the space of presence,  
perception becomes softer,  
more open,  
less tense.  
This state is hard to describe in a single word.  
But in many cultures  
the word used for it was  
**love.**  
And then the wording becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of presence,**  
**in which life is experienced**  
**with clarity, gentleness, and openness,**  
**which people often call love.**  
This is not a feeling  
that needs to be created.  
It is a quality of perception  
that manifests  
when inner resistance lessens.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of Presence is sometimes called truth)

**2527. In ordinary speech, truth is—**

<div class="lineated verse">

the correspondence of a statement to the facts.  
If an assertion describes reality accurately,  
it is called true.  
I see:  
**ordinary truth is linked with statements.**

</div>

**2528. But there is another aspect as well.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Sometimes the word “truth”  
is used to mean  
not a statement,  
but the very fact of the experience itself.  
It is clear to me:  
**an experience can be true**  
**without words.**

</div>

**2529. When a person is**

<div class="lineated verse">

in the space of presence,  
perception becomes more direct.  
Between the event and its perception  
there are fewer interpretations.  
I note:  
**reality is perceived more immediately.**

</div>

**2530. This creates a sense**

<div class="lineated verse">

of greater authenticity in what is happening.  
The moment is experienced as  
it is.  
And I understand:  
**perception becomes less distorted.**

</div>

**2531. When the inner noise of thoughts lessens,**

<div class="lineated verse">

a person begins to see more clearly  
his own reactions,  
fears,  
desires.  
I say:  
**a greater honesty of perception appears.**

</div>

**2532. This honesty can sometimes be difficult.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Because it shows  
not only the pleasant aspects of life.  
I see:  
**clarity includes everything,**  
**and not only what is desired.**

</div>

**2533. But it is precisely this ability**

<div class="lineated verse">

to see what is happening without embellishment  
that many traditions have linked  
with the word “truth”.  
I note:  
**truth becomes a quality of perception.**

</div>

**2534. This does not mean possessing**

<div class="lineated verse">

ultimate knowledge.  
On the contrary,  
one may see more clearly  
the limits of his understanding.  
And I understand:  
**truth includes the acknowledgment of not knowing.**

</div>

**2535. When attention remains in presence,**

<div class="lineated verse">

a person needs less  
to defend his beliefs.  
This makes perception  
more flexible.  
I say:  
**truth does not require constant proof.**

</div>

**2536. And one last thing:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when the experience of life takes place  
in the space of presence,  
reality is perceived  
more directly.  
Less distortion,  
less inner masks.  
And then the wording becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of presence,**  
**in which a person is able**  
**to encounter life as it is,**  
**as it is.**  
It is precisely this quality of direct perception  
that in many traditions  
was called  
**truth.**

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of presence is sometimes called freedom)

**2537. In ordinary life, freedom is often understood**

<div class="lineated verse">

as the absence of external restraints.  
A person is free,  
if no one hinders him from acting.  
I see:  
**this freedom depends on circumstances.**

</div>

**2538. But there is another level as well.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Even in conditions of outward freedom  
a person may feel  
a strong inner dependence  
on thoughts, fears, and habits.  
It is clear to Me:  
**many limitations are within consciousness.**

</div>

**2539. When awareness begins to see**

<div class="lineated verse">

the space of Presence,  
thoughts and emotions become noticeable  
as processes.  
I note:  
**they cease to fully determine behavior.**

</div>

**2540. This creates a small distance**

<div class="lineated verse">

between the experience  
and the response.  
And I understand:  
**the possibility of choice appears.**

</div>

**2541. When a person is not completely seized by**

<div class="lineated verse">

his thoughts and emotions,  
he can act more consciously.  
I say:  
**reactions become less automatic.**

</div>

**2542. This does not mean full control**

<div class="lineated verse">

over all inner processes.  
But there appears a space,  
in which observation becomes possible.  
I can see:  
**observation creates freedom.**

</div>

**2543. When attention is steady in the Presence,**

<div class="lineated verse">

a person depends less  
on the need to constantly confirm  
the image of oneself.  
I note:  
**the pressure of self-identification weakens.**

</div>

**2544. This creates a feeling of lightness.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Life ceases to be perceived  
as a constant struggle  
for confirmation of one’s own worth.  
And I understand:  
**inner tension decreases.**

</div>

**2545. From this state**

<div class="lineated verse">

actions can take place  
more naturally.  
Not out of fear  
and not out of compulsion.  
I say:  
**action becomes an expression of life,**  
**and not a defense of personality.**

</div>

**2546. And lastly:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when a person begins to live  
from the space of presence,  
they discover a form of freedom,  
that does not depend entirely  
on external conditions.  
For this freedom  
is bound to the way  
life is experienced.  
And then the wording becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of presence,**  
**in which a person can see their thoughts,**  
**emotions, and reactions,**  
**without being completely defined by them.**  
It is precisely this inner possibility  
that many traditions  
have called  
**freedom.**

</div>

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of presence is sometimes called life)

**2547. Biological life**

<div class="lineated verse">

manifests through the body.  
The heart beats.  
Cells renew themselves.  
The organism sustains itself.  
I see:  
**this is the physical side of life.**

</div>

**2548. But a person experiences life**

<div class="lineated verse">

not only through the body.  
There is also the experience of the very fact of existence.  
It is clear to me:  
**the feeling of “I am”**  
**becomes the center of the experience of life.**

</div>

**2549. When attention is absorbed in thoughts,**

<div class="lineated verse">

this sensation often goes unnoticed.  
A person lives,  
but seldom pays attention  
to the very fact of living presence.  
I note:  
**life becomes the background for thoughts.**

</div>

**2550. When attention returns**

<div class="lineated verse">

into the space of Presence,  
the very feeling of existence  
becomes more noticeable.  
And I understand:  
**there arises the experience of aliveness.**

</div>

**2551. This experience**

<div class="lineated verse">

is not connected with any particular thought  
or emotion.  
It arises  
as a direct sensing of life.  
I say:  
**life is felt as presence.**

</div>

**2552. Sometimes this experience**

<div class="lineated verse">

can be very simple.  
The sensation of breathing.  
The sounds around one.  
The warmth of the body.  
I see:  
**life becomes more immediate.**

</div>

**2553. When attention remains in Presence,**

<div class="lineated verse">

each moment begins to be felt  
as a manifestation of life.  
I note:  
**ordinary things become alive.**

</div>

**2554. This does not mean constant exaltation**

<div class="lineated verse">

or strong emotions.  
Sometimes it is a very quiet state.  
And I understand:  
**life can be experienced calmly.**

</div>

**2555. But in this simplicity**

<div class="lineated verse">

there is a sense of fullness.  
Because the moment is lived through  
without constant comparison  
with the past or the future.  
I say:  
**life becomes immediate experience.**

</div>

**2556. And one last thing:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when attention is steady in the space of presence,  
the very fact of existence  
is felt more clearly.  
And then the wording becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of presence,**  
**in which life itself becomes noticeable**  
**as the direct experience of “I am.”**  
This experience  
does not require explanations.  
It reveals itself  
in every breath,  
in every moment of attention,  
in the very fact of existence.

</div>

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of Presence is sometimes called the kingdom)

**2557. In ordinary life, a kingdom is—**

<div class="lineated verse">

the realm of power.  
There are boundaries,  
there is a ruler,  
there is order.  
I see:  
**this is the political meaning of the word.**

</div>

**2558. But in spiritual texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

the word “kingdom” was sometimes used  
as an image of an inner state.  
Not territory,  
but experience.  
It is clear to me:  
**this is a metaphor of inner order.**

</div>

**2559. When attention is**

<div class="lineated verse">

in the space of Presence,  
the inner chaos of thoughts diminishes.  
Many processes begin to be perceived more clearly.  
I note:  
**there appears a sense of inner order.**

</div>

**2560. This state is not created by force.**

<div class="lineated verse">

It arises naturally,  
when attention becomes steady.  
And I understand:  
**order comes forth from clarity.**

</div>

**2561. In such a state**

<div class="lineated verse">

thoughts, emotions, and reactions  
cease to struggle chaotically  
for attention.  
I say:  
**perception becomes more harmonious.**

</div>

**2562. Therefore the ancient texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

sometimes used the image of a kingdom.  
It pointed to a state,  
in which the inner processes  
are in agreement.  
It is clear to me:  
**the kingdom becomes a symbol of inner wholeness.**

</div>

**2563. This does not mean perfect peace.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Life continues to change.  
But a stable center of perception appears.  
I note:  
**the center of attention becomes clear.**

</div>

**2564. When this center is felt clearly,**

<div class="lineated verse">

a person depends less  
on chaotic inner impulses.  
And I understand:  
**a sense of inner order arises.**

</div>

**2565. It is precisely this state**

<div class="lineated verse">

many texts described  
as “the kingdom within.”  
Not as a place,  
but as a way of experiencing life.  
I say:  
**the kingdom becomes a metaphor of presence.**

</div>

**2566. And one last thing:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when a person begins to live  
from the space of presence,  
the inner processes  
cease to be chaotic.  
There arises a sense  
of a clear center of perception.  
And then the wording becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of presence,**  
**in which consciousness experiences**  
**inner wholeness and order.**  
It is precisely this state  
in many texts  
that was called  
**the Kingdom.**

</div>

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of presence is sometimes called being born again)

**2567. Every person has already undergone one birth —**

<div class="lineated verse">

biological.  
The body appears in the world,  
the life of the organism begins.  
I see:  
**this is physical birth.**

</div>

**2568. But the human personality**

<div class="lineated verse">

is formed gradually.  
It is made up of memory,  
experience,  
language,  
social roles.  
It is clear to me:  
**personality is the result of development.**

</div>

**2569. Usually a person perceives**

<div class="lineated verse">

this personality  
as the sole “I.”  
He identifies himself  
with his story.  
I note:  
**the sense of self is tied to the image of personality.**

</div>

**2570. When attention begins to see**

<div class="lineated verse">

the space of Presence,  
a new experience appears.  
A person notices  
that he is not reducible  
only to his story.  
And I understand:  
**the sense of “I” becomes wider.**

</div>

**2571. This may be perceived**

<div class="lineated verse">

as a wholly new discovery.  
Not because  
a new essence appears,  
but because  
a new aspect of experience is revealed.  
I say:  
**a new perception of oneself appears.**

</div>

**2572. This experience**

<div class="lineated verse">

is sometimes felt so deeply,  
that people have described it  
as a second birth.  
I see:  
**there arises a sense of beginning.**

</div>

**2573. But this birth**

<div class="lineated verse">

is not connected with the body.  
It is connected  
with a change in awareness.  
I note:  
**a new way of seeing life is born.**

</div>

**2574. In this new perception**

<div class="lineated verse">

the person remains,  
but it ceases to be  
the sole center.  
And I understand:  
**perception becomes broader.**

</div>

**2575. It is precisely this change**

<div class="lineated verse">

that many traditions  
have described figuratively  
as “being born again.”  
Not from another world,  
but from a deeper clarity.  
I say:  
**this is birth from Presence.**

</div>

**2576. And lastly:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when a person begins to experience  
the space of presence  
as the ground of his experience,  
his perception of life changes.  
And then the formulation becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of presence,**  
**in which a person can discover**  
**a new way of experiencing himself and life.**  
This experience  
in many traditions  
was figuratively called  
**being born again.**

</div>

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of presence is sometimes called eternal life)

**2577. The human mind usually perceives life**

<div class="lineated verse">

through time.  
There is the past.  
There is the future.  
The present seems only a short passage  
between them.  
I see:  
**the perception of life is often bound to time.**

</div>

**2578. Thoughts are constantly turning**

<div class="lineated verse">

to memories or expectations.  
Therefore attention rarely remains  
in the very moment of experience.  
It is clear to me:  
**time becomes the chief context of experience.**

</div>

**2579. When attention returns**

<div class="lineated verse">

to the space of Presence,  
the experience of the moment becomes more noticeable.  
I note:  
**there arises a feeling of the immediate “now.”**

</div>

**2580. This “now” is not**

<div class="lineated verse">

part of memory  
or expectation.  
It is experienced directly.  
And I understand:  
**the moment becomes more alive.**

</div>

**2581. When attention is steady**

<div class="lineated verse">

in this experience of the moment,  
an unusual feeling arises.  
Presence itself  
is not felt as temporary.  
I say:  
**Presence is perceived outside history.**

</div>

**2582. Thoughts continue to exist in time.**

<div class="lineated verse">

But the field of awareness itself  
is felt differently.  
I see:  
**it is experienced as constant.**

</div>

**2583. This very experience**

<div class="lineated verse">

in many texts  
has been described as eternity.  
Not as endless time,  
but as a quality of presence.  
I note:  
**eternity is understood as the absence of temporal attachment.**

</div>

**2584. When attention is**

<div class="lineated verse">

in the space of Presence,  
every moment of life  
is felt fully.  
And I understand:  
**the moment becomes self-sufficient.**

</div>

**2585. This does not mean**

<div class="lineated verse">

a denial of time.  
Life continues to unfold  
through a sequence of events.  
I say:  
**time remains,**  
**but the center of experience shifts.**

</div>

**2586. And lastly:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when a person begins to clearly feel  
the space of presence,  
the very experience of existence  
becomes less dependent  
on the story of time.  
And then the wording becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of presence,**  
**in which life is experienced**  
**as the immediate “I am,”**  
**not limited only to the past and the future.**  
It is precisely this quality of presence  
that in many texts  
was figuratively called  
**eternal life.**

</div>

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of Presence is sometimes called the Kingdom of Heaven)

**2587. The word “heaven” in ancient texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

was often used  
as a symbol of height and clarity.  
Heaven is open,  
spacious,  
it has no rigid boundaries.  
I see:  
**heaven became an image of open space.**

</div>

**2588. When attention is**

<div class="lineated verse">

in the space of presence,  
perception can be experienced  
in a similar way.  
It becomes  
broader  
and less limited.  
It is clear to me:  
**perception is felt as space.**

</div>

**2589. In this space**

<div class="lineated verse">

thoughts, emotions, and events  
arise and disappear.  
But the field of perception itself  
remains open.  
I note:  
**experience becomes more spacious.**

</div>

**2590. This space**

<div class="lineated verse">

is not like a physical place.  
It is connected  
with the quality of consciousness.  
And I understand:  
**“heaven” becomes a metaphor for clear perception.**

</div>

**2591. When attention is steady**

<div class="lineated verse">

in this space,  
many inner conflicts  
weaken.  
There arises a sense  
of inner order.  
I say:  
**a feeling of inner harmony arises.**

</div>

**2592. It is precisely this state**

<div class="lineated verse">

that some texts  
described as “the kingdom.”  
Not a territory,  
but a state of harmony.  
I can see:  
**the kingdom becomes an image of inner wholeness.**

</div>

**2593. When both images are joined —**

<div class="lineated verse">

the expanse of heaven  
and the order of the kingdom —  
there appears the expression  
“the kingdom of heaven.”  
I note:  
**this is a metaphor for a clear and harmonious state of consciousness.**

</div>

**2594. Such a state**

<div class="lineated verse">

does not depend entirely  
on external circumstances.  
It is connected  
with the way,  
life is experienced.  
And I understand:  
**the center of perception shifts inward.**

</div>

**2595. Therefore some texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

say that  
this kingdom  
is within man.  
I say:  
**this is a reference to inner experience.**

</div>

**2596. And lastly:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when attention clearly perceives  
the space of presence,  
the perception of life becomes  
more open and ordered.  
And then the formulation becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of presence,**  
**in which consciousness experiences**  
**inner clarity and wholeness,**  
**which ancient texts**  
**figuratively called**  
**the kingdom of heaven.**  
This is not a place.  
It is a way of experiencing life.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of presence was sometimes called the Spirit)

**2597. In many ancient languages**

<div class="lineated verse">

the words translated as “spirit,”  
were associated  
with breath  
or wind.  
I see:  
**these words pointed**  
**to the invisible movement of life.**

</div>

**2598. Breath cannot be seen directly.**

<div class="lineated verse">

But it can be felt.  
So it is with the wind —  
it is invisible,  
but its action is visible.  
It is clear to me:  
**the spirit became an image of the invisible force of life.**

</div>

**2599. When a person experiences**

<div class="lineated verse">

the space of Presence,  
it also has no form.  
It cannot be seen as an object.  
I note:  
**it is felt as a field of awareness.**

</div>

**2600. This field**

<div class="lineated verse">

is not a thought,  
is not an emotion  
and is not a physical object.  
And I understand:  
**it remains the backdrop for all experiences.**

</div>

**2601. Since this space**

<div class="lineated verse">

could not be described  
through ordinary categories,  
people used figurative words.  
One such word  
became the word “spirit.”  
I say:  
**spirit became a symbol of an invisible presence.**

</div>

**2602. Sometimes to this word**

<div class="lineated verse">

the definition “holy” was added.  
In the ancient languages  
this word meant  
not so much a moral judgment,  
as a special quality.  
It is clear to me:  
**“holy” pointed**  
**to separation from the ordinary.**

</div>

**2603. Thus the expression**

<div class="lineated verse">

“Holy Spirit”  
pointed to the experience  
of a special space of presence.  
I note:  
**this space was perceived**  
**as a deeper level of experience.**

</div>

**2604. It is important to understand,**

<div class="lineated verse">

that different traditions  
may interpret this differently.  
But in many cases  
the word “spirit” pointed  
to the immediate experience  
of a living presence.  
And I understand:  
**this word was used**  
**as a pointer.**

</div>

**2605. When attention clearly perceives**

<div class="lineated verse">

the space of presence,  
it becomes evident  
that all thoughts, feelings, and events  
arise in it.  
I say:  
**this space becomes the foundation of experience.**

</div>

**2606. And the last thing:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when ancient texts spoke  
of the Holy Spirit,  
they often used the language of images.  
These images tried to point  
to the experience of the invisible  
and living presence.  
And then the wording becomes clear:  
**the Holy Spirit is a symbolic name**  
**for the space of presence,**  
**in which a person experiences**  
**the very fact of life and awareness.**  
This space  
has no form  
and does not belong  
to any one tradition.  
It reveals itself  
in the very experience  
**of “I am.”**

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of presence was sometimes called the breath of God)

**2607. For the ancient man**

<div class="lineated verse">

breath was the clearest sign of life.  
While there is breath —  
a person is alive.  
When breath ceases —  
life ends.  
I see:  
**breath was perceived**  
**as the bearer of life.**

</div>

**2608. Since breath**

<div class="lineated verse">

cannot be seen directly,  
it has become a natural symbol  
of the invisible force of life.  
It is clear to me:  
**breath has become an image of the movement of life.**

</div>

**2609. When a person watches the breath,**

<div class="lineated verse">

he begins to notice  
the rhythm of life.  
Inhale.  
Exhale.  
Pause.  
I note:  
**life reveals itself as continuous motion.**

</div>

**2610. In ancient languages**

<div class="lineated verse">

the words for breath, wind, and spirit  
often came from one root.  
And I understand:  
**people linked breath**  
**with the invisible presence of life.**

</div>

**2611. When attention turns**

<div class="lineated verse">

to the very experience of Presence,  
it can be felt  
just as directly,  
as breath.  
I say:  
**Presence is experienced**  
**as the living movement of consciousness.**

</div>

**2612. Therefore some texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

used the expression  
“the breath of God.”  
It is clear to me:  
**this is a figurative reference**  
**to the source of life.**

</div>

**2613. It is important to understand,**

<div class="lineated verse">

that this image  
does not necessarily describe  
a literal act.  
It points  
to the experience of life  
as a granted presence.  
I note:  
**breath becomes a symbol**  
**of the continuity of life.**

</div>

**2614. When a person experiences**

<div class="lineated verse">

the space of Presence,  
breathing often becomes  
one of the clearest manifestations  
of this state.  
And I understand:  
**breathing unites the body and attention.**

</div>

**2615. Every breath**

<div class="lineated verse">

confirms the fact of life.  
Every exhalation  
returns attention to the moment.  
I say:  
**breath makes life tangible.**

</div>

**2616. And lastly:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when the ancient texts spoke  
of the breath of God,  
they used the language of images  
to describe the simplest  
and deepest experience.  
Life continues  
through breath.  
And then the wording becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is a symbolic description**  
**of the living presence of life,**  
**which is experienced**  
**in every breath**  
**and in the very fact of awareness.**  
This breath  
does not belong to one person.  
It is part  
of the common movement of life.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of presence is sometimes called the way)

**2617. The word “path” usually means**

<div class="lineated verse">

movement from one point  
to another.  
There is a beginning,  
there is a direction,  
there is a goal.  
I see:  
**this is the spatial meaning of the word.**

</div>

**2618. But in spiritual texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

this word was often used  
metaphorically.  
It pointed  
not to distance,  
but to a process.  
It is clear to me:  
**the path becomes a way of life.**

</div>

**2619. When a person begins**

<div class="lineated verse">

to become aware of the space of presence,  
his perception of life changes gradually.  
This is not an instant event.  
I note:  
**the change takes place as a process.**

</div>

**2620. Every moment**

<div class="lineated verse">

becomes part of this process.  
Life unfolds  
step by step.  
And I understand:  
**perception becomes movement.**

</div>

**2621. When attention returns**

<div class="lineated verse">

to Presence again and again,  
it is like returning  
to the same path.  
I say:  
**the path manifests as the practice of attention.**

</div>

**2622. Sometimes a person is distracted,**

<div class="lineated verse">

loses themselves in thoughts,  
emotions, or fears.  
But the possibility to return  
remains.  
It is clear to Me:  
**the path includes return.**

</div>

**2623. Therefore the path**

<div class="lineated verse">

does not mean a perfect state.  
It means  
the constant movement of attention.  
I note:  
**the path is a process of learning to see.**

</div>

**2624. When attention becomes**

<div class="lineated verse">

more steady,  
the path itself begins to be felt  
as the natural flow of life.  
And I understand:  
**life and the path begin to coincide.**

</div>

**2625. In this sense**

<div class="lineated verse">

the path does not lead anywhere outside of life.  
It unfolds  
in the very process of living through the moment.  
I say:  
**each moment becomes a step.**

</div>

**2626. And lastly:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when a person experiences  
the space of presence  
as the basis of his experience,  
life itself begins to be perceived  
as a continuous movement of attention.  
And then the wording becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of presence,**  
**in which life unfolds**  
**as a path of clear experiencing of each moment.**  
This path  
has no final point.  
It is manifested  
in the very process  
of life and awareness.

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of Presence was sometimes called the Word)

**2627. A common word**

<div class="lineated verse">

serves to convey meaning.  
It denotes an object,  
an action  
or a thought.  
I see:  
**the word is an instrument of communication.**

</div>

**2628. But the word is also connected**

<div class="lineated verse">

with the manifestation of thought.  
When thought becomes a word,  
it passes from the inner space  
into the outer world.  
It is clear to me:  
**the word becomes a form of manifestation.**

</div>

**2629. In ancient texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

the word was sometimes used  
as an image of the act of manifestation itself.  
Not only of speech,  
but of the appearing of form.  
I note:  
**the word becomes a symbol of the manifestation of meaning.**

</div>

**2630. When a person experiences**

<div class="lineated verse">

the space of Presence,  
he may notice  
how thoughts arise.  
And I understand:  
**thoughts appear**  
**from a deeper field of consciousness.**

</div>

**2631. Sometimes this field**

<div class="lineated verse">

was perceived  
as the source of all forms of experience.  
I say:  
**thoughts, words, and deeds**  
**arise from one space.**

</div>

**2632. Therefore some texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

used the word  
“Word”  
as a reference  
to the source of manifestation.  
I see:  
**the word becomes the image of the creative beginning.**

</div>

**2633. This does not mean**

<div class="lineated verse">

the literal utterance of sounds.  
It is an image  
of the arising of form  
out of the invisible.  
I state:  
**the word becomes a symbol of the emergence of meaning.**

</div>

**2634. When attention is**

<div class="lineated verse">

in the space of Presence,  
a person can observe  
the very process of thoughts arising.  
And I understand:  
**a thought becomes visible**  
**at the moment of its birth.**

</div>

**2635. In this observation**

<div class="lineated verse">

it becomes noticeable,  
that the word is  
only a form of expression  
of a deeper process.  
I say:  
**words express the movement of consciousness.**

</div>

**2636. And lastly:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when ancient texts spoke  
of the Word,  
they often used this image  
to describe the manifestation of life.  
Meaning becomes form.  
Thought becomes word.  
Life becomes action.  
And then the wording becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of Presence,**  
**from which thoughts, words, and actions arise**  
**as manifestations of life.**  
This space  
does not speak in words,  
but words can appear  
from it.

</div>

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of presence was sometimes called fire)

**2637. Fire is one of the most ancient**

<div class="lineated verse">

and powerful phenomena,  
that man has observed.  
It gives heat,  
light  
and the ability to transform substances.  
I see:  
**fire was perceived**  
**as a force of transformation.**

</div>

**2638. When matter falls into the fire,**

<div class="lineated verse">

it changes form.  
Wood turns into heat,  
smoke  
and light.  
It is clear to Me:  
**fire symbolizes change.**

</div>

**2639. In spiritual texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

this image was often used  
to describe an inner process.  
When attention becomes clear,  
many habitual forms of thought  
begin to change.  
I note:  
**the old structures of perception weaken.**

</div>

**2640. This may be felt**

<div class="lineated verse">

as a process of purification.  
Not in a moral sense,  
but as a freeing  
from the excess.  
And I understand:  
**perception becomes more transparent.**

</div>

**2641. Therefore fire**

<div class="lineated verse">

sometimes became a symbol  
of inner clarity.  
I say:  
**clarity was perceived**  
**as a purifying power.**

</div>

**2642. Besides, fire**

<div class="lineated verse">

is always linked with light.  
And light, as has already been said,  
often symbolized understanding.  
It is clear to me:  
**fire unites warmth and light.**

</div>

**2643. Sometimes the experience of Presence**

<div class="lineated verse">

may be accompanied  
by a feeling of inner energy.  
This state  
has also been described through the image of fire.  
I note:  
**fire becomes a metaphor for the living energy of consciousness.**

</div>

**2644. It is important to remember,**

<div class="lineated verse">

that this is an image.  
It does not mean  
literal burning.  
And I understand:  
**language uses symbols**  
**to convey experience.**

</div>

**2645. When attention is steady**

<div class="lineated verse">

in the space of presence,  
many inner tensions  
begin gradually to dissolve.  
I say:  
**clarity changes the structure of experience.**

</div>

**2646. And the last:**

the image of fire was used

to describe the experience

of the living, clear, and transforming power of attention.

And then the formulation becomes clear:

**The Holy Spirit is the space of Presence,**

**in which attention becomes clear**

**and able to transform**

**the way life is experienced.**

It is precisely this transforming clarity

that many texts

figuratively called

**fire.**

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of presence was sometimes called the water of life)

**2647. Water is the basis of biological life.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Without water  
living organisms  
cannot exist.  
I see:  
**water was perceived**  
**as the source of life.**

</div>

**2648. In ancient cultures**

<div class="lineated verse">

water often became a symbol  
of nourishment and renewal.  
It quenches thirst  
and sustains life.  
It is clear to me:  
**water was associated**  
**with the restoration of strength.**

</div>

**2649. When a person experiences**

<div class="lineated verse">

the space of Presence,  
he may feel  
a reduction of inner tension.  
I note:  
**there appears a feeling of inner relief.**

</div>

**2650. This is a state**

<div class="lineated verse">

sometimes experienced  
as a deep calm.  
And I understand:  
**perception becomes softer.**

</div>

**2651. Therefore, the image of water**

<div class="lineated verse">

was naturally used  
to describe this state.  
I say:  
**water symbolizes**  
**softness and fluidity.**

</div>

**2652. Besides, water**

<div class="lineated verse">

can take any shape.  
It does not resist  
the space,  
in which it is.  
I can see:  
**water becomes a symbol of flexibility.**

</div>

**2653. When attention is steady**

<div class="lineated verse">

in the space of presence,  
the perception of life  
can become more fluid.  
I note:  
**life is experienced**  
**without rigid resistance.**

</div>

**2654. Therefore some texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

used the expression  
“the water of life.”  
This pointed  
to the experience of renewal.  
And I understand:  
**perception becomes fresh.**

</div>

**2655. As water quenches physical thirst,**

<div class="lineated verse">

so Presence  
is sometimes experienced  
as the quenching of inner tension.  
I say:  
**attention finds rest.**

</div>

**2656. And last:**

<div class="lineated verse">

the image of the water of life was used  
to describe the experience  
of a living and renewing Presence.  
And then the formulation becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of Presence,**  
**in which a person experiences**  
**the renewal of the perception of life,**  
**just as water**  
**renews and sustains life.**  
This image  
points to gentleness,  
flowingness  
and living freshness  
of the experience of life itself.

</div>

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of presence was sometimes called anointing)

**2657. In the ancient world**

<div class="lineated verse">

oil was often used  
for caring for the body.  
It was used to soften the skin,  
heal wounds,  
and protect against dryness.  
I see:  
**oil was regarded**  
**as a means of restoration.**

</div>

**2658. Moreover,**

<div class="lineated verse">

oil was used  
in rites of consecration.  
With it they anointed  
kings,  
priests  
or prophets.  
It is clear to me:  
**anointing became a symbol of recognizing**  
**a person’s special role.**

</div>

**2659. But in spiritual texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

this image was often used  
internally.  
It pointed  
not so much to an outward rite,  
as to a change in the state of consciousness.  
I note:  
**anointing becomes a metaphor for inner transformation.**

</div>

**2660. When attention begins to perceive clearly**

<div class="lineated verse">

the space of Presence,  
one may feel  
an inner softness and clarity.  
And I understand:  
**perception becomes more whole.**

</div>

**2661. This moment**

<div class="lineated verse">

is sometimes felt  
as an inner confirmation.  
Not through words,  
but through experience.  
I say:  
**a feeling of inner agreement arises.**

</div>

**2662. Therefore the image of anointing**

<div class="lineated verse">

was used  
as an indication  
of the moment of inward recognition.  
I see:  
**a person begins to feel**  
**his connection with life.**

</div>

**2663. This experience**

<div class="lineated verse">

is not tied to external status.  
It is tied  
to clarity of perception.  
I note:  
**the value of a person**  
**is experienced directly.**

</div>

**2664. When attention is in**

<div class="lineated verse">

the space of Presence,  
there appears a feeling  
of inner wholeness.  
And I understand:  
**a person ceases to seek confirmation**  
**only in the outer.**

</div>

**2665. It is precisely this state**

<div class="lineated verse">

that was sometimes described  
by the image of anointing.  
Not as the action of oil,  
but as a sign  
of inner clarity.  
I say:  
**anointing becomes a symbol**  
**of the inward recognition of life.**

</div>

**2666. And last:**

<div class="lineated verse">

the image of anointing was used  
to describe an experience,  
in which a person feels  
a deep connection with life  
and clarity of his presence.  
And then the formulation becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of presence,**  
**in which a person experiences**  
**inner clarity and agreement with life.**  
It is precisely this experience  
that many texts  
figuratively called  
**anointing.**

</div>

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of presence was sometimes called the voice)

**2667. Every person**

<div class="lineated verse">

is familiar with the inner dialogue.  
Thoughts appear  
as though in the form of speech.  
I see:  
**the mind often thinks in words.**

</div>

**2668. This inner stream of thoughts**

<div class="lineated verse">

can be quick  
and contradictory.  
Sometimes thoughts argue with one another.  
It is clear to me:  
**the inner dialogue**  
**is not always clear.**

</div>

**2669. But sometimes there arises**

<div class="lineated verse">

another experience.  
A thought appears  
very quietly  
and clearly.  
I note:  
**it is not accompanied by an inner dispute.**

</div>

**2670. Such a thought**

<div class="lineated verse">

can be felt  
as simple understanding.  
Not as a long argument.  
And I understand:  
**it is perceived**  
**as immediate clarity.**

</div>

**2671. In ancient texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

such moments  
were sometimes described  
through the image of a voice.  
I say:  
**the voice becomes a symbol of clear understanding.**

</div>

**2672. This voice**

<div class="lineated verse">

is not necessarily bound  
to sound.  
It may appear  
as a thought  
or a sense of direction.  
I see:  
**this is an experience of inner knowing.**

</div>

**2673. When attention is steady**

<div class="lineated verse">

in the space of Presence,  
such moments of clarity  
become more noticeable.  
I note:  
**inner understanding arises more easily.**

</div>

**2674. Therefore some texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

speak of a voice,  
which can be heard  
in silence.  
And I understand:  
**silence makes clarity visible.**

</div>

**2675. It is important to remember,**

<div class="lineated verse">

that this image  
does not mean a literal sound.  
It points  
to the form of inner understanding.  
I say:  
**the voice becomes a metaphor for clarity.**

</div>

**2676. And one last thing:**

when attention is in

the space of presence,

sometimes there arises a simple and clear understanding

of how to act

or what is true.

And then the wording becomes clear:

**The Holy Spirit is the space of presence,**

**in which the clarity of understanding**

**can appear**

**as a quiet inner knowing.**

It is precisely this experience

that many texts

have figuratively called

**a voice.**

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of Presence is sometimes called the witness)

**2677. A person is able to observe**

<div class="lineated verse">

one’s own thoughts.  
A thought appears —  
and a person can notice it.  
I see:  
**thoughts can become an object of observation.**

</div>

**2678. The same thing happens**

<div class="lineated verse">

with emotions.  
A person may feel fear,  
joy  
or sorrow  
and at the same time be aware of  
this experience.  
It is clear to me:  
**emotions also can be observed.**

</div>

**2679. Even the sensations of the body**

<div class="lineated verse">

can become an object of attention.  
Pain,  
heat,  
movement  
can be noticed.  
I note:  
**experiences can be brought to awareness.**

</div>

**2680. When a person begins**

<div class="lineated verse">

to notice this clearly,  
the question arises:  
what exactly  
is observing all these processes?  
And I understand:  
**there appears the sense of an observing presence.**

</div>

**2681. This presence**

<div class="lineated verse">

is not a thought.  
Thoughts arise  
and disappear.  
I say:  
**the observation remains.**

</div>

**2682. This presence**

<div class="lineated verse">

is not an emotion.  
Emotions may change.  
I can see:  
**observation remains the background.**

</div>

**2683. Therefore some traditions**

<div class="lineated verse">

used the image  
of a witness.  
A witness is  
one who sees what is taking place.  
I record:  
**a witness symbolizes pure observation.**

</div>

**2683. This witness**

<div class="lineated verse">

does not interfere in events.  
He simply observes.  
And I understand:  
**observation in itself**  
**is not action.**

</div>

**2684. When attention clearly senses**

<div class="lineated verse">

this space of observation,  
many experiences  
become less gripping.  
I say:  
**observation creates a space**  
**of perception.**

</div>

**2685. And one last thing:**

<div class="lineated verse">

the image of the witness was used  
to describe the capacity of consciousness  
to observe its own experience.  
And then the wording becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of Presence,**  
**in which a person is able**  
**to observe thoughts, emotions, and experiences**  
**as phenomena of consciousness.**  
It is precisely this capacity  
that many texts  
metaphorically called  
**the witness.**

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of presence is sometimes called silence)

**2687. Silence is usually understood**

<div class="lineated verse">

as the absence of sounds.  
When sounds disappear,  
we say,  
that it has become quiet around us.  
I see:  
**this is the physical meaning of silence.**

</div>

**2688. But there is another level as well.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Even in complete outward silence  
within a person  
the flow of thoughts may continue.  
It is clear to Me:  
**inner noise can remain.**

</div>

**2689. When attention begins**

<div class="lineated verse">

to observe this stream of thoughts,  
sometimes a change takes place.  
Thoughts gradually slow down.  
I note:  
**the inner noise diminishes.**

</div>

**2690. In such moments**

<div class="lineated verse">

there may arise a feeling  
of inner peace.  
And I understand:  
**perception becomes calmer.**

</div>

**2691. This state**

<div class="lineated verse">

does not necessarily mean  
a complete absence of thoughts.  
But thoughts  
cease to dominate.  
I say:  
**silence becomes the space**  
**between thoughts.**

</div>

**2692. When attention remains**

<div class="lineated verse">

in this space,  
there arises a feeling  
of deep simplicity.  
It is clear to me:  
**the experience becomes clear.**

</div>

**2693. Therefore many texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

used the word  
“silence”  
to describe  
this state.  
I note:  
**silence becomes a symbol of**  
**peaceful presence.**

</div>

**2694. Sometimes in this silence**

<div class="lineated verse">

it becomes noticeable  
the awareness itself.  
Not thoughts,  
but the space,  
in which they arise.  
And I understand:  
**awareness becomes clear.**

</div>

**2695. This experience**

<div class="lineated verse">

does not depend on outer conditions.  
Even among sounds  
inner silence  
may remain.  
I say:  
**silence becomes the quality of attention.**

</div>

**2696. And last:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when attention clearly perceives  
the space of presence,  
it can be felt  
as deep inner silence.  
And then the phrase becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of presence,**  
**in which consciousness experiences**  
**a calm clarity**  
**beyond inner noise.**  
It is precisely this experience  
that many texts  
have figuratively called  
**silence.**

</div>

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of presence is sometimes called the world)

**2697. In ordinary life**

<div class="lineated verse">

peace is often understood  
as the absence of conflict.  
When the struggle ends,  
peace comes.  
I see:  
**this is the social meaning of the word.**

</div>

**2698. But within a person**

<div class="lineated verse">

there can also be conflict.  
Thoughts may contend,  
desires may be at odds with one another.  
It is clear to me:  
**inner conflict**  
**creates tension.**

</div>

**2699. When attention begins to perceive clearly**

<div class="lineated verse">

the space of presence,  
the inner processes  
become more noticeable.  
I observe:  
**thoughts and emotions cease to mingle.**

</div>

**2700. This creates a sense**

<div class="lineated verse">

of greater space  
between experiences.  
And I understand:  
**inner tension lessens.**

</div>

**2701. When the tension eases,**

<div class="lineated verse">

there appears a state  
of calm balance.  
I say:  
**there arises a feeling of inner peace.**

</div>

**2702. This state**

<div class="lineated verse">

does not mean the complete absence of emotions.  
Life continues  
to call forth various reactions.  
I can see:  
**the world arises**  
**as stability within changes.**

</div>

**2703. Therefore many texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

used the word  
“peace”  
to describe  
this experience.  
I note:  
**peace becomes a symbol of**  
**inner harmony.**

</div>

**2704. When attention is steadfast**

<div class="lineated verse">

in the space of presence,  
a person can endure  
difficult situations  
without total inner collapse.  
And I understand:  
**the world becomes a quality of consciousness.**

</div>

**2705. This state**

<div class="lineated verse">

does not depend entirely  
on outward circumstances.  
It is connected  
with the way  
life is experienced.  
I say:  
**peace arises**  
**from the clarity of perception.**

</div>

**2706. And one last thing:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when attention clearly perceives  
the space of presence,  
inner conflicts  
grow weaker.  
There appears a sense  
of deep stability.  
And then the wording becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of presence,**  
**in which consciousness experiences**  
**inner harmony and peace.**  
It is precisely this state  
that in many texts  
was figuratively called  
**peace.**

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of presence is sometimes called joy)

**2707. Ordinary joy**

<div class="lineated verse">

often depends  
on external circumstances.  
When something desired happens,  
a feeling of joy arises.  
I see:  
**such joy is connected**  
**with events.**

</div>

**2708. But there is another kind of joy as well.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Sometimes a person may feel  
quiet joy  
for no apparent reason.  
It is clear to Me:  
**this state is not connected**  
**with any specific event.**

</div>

**2709. When attention is**

<div class="lineated verse">

in the space of presence,  
inner tension diminishes.  
I note:  
**a feeling of lightness arises.**

</div>

**2710. This lightness**

<div class="lineated verse">

can manifest  
as quiet contentment.  
And I understand:  
**life begins to feel**  
**less heavy.**

</div>

**2711. Such a state**

<div class="lineated verse">

is not necessarily accompanied  
by strong emotions.  
It may be  
very quiet.  
I say:  
**it is joy without excitement.**

</div>

**2712. When a person experiences**

<div class="lineated verse">

the very fact of existence  
more clearly,  
a feeling of gratitude sometimes arises.  
I can see:  
**life begins to be perceived**  
**as a value in itself.**

</div>

**2713. From this experience**

<div class="lineated verse">

joy can naturally arise.  
Not because  
everything is perfect,  
but because  
life is felt as alive.  
I note:  
**joy becomes a quality of presence.**

</div>

**2714. Therefore many texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

used the word  
“joy”  
to describe  
this state.  
And I understand:  
**joy becomes a sign**  
**of living perception.**

</div>

**2715. This joy**

<div class="lineated verse">

can coexist  
even with the hardships of life.  
It does not negate  
other experiences.  
I say:  
**joy becomes the backdrop**  
**for different states.**

</div>

**2716. And lastly:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when attention is steady  
in the space of presence,  
life can be experienced  
with a feeling of lightness and aliveness.  
And then the wording becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of presence,**  
**in which a person can experience**  
**the quiet joy of the very fact of life.**  
It is precisely this experience  
that in many texts  
was figuratively called  
**joy.**

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of presence is sometimes called power)

**2717. Physical strength**

<div class="lineated verse">

is bound to the body.  
It is manifested  
in the ability to move,  
lift  
or alter material things.  
I see:  
**this is the force of action.**

</div>

**2718. But man experiences**

<div class="lineated verse">

another form of strength —  
inner.  
It is shown  
in steadfastness,  
clarity  
and the ability to keep moving.  
It is clear to me:  
**this strength is connected**  
**with the state of consciousness.**

</div>

**2719. When attention is**

<div class="lineated verse">

in the space of presence,  
many inner conflicts  
grow weaker.  
I note:  
**energy stops being spent**  
**on inner struggle.**

</div>

**2720. This creates a feeling**

<div class="lineated verse">

of greater inner collectedness.  
And I understand:  
**steadiness appears.**

</div>

**2721. Out of this steadiness**

<div class="lineated verse">

a person can act  
more calmly  
and decisively.  
I say:  
**action becomes more precise.**

</div>

**2722. This Power**

<div class="lineated verse">

is not bound  
to aggression.  
On the contrary,  
it often appears  
as calm confidence.  
It is clear to me:  
**power can be quiet.**

</div>

**2723. When attention is steady**

<div class="lineated verse">

in the space of Presence,  
a person depends less  
on immediate impulses.  
I note:  
**the ability appears to endure difficulties.**

</div>

**2724. It is precisely this state**

<div class="lineated verse">

that many texts  
described with the word  
“power.”  
And I understand:  
**power becomes a quality of clear consciousness.**

</div>

**2725. does not suppress life.**

<div class="lineated verse">

It sustains  
movement through hardships.  
I say:  
**strength becomes a support.**

</div>

**2726. And the last thing:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when attention is steady  
in the space of presence,  
a person begins to feel  
a deep inner steadiness.  
And then the wording becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of presence,**  
**in which a person discovers**  
**a quiet inner strength**  
**to remain clear**  
**and alive**  
**in any circumstances.**  
It is precisely this experience  
that in many texts  
was figuratively called  
**power.**

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of presence is sometimes called the light of life)

**2727. Light makes**

<div class="lineated verse">

vision possible.  
Without light  
the eye does not discern forms.  
I see:  
**light opens the world to perception.**

</div>

**2728. Therefore light**

<div class="lineated verse">

naturally became a symbol of  
understanding.  
When a person begins  
to see something clearly,  
we say:  
it has become clear.  
It is clear to me:  
**light has become an image of clarity.**

</div>

**2729. And life**

<div class="lineated verse">

is perceived  
as motion,  
change  
and experience.  
I note:  
**life is the process of experience.**

</div>

**2730. When attention clearly perceives**

<div class="lineated verse">

the space of Presence,  
the perception of life  
becomes more transparent.  
And I understand:  
**life begins to be felt more clearly.**

</div>

**2731. Thoughts, emotions, and events**

<div class="lineated verse">

cease altogether  
to eclipse the experience of life.  
I say:  
**there arises a feeling of inner light.**

</div>

**2732. This light**

<div class="lineated verse">

is not physical.  
It is connected  
with the quality of perception.  
I can see:  
**clarity becomes experiential.**

</div>

**2733. When this clarity is joined**

<div class="lineated verse">

with the experience of life,  
an image arises  
“the light of life.”  
I note:  
**life is perceived as illuminated by attention.**

</div>

**2734. This state**

<div class="lineated verse">

does not require special circumstances.  
It is connected  
with the fact that,  
how a person perceives what is happening.  
And I understand:  
**attention becomes the source of light.**

</div>

**2735. Therefore some texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

speak of the light,  
which illumines life.  
I say:  
**this is the image of a clear presence.**

</div>

**2736. And last:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when attention is steady  
in the space of presence,  
life itself  
begins to be experienced  
with greater clarity.  
And then the formulation becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of presence,**  
**in which attention illumines**  
**the very process of life.**  
It is precisely this experience  
in many texts  
was figuratively called  
**the light of life.**

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of presence is sometimes called simplicity)

**2737. The human mind is inclined**

<div class="lineated verse">

to complicate what is happening.  
It creates explanations,  
theories,  
comparisons.  
I see:  
**thinking often increases complexity.**

</div>

**2738. But life itself**

<div class="lineated verse">

takes place much more simply.  
Breathing takes place.  
The heart beats.  
A moment arises.  
It is clear to me:  
**the basic processes of life are simple.**

</div>

**2739. When attention returns**

<div class="lineated verse">

to the space of presence,  
the experience of life  
becomes less overloaded with thoughts.  
I note:  
**perception becomes simpler.**

</div>

**2740. This does not mean**

<div class="lineated verse">

the loss of depth.  
On the contrary,  
depth becomes clearer  
without unnecessary interpretations.  
And I understand:  
**simplicity reveals depth.**

</div>

**2741. In this state**

<div class="lineated verse">

life is experienced  
not as a complex system of problems,  
but as a succession of moments.  
I say:  
**every moment becomes sufficient.**

</div>

**2742. Many spiritual texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

therefore spoke  
of a return to simplicity.  
It is visible to me:  
**simplicity becomes a sign of clarity.**

</div>

**2743. When attention is steady**

<div class="lineated verse">

in the space of Presence,  
many inner conflicts  
cease to seem so complicated.  
I note:  
**complexity is often created by thoughts.**

</div>

**2744. And simplicity**

<div class="lineated verse">

arises  
when attention sees what is happening  
directly.  
And I understand:  
**life becomes clear without needless explanations.**

</div>

**2745. Therefore many texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

spoke of simplicity  
as a sign of inner wisdom.  
I say:  
**simplicity becomes the expression of clear consciousness.**

</div>

**2746. And lastly:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when attention clearly perceives  
the space of presence,  
life ceases to be felt  
as excessively complex.  
It becomes  
the immediate experience of the moment.  
And then the formulation becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of presence,**  
**in which life is experienced**  
**in its simple and clear fullness.**  
It is precisely this clarity  
that many texts  
have figuratively called  
**simplicity.**

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of presence is sometimes called home)

**2747. A home in the ordinary sense**

<div class="lineated verse">

— is the place,  
where a person returns.  
After the road,  
work  
or long journeys.  
I see:  
**home is connected**  
**with return.**

</div>

**2748. Home is also perceived**

<div class="lineated verse">

as a space of safety.  
There one can  
relax  
and stop being on guard.  
It is clear to me:  
**home is bound**  
**with a feeling of peace.**

</div>

**2749. Within a person**

<div class="lineated verse">

there is often a constant movement of thoughts.  
Attention  
passes from one idea  
to another.  
I observe:  
**the mind is in constant motion.**

</div>

**2750. When attention returns**

<div class="lineated verse">

to the space of Presence,  
this movement may for a time  
grow quiet.  
And I understand:  
**there arises the feeling of an inner return.**

</div>

**2751. This state**

<div class="lineated verse">

is often experienced  
as familiar and natural.  
Not as something new,  
but as that  
which has always been available.  
I say:  
**a sense of recognition appears.**

</div>

**2752. Therefore some texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

used the image of a house.  
It points  
to a return  
to the natural state of attention.  
I can see:  
**the house becomes a symbol of**  
**inner peace.**

</div>

**2753. When attention is steady**

<div class="lineated verse">

in the space of presence,  
a person may feel  
a deep inner support.  
I note:  
**a sense of rootedness appears.**

</div>

**2754. This state**

<div class="lineated verse">

does not depend entirely  
on outward conditions.  
It is connected  
with the way  
life is experienced.  
And I understand:  
**the home becomes a quality of consciousness.**

</div>

**2755. Therefore many texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

speak  
of a return home.  
This expression  
points  
to the return of attention  
to the very fact of Presence.  
I say:  
**home is a state**  
**of clear abiding.**

</div>

**2756. And one last thing:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when a person begins to clearly experience  
the space of presence,  
it may be felt  
as a place of inward return.  
And then the wording becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of presence,**  
**in which a person experiences**  
**inner peace**  
**and the feeling of returning to oneself.**  
It is precisely this experience  
that in many texts  
was figuratively called  
**home.**

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of Presence is sometimes called the beginning)

**2757. Beginning is usually understood**

<div class="lineated verse">

as the first moment of an event.  
Any process  
has a starting point.  
I see:  
**beginning is connected**  
**with arising.**

</div>

**2758. But if one carefully observes**

<div class="lineated verse">

the process of thinking,  
it becomes noticeable  
that an interesting phenomenon occurs.  
Thoughts appear.  
It is clear to me:  
**a thought arises**  
**from an unknown source.**

</div>

**2759. Before the thought has taken shape,**

<div class="lineated verse">

it is not yet there.  
But then it appears  
in consciousness.  
I note:  
**every thought has a moment of birth.**

</div>

**2760. If the attention becomes sufficiently still,**

<div class="lineated verse">

sometimes one can notice  
the very moment a thought appears.  
And I understand:  
**a thought arises**  
**from a deeper space.**

</div>

**2761. This Space**

<div class="lineated verse">

is not thought.  
It precedes thought.  
I say:  
**it becomes the background**  
**for the arising of experience.**

</div>

**2762. Therefore some texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

called this space  
the beginning.  
Not the beginning of time,  
but the beginning of experience.  
It is clear to me:  
**all experience**  
**arises from this field.**

</div>

**2763. Thoughts arise.**

<div class="lineated verse">

Emotions arise.  
Sensations arise.  
I observe:  
**everything arises**  
**in one space.**

</div>

**2764. When attention clearly perceives**

<div class="lineated verse">

this space,  
it becomes noticeable  
that every moment  
arises anew.  
And I understand:  
**life is constantly beginning.**

</div>

**2765. Therefore some texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

spoke of a return  
to the beginning.  
This pointed  
to the experience  
of the source of experience.  
I say:  
**the beginning becomes a symbol**  
**of living presence.**

</div>

**2766. And lastly:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when attention clearly perceives  
the space of presence,  
it becomes noticeable  
that all experiences  
arise from it.  
And then the formulation becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of presence,**  
**from which constantly arises**  
**each new moment of experience.**  
It is precisely this experience  
that in many texts  
was figuratively called  
**the beginning.**

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (why presence-space was sometimes called the end)

**2767. Usually the word “end”**

<div class="lineated verse">

means the completion of a process.  
An event comes to an end.  
A story comes to its finale.  
I see:  
**the end is bound up with the completion of movement.**

</div>

**2768. But many processes**

<div class="lineated verse">

within a person  
have no clear ending.  
Thoughts continue to arise.  
Desires succeed one another.  
It is clear to me:  
**the mind tends to sustain unceasing movement.**

</div>

**2769. This movement often creates**

<div class="lineated verse">

the feeling of endless searching.  
A person seeks answers,  
solutions,  
confirmation.  
I note:  
**searching becomes a constant state.**

</div>

**2770. When attention returns**

<div class="lineated verse">

to the space of Presence,  
this seeking may for a time  
cease.  
And I understand:  
**there arises a cessation of inward movement.**

</div>

**2771. This stopping**

<div class="lineated verse">

does not mean the cessation of life.  
Life goes on.  
I say:  
**only the inner seeking stops.**

</div>

**2772. In such moments**

<div class="lineated verse">

a person may feel  
that nothing needs to be added  
to what is happening.  
It is seen to me:  
**there arises a feeling of completeness.**

</div>

**2773. Therefore some texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

used the word  
“end.”  
It pointed  
to the completion of the inner search.  
I record:  
**the end becomes a symbol of peace.**

</div>

**2774. When seeking ceases,**

<div class="lineated verse">

attention can be wholly  
in the moment.  
And I understand:  
**life is perceived**  
**without the need to correct it.**

</div>

**2775. This state**

<div class="lineated verse">

does not mean final knowledge.  
It means  
a temporary cessation  
of the constant striving to seek.  
I say:  
**the end becomes a point of rest.**

</div>

**2776. And the last thing:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when attention clearly perceives  
the space of presence,  
the inner search may  
come to a temporary end.  
And then the formulation becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of presence,**  
**in which the inner movement of seeking**  
**may come to a stop.**  
It is precisely this stopping  
that many texts  
figuratively called  
**the end.**

</div>

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of presence is sometimes called fullness)

**2777. The ordinary human experience**

<div class="lineated verse">

is often bound up with a sense of lack.  
One wants something else:  
knowledge,  
recognition,  
security,  
meaning.  
I see:  
**the mind often experiences reality**  
**through the idea of lack.**

</div>

**2778. From this feeling**

<div class="lineated verse">

there arises a striving  
to add something to life.  
Seeking becomes  
the main movement.  
It is clear to Me:  
**the desire for fulfillment**  
**supports inner activity.**

</div>

**2779. But sometimes there arises**

<div class="lineated verse">

another experience.  
For a short time  
the inner seeking  
ceases.  
I note:  
**there appears a sense of the sufficiency of the moment.**

</div>

**2780. In this state**

<div class="lineated verse">

life is experienced  
as already happening  
and complete in itself.  
And I understand:  
**the moment requires no addition.**

</div>

**2781. This does not mean**

<div class="lineated verse">

that desires disappear  
or goals.  
But the feeling disappears,  
that without their fulfillment  
life is incomplete.  
I say:  
**life is felt as whole.**

</div>

**2782. Therefore many texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

used the word  
“fullness.”  
It points  
to the experience  
of sufficiency.  
I see:  
**fullness becomes a quality of presence.**

</div>

**2783. When attention is steady**

<div class="lineated verse">

in the space of Presence,  
the very fact of existence  
begins to be perceived  
as complete.  
I note:  
**life is felt as a whole.**

</div>

**2784. This experience**

<div class="lineated verse">

can be very quiet.  
Without strong emotions  
or dramatic sensations.  
And I understand:  
**fullness manifests as calm completion.**

</div>

**2785. Therefore some texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

say  
of the fullness of life  
or the fullness of spirit.  
I say:  
**this is the image of experiencing wholeness.**

</div>

**2786. And the last thing:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when attention clearly perceives  
the space of presence,  
life can be experienced  
as already completed  
in every moment.  
And then the wording becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of presence,**  
**in which life is felt**  
**as whole and sufficient**  
**in the very fact of its manifestation.**  
It is precisely this experience  
that in many texts  
was figuratively called  
**fullness.**

</div>

### ABOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of presence was sometimes called rest)

**2787. Usually the word “rest”**

<div class="lineated verse">

is understood as the absence of movement.  
The body rests.  
Action ceases.  
I see:  
**rest is often associated**  
**with the stopping of activity.**

</div>

**2788. But life**

<div class="lineated verse">

cannot come to a complete halt.  
Breathing continues.  
The heart continues to beat.  
It is clear to Me:  
**life remains movement.**

</div>

**2789. Therefore spiritual texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

use the word “peace”  
in another sense.  
It is connected  
not with the ending of life.  
I note:  
**peace refers to inner perception.**

</div>

**2790. Within a person**

<div class="lineated verse">

there can exist  
a constant tension of attention.  
Thoughts evaluate,  
compare,  
plan.  
And I understand:  
**the mind sustains inner activity.**

</div>

**2791. When attention returns**

<div class="lineated verse">

to the space of presence,  
this tension may lessen.  
I say:  
**the inner movement becomes softer.**

</div>

**2792. At this moment**

<div class="lineated verse">

life continues  
just as before.  
But perception  
becomes calmer.  
I see:  
**the experience of peace appears.**

</div>

**2793. This repose**

<div class="lineated verse">

is not indifference.  
A person still  
feels  
and acts.  
I record:  
**repose is compatible with life.**

</div>

**2794. He manifests**

<div class="lineated verse">

as the absence of inner struggle  
with what is happening.  
And I understand:  
**peace is connected**  
**with the acceptance of the moment.**

</div>

**2795. Therefore many texts**

<div class="lineated verse">

speak of the repose of the spirit.  
This expression  
points to a state,  
in which inner resistance  
grows weaker.  
I say:  
**repose becomes a quality of presence.**

</div>

**2796. And one last thing:**

<div class="lineated verse">

when attention clearly perceives  
the space of presence,  
life can be experienced  
without inner tension.  
And then the wording becomes clear:  
**The Holy Spirit is the space of presence,**  
**in which life is experienced**  
**without inner struggle**  
**and tension.**  
It is precisely this state  
that in many texts  
was figuratively called  
**rest.**

</div>

### ON THE HOLY SPIRIT (why the space of presence was sometimes called silence)

**2797. In the ordinary sense**

<div class="lineated verse">

silence is the absence of noise.  
When sounds cease,  
it is said  
that silence has come.  
I see:  
**silence is perceived**  
**as the absence of sounds.**

</div>
